column1: Text

As cars gained steam in the early 1900s, especially with Ford's famous Model T, they've become super common. But now, more people are wondering if we use cars too much, and honestly, I think it might be good to chill out on driving so much.

Elizabeth Rosenthal wrote a really interesting article called "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," and it points out a big problem. Experts say that cars, which are all over the place in suburban areas like you see in Shanghai or Chicago, make it really hard to cut down on pollution from vehicles. While cars cause 12% of Europe's pollution, it's a whopping 50% in the US because we rely on cars so much! Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," really shows how bad cars are for the environment. Paris had to ban cars based on their license plates just to deal with smog!

Then there's Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," which is actually pretty cool. Millions of people in Colombia ditched their cars for a day and walked, biked, or took the bus. The streets became so peaceful! This yearly event bans private cars, but buses and taxis can still get around the city of seven million people. It's led to cleaner air and has helped the city get a makeover with new parks, sports areas, sidewalks, and businesses popping up.

So, it seems like cutting back on cars and having car-free days can really help, like cleaner air and making cities better. What happened in Colombia shows that it's possible to ease traffic and improve things. Maybe if we did something similar in America, like biking or walking more for short trips, we could make things more sustainable. I honestly think cutting back on cars is a pretty good idea.

Transportation is necessary for pretty much every country across the world. It helps everyone get to places faster, with things like cars and buses. But hey, there's a downside; all that driving creates a lot of pollution. Despite the fact that most people use cars daily, it also adds harmful stuff to the air, and we should find ways to cut back.

Check out this tiny German town called Vauban. These people are trying to live without owning cars. Their homes are nearby grocery stores, instead of far out. Getting around is easier. Obviously, they still have cars, but check this out: almost three-quarters of the people living there don't even own one! Weird, huh? In fact, more than half the families said they got rid of their car just to live there. A lot of the people living in Vauban say they don't feel as stressed when they don't have to drive that much. Currently, 12% of the stuff hurting the planet is caused by the cars people drive. Now, listen to this! That number goes way up to 50% in places where everyone drives, such as many parts of the United States.

What about Paris? Smog was getting pretty bad so they came up with a solution. So, on Mondays, if your license plate ended in an even number, you could not drive your car. No exceptions! Or you got a ticket. The next day, they switched it and banned the odd numbers. Guess what happened? The traffic was reduced by 60% in just five days! A huge cause of the smog problem came from cars with diesel engines, which most cars in France were. But once they stopped banning the odd numbers, it didn't work as well as they hoped.

Imagine an entire day with absolutely zero cars! That actually happens one day every year in Bogota, Colombia. People don't drive their own cars, unless it's a taxi or bus. You can drastically reduce pollution with just one day of no cars. Since the 90s, they have been doing this. After that, they added bike paths, parks, and centers. Living without a car could be difficult, but it's for the best.

For the most part, we're most likely not going to eliminate cars anytime soon. But, countries should try to reduce pollution and encourage everyone to drive less. It would make a difference if we did something about the air pollution. Cars are relied on, but overusing them causes damage to our environment and health.

Elisabeth Rosenthal suggests America's car craze might be fading. Her main point? Car use is dropping, which, surprisingly, could be a good thing! Less driving? That might mean safer roads, healthier people, and less pollution. Basically, we could rely less on cars.

First off, driving less could make us safer and healthier, which is super important. Andrew Selsky talked about how quiet the streets were on a car-free day in Colombia – so peaceful! No traffic jams mean less stress, since traffic messes with our schedules and stresses us out. Less driving? Less traffic and stress! Heidrun Walter said ditching her car made her way less stressed and way happier. Plus, driving less gets us moving. Cars are convenient, sure, but we're all so busy that we forget to exercise. So, driving less makes us more active. Selsky noticed tons of parks and sports places popping up. Fewer cars mean more parks, and more parks mean more chances to work out. Basically, less driving equals less stress and better health!

Next up, less driving is good for the planet. Maybe not everyone cares about the environment, but it's a big deal. Pollution is bad for everything, including us, and cars make a lot of pollution. Rosenthal says less driving "will have beneficial implications for carbon emissions and the environment." Less driving? Less pollution. It's a chance to make the world better and have a brighter future. Pollution is serious, and drivers play a part.

Also, we'd be less stuck on cars. A lot of people think they NEED their cars, not realizing they're actually addicted. Technology has changed us, and we're losing touch with nature. Being dependent isn't always a good thing. David Goldberg says, "all our development since World War II has been centered on the car, and that will have to change." Some might disagree, but relying on something that could hurt us isn't progress. Rosenthal points out that "cash-strapped Americans could not afford new cars, and the unemployed weren't going to work anyway." Not everyone can even afford a car. People who rely on cars have lost touch with nature and don't always think about the environment. Less driving could fix all of that.

Basically, driving less could lead to better health, less pollution, and less car dependence. It's a cleaner, safer world for everyone. Maybe America's making progress by driving less and focusing on what's good for all of us.

Do you drive often? Do you use your car for work or shopping? Have you ever thought about how many others are driving, even for short distances? It's simple to forget the effect of each car trip on the environment. Can you skip driving today? Many say, "I need my car!" But, cutting back on car use is possible. Cities such as Havana, Paris, and Bogotá have lowered driving rates with success. Driving less has a lot of good things for you and for everyone.

Oddly enough, less driving can lift your spirits. Heidrun Walter pointed out that a person felt less stress without a car. Think about road rage. It is not good for you. Driving needs focus – finding routes, dealing with mean drivers – which makes you stressed. Big cities like New York and Paris often have lots of traffic and pollution. Robert Duffer wrote in "Paris bans driving due to smog" that car fumes get stuck by warm air, which is not good. Duffer also noted that Paris banned cars for a short time because of bad pollution, which cleaned the air quickly. People can walk, bike, or skate then, which is good exercise and makes you feel better than sitting in a car.

Cutting back on cars also makes the city prettier. Pollution hurts plants and smells bad, making the environment dark. Less driving means less bad fumes and a cleaner city. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote in "In German Suburb, Life goes on Without Cars" that fewer cars mean more people walking, which leads to more sidewalks and stores. Andrew Selsky added that they added parks, sports places, nice sidewalks, new restaurants, and cool stores. The Environmental Protection Agency likes "car-reduced" areas, as Rosenthal said, which makes pollution less. Bogotá, Colombia, has a "car-free" day each year, giving fines to drivers. Although giving fines is a maybe, it works, reducing smog. So, driving less helps a lot with your health, better cities, and a cleaner place to live.

Cars are pretty cool! They're maybe a really big deal, to be honest. People go from place to place because of them. Isn't that what they're supposed to do? We want to get there fast, and cars do just that! You can go far in a blink—not much else is as good. Sure, there are trains and buses, but cars are seen as better. But, driving a lot makes us stressed and hurts the Earth. Buses and trains mean no car repairs or traffic jams, so there's less pollution! Using cars less is a good thing.

This is good for us and the planet. It's important to have less dirty air. Cars make bad stuff—greenhouse gases—come out of the exhaust pipe. One car isn't a big deal, but tons of cars are a problem, especially in crowded cities like Paris. Because of really bad pollution, they stopped people from driving. Robert Duffer said in "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog" that if your license plate ended in an even number, you couldn't drive or you'd have to pay money. Then, they switched it to odd numbers! This made 60% less traffic! Andrew Selsky said in "Carfree Day Is Spinning into a Big Hit in Bogota" that Bogota, Colombia, has days with no cars to help the air, get people to try other ways to get around, and make things better for the environment. All over the world, people know that not using cars as much helps the Earth.

Think about less stress! Driving can make you nervous. Traffic jams, always fixing the car, and noise make you more stressed. Driving less means less stress! You don't have to sit in traffic, fix your car, or deal with loud noises. Elisabeth Rosenthal said that Hartmut Walter said in "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." He said that getting rid of his car made him happier and less stressed. Andrew Selsky also said in "Carfree Day Is Spinning into a Big Hit in Bogota," that car-free days are "a good opportunity to take away stress..." It's good to choose peace. Less stress makes life better.

Using cars less is good for the Earth and for people. The Earth really needs our help. Also, stress is really bad for you. We can make the Earth better and have less stress by doing this. At first, it might not make sense, but lots of places are doing it and it works. This makes things better.

The Electoral College? Such a confusing system! Instead of directly selecting the president, we elect electors who then make the choice for us, whether they lean Democrat or Republican. According to source two, these electors can disregard our preferences! How democratic is that? We vote hoping for someone who represents us and improves our lives, but this system undermines that. The biggest problem? The "winner-take-all" rule is just awful.

We don't directly elect our president today. As source two notes, we vote for electors, each state getting a specific number. These electors then determine the winner. Source two states there are 538 electors, with 270 needed to win. The number of electors a state has corresponds to its congressional representation. For a fair system, every vote must count! Eliminating the Electoral College would ensure every vote matters. Candidates would earn votes reflecting actual popular support, and ties would be rare. Currently, if the majority in your state favors someone else, your vote is wasted. Get rid of the college, and suddenly, your vote counts!

Source two mentions "faithless" electors, individuals who might vote for the opposing candidate! This is insulting and harmful to America. A Republican elector voting Democrat effectively invalidates Republican votes. We only elect a president every four years. Electors changing their vote? Completely unacceptable.

Sources two and three repeatedly highlight the "winner-take-all" issue. If you lose, your votes are essentially useless. The victor gets everything! It's as if voters who supported the loser secretly wanted the winner.

Therefore, state senators, abolish the Electoral College! It's a terrible system. It makes some votes more valuable than others. It allows electors to vote based on their personal whims. And the "winner-take-all" approach? It simply discards votes for the losing candidates.

Dear Senator,

I'm writing to ask for a review of how we pick our leader. It's really important that we get rid of the Electoral College for a system that truly shows what the people want. Right now, this system is just not fair – electors can totally change what the people want!

I get that changing the Electoral College may seem scary. Yet, doing so could be much better in the long run. A direct vote might make candidates promise too much just to get votes. And, we must think about ties and fairness in a direct vote system. So, while totally ditching the Electoral College may not be the answer, fixing it has to happen.

Now, those electors hold too much power in who becomes president. This "winner-takes-all" thing we do in the Electoral College hurts voters. Candidates only care about a few "swing" states. Like in 2000, they skipped seventeen states! Because of stuff like this, the Electoral College is old and needs a fix or total change.

Candidates pick electors to help them get votes. These electors help get support, without the candidate having to go to every state. Basically, the candidate just waits for the results. The candidate could even win without trying much because electors decide. This makes it easy for candidates to bribe electors in key states to get their vote.

When people vote, they’re voting for electors, not the candidates. Electors are picked in ways like state meetings or by the candidates. A big problem with the Electoral College is the "disaster factor." It allows for bad stuff, like in 1960, when people in Louisiana almost switched Democratic electors to ones against John F. Kennedy.

So, the Electoral College is an old way to do elections. It gives power to electors, not the people. It made sense when society wasn't as smart, but it doesn't work for our democracy now.

Okay, everyone, listen up! Cars are everywhere. They're convenient, sure, but what if we used them less? Think: cleaner air and more money in your pocket. Seriously, driving less is a smart idea.

Take Paris, for instance. Robert Duffer noted: "Paris bans driving due to smog." Their solution was clever. One day, odd-numbered plates could drive; even plates the next. Break the rules, pay a fine. They even had free public transport for a week! Traffic and smog? Gone! The air cleared up, and people saved money. Duffer reported congestion fell 60%! He stated, "Congestion was down 60 percent in the capital of France, after five days of intensifying smog... rivaled Beijing, China, which is known as one of the most polluted cities in the world." Bad smog, but fewer cars made a HUGE difference.

Also, look at Vauban, Germany. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote "In German Suburb, life goes on without cars." They went all-out, nearly abandoning cars. About 70% don't even own one, as parking is expensive outside the town, and cars are mostly banned. They really enjoy it. Heidrun Walter said "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Fewer young people even bother getting licenses! Rosenthal wrote in "The end of car culture" that cars are now just tools for getting around. Nobody cares much about the car itself, just getting places easily.

Basically, cars get you places, but so do buses, trains, bikes, and carpools. Some are even free! And less pollution and traffic? It's a win-win for both our health and the world.

Plumer (Source 2) argues that the Electoral College is, without a doubt, unjust, old-fashioned, and makes no sense. Lots of people agree, thinking it's either a disaster waiting to happen or just plain broken. Some folks defend it, saying it stops tied elections or gives bigger states more power, but most don't buy these reasons. Those who hate the Electoral College really want a simple popular vote, seeing it as way better.

A big problem is that votes aren't equal. Electors have more say than regular people. Direct voting would mean every single vote counts the same. Plus, the chance that electors could be swayed or bought off messes up the whole idea of democracy.

Many think the Electoral College is a thing of the past. Plumer (Source 2) mentions Bob Dole wanting to "Abolish the Electoral College!" A whole bunch of people feel the same, preferring direct voting over a small group deciding. Some even joke that a little kid throwing darts could do a better job. Plumer (Source 2) says, "Over 60 percent of voters want a direct election," and that feeling's been getting stronger since 2000.

Honestly, the Electoral College is weird. Electors, not us, pick the president, which is confusing. Plumer (Source 2) says, "You vote for electors, who then vote for the president. Who are they? Usually, just regular folks. Who picks them? It depends on the state – maybe a party group, or the candidate. Can we control how they vote? Not always! Do people accidentally vote for the wrong electors? Sometimes!" This complicated setup makes you wonder who's really in charge.

The Electoral College might have a few good points, like keeping things stable, but most people think it's more bad than good. A simple popular vote seems much fairer. Like Plumer (Source 2) puts it, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational!"

The Electoral College, established by our founders and secured within our Constitution, has been around for quite a while. I think we should keep this system because it has worked pretty well so far, and there's no real reason to get rid of it. It makes sense for the Electoral College to stay because it helps in about five important situations.

Sure, the Electoral College has some risks. Electors could go against their party and vote however they want, out of the blue. Basically, we vote for electors, and they then pick the president. These electors could just ignore what the people want and vote their own way. Plus, a state could send in two different lists of electors, making things really messy. And while a tie is super unlikely in a regular vote, the Electoral College always has that chance. Candidates often skip campaigning in some states because one party is way ahead, thanks to the "winner-takes-all" thing. With a direct vote, candidates might try harder in every state to get more votes. But, most arguments against the Electoral College are about weird situations, so it's smart to look at the other side too.

Swing states are the real deciders in presidential elections, so voters there are usually very informed. They know their votes matter, so they pay close attention to the news and what the candidates are doing. Candidates spend lots of time and money in these states to win them over. Lots of other states are pretty much decided before the election even starts because one party is so strong. Voters in swing states probably think harder about who they like and what they believe in, which might make them the best people to choose our leader.

The Electoral College stops us from needing extra elections, which cuts down on confusion. Without it, a super close direct vote could cause big problems. For example, Nixon winning in 1968 and Clinton in 1992 with only about 43% of the vote shows why the Electoral College is important.

I get why some voters in states that lean heavily one way might not like the Electoral College. They might not get as involved in campaigns, thinking their votes won't change anything. But one person's vote is tiny, and not everyone voted in 2012 anyway. For most people, just saying who they want is more important than swaying the whole election. Getting involved in how our government works makes your vote more likely to actually matter.

Esteemed Senator,

I implore you, let's hold onto the Electoral College! It’s made up of 538 folks. To snag the White House, a hopeful needs 270+ votes. States pick these electors, usually connected to a candidate's party. Keeping this system? Super important! It guarantees a clear victory and a president for all of us.

One HUGE reason to keep it? It pretty much always hands one person a clear win. Sure, a tie is *possible*, but like, really unlikely, even with an even number of electors. Back in 2012, Obama scored roughly 62% of the electoral votes, but only 51% of the actual votes. Most states? Winner takes *all* the electoral votes. So, even a tiny lead can mean a huge electoral win. But hey, this "winner-take-all" thing means folks mostly chase votes in swing states, and ignore the rest. Still, the winner usually ends up with way more electoral votes than overall votes.

And, the Prez has gotta represent *everyone*. The Electoral College forces candidates to get votes from everywhere. No one area has enough votes to pick the president all by themselves. Even if you're super popular in one area, you still gotta hit the campaign trail in other states. More votes in your easy states don't get you more electoral votes. A president without support across the map? Not a great leader. People in other areas might think their votes don't matter or the president doesn't care about them.

Bottom line: we *must* keep the Electoral College. It stops ties and makes sure the president is for *every* American.

Heidrun Walter said she was always stressed owning a car, but now loves the freedom of being car-free. This shows a growing trend of people moving away from needing cars. Cars used to be seen as key for getting around, but lower car sales and less driving show people are starting to see the good sides of using cars less. One big plus is that it can help with the greenhouse effect, reduce smog and air pollution, and help other parts of the economy, not just cars and transport.

The greenhouse effect, made worse by car fumes, is a danger to nature and our lives. The article "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars" says cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas emissions, and about 50% in some US areas. If we use cars less, we could cut emissions, which would help the Earth. This could have a good effect on big problems like global warming and rising sea levels. So, using cars less is good because it can lower greenhouse gas emissions.

Smog and air pollution are also big problems, harming both our health and the environment. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," says that "After Days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city." This five-day ban made the air cleaner. If we did this more often, we could cut pollution and smog. It could also make people use other ways to get around, like buses, bikes, and walking. "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" shows how stopping car use in a city can lead to more bike paths, parks, sports centers, and better sidewalks, as well as traffic rules. Therefore, using cars less can cut smog and air pollution and make cities better places to live.

Finally, using cars less can help the economy in different ways. One source says that lots of US transport money has gone to highways. Moving away from needing cars could free up money for things like schools, government programs, and local projects. Also, using cars less can save resources, letting people spend money on other things, as another source says. The fact that people are driving less shows this is happening. One source notes that "Demographic shifts in the driving population suggest that the trend may accelerate. There has been a large drop in the percentage of 16 to 39yearolds getting a license." This lets us focus on making the economy better. In short, using cars less has economic benefits, like giving the government more money to spend, saving people money, and helping the economy grow.

In conclusion, using cars less has many good points. These limits can boost the economy, cut greenhouse gas emissions, and reduce smog and air pollution in busy cities.

Picking a president is a BIG deal because what they do WILL change the country, for better or worse. The electoral college tries to show what each state wants, even though people argue about how well it works. I think it's a pretty good way to see what most people in each state want, which shows what the whole country is feeling.

Some folks complain that the electoral college doesn't pick the BEST president and isn't fair. But, hey, we still get to CHOOSE! Our voices still MATTER a lot. At the end of the day, every vote is important, WAY better than not voting at all. Sure, things could be better, but nothing's perfect. You can't please everyone. So, I think the electoral college does a decent job of adding up the votes.

It's not simple, but finding a BETTER way is tough. Mostly, people worry that the electoral college doesn't REALLY show what we ALL want. But I don't buy it. When you vote, you're trying to get your person elected. Your vote helps the electoral college go the way you want. This makes the states work together because the majority vote shows what each state wants, which I think is pretty cool.

Some complain the electoral college gives bigger states too much power. Okay, maybe a little, but maybe that's okay. States with more people SHOULD get more votes. But each vote still counts the same, so they can't get TOO much power. Even if some don't like it, the electoral college is a FAIR way to see what the country as a WHOLE is feeling, which is what you'd expect.

In the end, the electoral college DOES show what each state wants by using the majority vote to pick the president. It's not perfect, but just counting all the individual votes might be even WORSE. Every system has problems, but the electoral college is a pretty organized way to count votes over time. Not everyone will love it, but you can't make everyone happy!

Dear Senator, So, I've been thinking about the best way to pick our President and their crew, and I've landed on something. We have two ways of doing it: the Electoral College, or just counting up all the votes. After really thinking it over, I think one of these is way better.

With the Electoral College, these electors get together and vote for the President and Vice President. These 538 people decide who wins the election. Honestly, I don't like this system. We think we're voting for the president, but really, we're just picking electors who then make the call. Why should a small group, out of millions, get to decide who the president is, while the rest of us just wait to see what they say? It doesn't seem fair that their opinions matter so much more than everyone else's when it comes to picking who leads the country.

Now, if we had a popular vote system, every single citizen could vote directly for the President and the team they want. This lets us say who we want in charge, and hopefully make things better. Even if your candidate loses, your vote still counts and adds to the overall voice of the country. The Electoral College might give us a faster result, but knowing your vote really matters could get more people involved and thinking hard about the candidates. We get to say what we want, instead of just hoping an elector agrees with us.

I know the Electoral College has some good points, like making sure we have a clear winner. Some might say that picking electors is the same as voting directly. But, these small pluses don't matter as much as letting everyone vote directly.

Basically, when we're figuring out how to pick the president, we have to ask: How do you want to have your say? Do you want to be directly involved, or let someone else do it for you? For me, it's easy: I want the system that makes our democracy louder.

Imagine that nice walk in the park, maybe with your family. Pollution is making it harder to do that. We use cars too much instead of buses, walking, or sharing rides. It's super important to drive less. It costs a lot to own a car, and it also stops us from using other ways to get around and hurts the environment too.

Owning a car can really hit your wallet in ways you don't expect. In Vauban, Germany, parking spots can cost a crazy $40,000 – more than some houses! Bogota, Columbia makes people pay about $25 if they drive on days cars aren't allowed. And if you drive in Paris, you might get a 22-euro fine depending on whether your license plate is even or odd that day. Plus, gas is always expensive. Rich people might not care, but it's a big deal for families with kids.

We often forget how great public transportation is. What if there were no buses, trains, or subways? Just try driving into New York City then! Bogota makes people take the bus or walk to avoid getting fined on car-free days. The mayor, Antanas Mockus, was happy because many people did it even when it rained. Other cities like Cali and Valledupar started doing it too, and people from other countries came to see. New York even has a bike-sharing program to make it easier to get around. Public transportation is really important for saving the planet, even if some people don't think so.

You can't even see the stars sometimes because of smog. Paris had so much pollution that they stopped cars for a bit. It got 60% better in five days! But it's tricky because people in France like using diesel, which pollutes a lot. In Bogota, the car-free day gives everyone a break from the smog. And Vauban doesn't have much smog because there aren't many cars. Even small changes can help the environment.

Vauban and Bogota show that driving less makes pollution better. Paris needs to stop cars to get rid of smog. Vauban makes parking expensive, and Bogota fines drivers, so people walk or take the bus. We really need to drive less because it saves money, helps us find other ways to travel, and makes the air cleaner.

If we ditch the car, our town can win big. Cities all over, like in France and Colombia, are pushing people to find other ways to get around. People say they feel way less stressed when they have choices besides driving. Studies here at home also show that fewer folks are buying cars. It's the perfect time to copy cool ideas from places like Vauban or Bogota and spend less time stuck in traffic.

Can you believe a German neighborhood is living proof that you don't need cars? Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote about Vauban, Germany, and how it's basically "car-free." Most people there love using public transportation. The article even said that "70% of families don't have cars, and many sell theirs when they move there!" One person said she feels more relaxed and happy without a car, because she's not stressed about driving. Lots of places want to do the same thing and cut down on cars to help the environment. Get ready to see more "smart planning" spots pop up. If we all work together to drive less, our town could be a car-free dream.

Bogota, Colombia, has a car-free day to get people to try other ways to travel and to clean up the air. People who see it for the first time are amazed by how much better the city feels. Going "car-free" makes people move more and makes the city look nicer. News stories talk about all the new parks and sports areas, the wide and clean sidewalks, and the cool shops and restaurants. It's not just about cleaner air; the city becomes a more fun and pretty place to be!

So, what if we did what Bogota and Vauban do? Fewer people are buying cars around here already. Some aren't even learning to drive. One writer said Americans aren't as crazy about cars anymore. Another said that we drove the most miles a while back, and it's been going down since then. Young people are a big reason for this change. They use social media, phones, carpools, and public transportation more, so they drive less. Car companies might have a tough time, but a lot of people think this will lead to better and easier ways to travel.

Basically, driving less means less pollution, less stress, nicer neighborhoods, more exercise, and saving resources. Cities everywhere are trying to cut down on cars to fight pollution. If we go green and car-free, we'll be part of a global change!

Dear Senator, I am writing to ask you to get rid of the Electoral College. I really think that the person who becomes president should be picked only by the national popular vote. To be frank, I see the Electoral College as not fair and a big threat to what the U.S. Constitution is all about.

The Electoral College weakens the basic democracy of our country. We vote, yes, but the Electoral College can change our choice, which feels like a slap in the face! It makes you wonder if we are a real democracy. Remember the 2000 election? Gore won the most votes, but Bush won because of the Electoral College. As "The Indefensible Electoral College" says, "Over 60% of voters want a direct election, not this system." It is clear: most Americans want a simple vote.

Also, I really do not like the Electoral College because it puts the U.S. Constitution at risk, especially our rights. The idea is that we, the people, pick our president. But, when the one with the most votes loses because of the Electoral College, it hurts democracy. "The Indefensible Electoral College" worries about weird elections, saying, "What if a state sends in two sets of electors? It happened in Hawaii in '60. Luckily, Nixon okayed only the electors for his rival." Think about the chaos if this happens again and slips by, giving a candidate a win they do not deserve! The Electoral College is a disaster waiting to happen.

So, let us get rid of the Electoral College! It is not fair, and it is a threat to our U.S. Constitution. Please think about this as you decide what you think about this important issue.

Across the world, getting from A to B really messes with how clean the air is. A lot of folks might not realize just how much better things would be if we didn't use cars so much. See, those cars are constantly pumping out nasty fumes. So, before you jump into buying a car, seriously think about it. Honestly, all the good stuff about having a car? It just doesn't stack up against all the bad stuff they do to the environment.

Think about it: cars, big trucks, and buses? They all guzzle gasoline, and that's just bad news for the Earth. These fuels are hurting the very planet we live on! And if we keep going like this, all that car pollution could wreck the ozone layer. Guess what? We end up with no place to live! Plus, we lean on cars way too much. Even when we could easily walk or bike, we still jump in the car. That just makes the environmental situation worse. Look at Paris, they put some driving bans in place to fight pollution. And get this: one source says traffic dropped by 60% after just five days of heavy smog! You can describe transportation a lot of ways, but "death wish" pretty much nails it.

Owning a car hits your wallet in more ways than just buying it; there's a whole bunch of costs that come along with it. Check out Vauban, Germany, one source points out that you can only own a car there if you buy a parking spot. And that spot? It can cost you $40,000 on top of the house! Then you gotta factor in gasoline, which is super pricey. And don't forget insurance, that just adds to the financial headache. On top of all that, driving can be a real pain. Risky, stressful, and no fun at all.

Vehicles are everywhere now, a big part of how we live. If we didn't rely on them so much, it would really help the Earth. Using cars a lot makes pollution worse, and the air gets really bad.

Bogota, Colombia, started a "No Car Day," which is a cool idea for other places. On this day, lots of Colombians walked, biked, or took the bus, which made the city way less crowded. Even though it rained a bit, most people still joined in. The mayor said the weather didn't matter much. They want people to use other ways to get around and make the air cleaner. But, doing something like this might be hard in the U.S., where everyone loves their cars.

Lots of cities are trying to fix the air pollution from cars. Paris made it so you couldn't drive to stop really bad pollution. At first, people weren't happy. Reuters said they gave out almost 4,000 tickets and took away 27 cars. But, traffic went down by about 60 percent! Even though there were fewer cars, the air was still smoggy, like in Beijing. It's not sure if people will like these rules for a long time.

If you stop or limit cars, people might get mad because they can't get around as easily. But, new studies show that fewer Americans are buying cars, driving, or getting their license. A professor from Drexel University thinks this might be a big change. People who stopped driving when things were tough might not go back to it, so car use could keep going down in the U.S., which would make the air better.

Using cars a lot really messes up the world's pollution. It's hard to use cars less, and there might be some problems. But, it could really help. Imagine if the air was cleaner and more people walked, biked, and skated. Cutting back on cars could make life better and help the environment. Driving less can really help the planet.

Whether cutting back on driving has an effect hinges on where you live and how much you commit to it. There are trials happening all over the world that are looking into towns with fewer or no cars. Places with really bad pollution frequently cut back on car use a lot to make the air better.

Rosenthal's piece, "In German Suburb..." paints a clear picture of what it is like to live without a car. The way everything is set up makes it easier to walk and bike around. This change is really important. Because of pollution, it looks like a good idea to try this in more places around the world.

Some cities are dealing with really bad pollution problems. In Duffer's piece, "Paris bans driving...", the smog in Paris is talked about, which led to a ban on driving. People who broke the rules had to pay fines and had their cars taken away because people were angry. Duffer points out that "Congestion was down 60 percent...", which shows that the ban really did help to cut down on traffic.

There are programs all over the world that are trying to get people to use transportation that doesn't cause pollution. Selsky's "Carfree day..." shows how well it worked in Bogota. Even when it rained, millions of people started walking, biking, or skating. Selsky also says that Bogota feels more welcoming. "Parks…sidewalks,” have been upgraded, which makes the city better. It helps everyone.

We have to adapt to all the changes happening so quickly in the world. Younger people are starting to wonder if owning a car is really necessary, and they are choosing options that are cheaper and better for the environment. Not as many people think that everyone needs to own a car. It's still around, but it's not as common as it used to be.

The Electoral College? A continued source of major contention! Should it be kept, or should it be done away with? It comes across as quite... strange. Why maintain a system wherein citizens vote for an individual who *then* casts a vote for the president? If the objective of the United States is to empower its citizens to select the president, why is the popular vote not the decisive factor?

When casting a vote for a presidential hopeful, what action are you truly undertaking? Are you selecting the individuals who will then proceed to vote? Seriously? According to the article, "Does the Electoral College Work?", citizens are casting their ballots for "your candidate's electors." The Constitution affirms the right to vote for our president, but how is this logical? It appears inconsistent with the ideals of America and the voting system it should embody. Adding insult to injury, can we be certain of the reliability of these electors? The article "The Indefensible Electoral College" states, "...Can voters control whom their electors vote for? Not always." Therefore, the candidate you endorse may *not* receive your vote? The Electoral College could impede your ability to directly vote and undermine your American right to have your vote carry weight! Given its lack of coherence, your voice deserves to be genuinely heard.

Some assert that the Electoral College ensures the appointment of a president; however, does it guarantee that the chosen president reflects the preference of the majority? Seldom. The popular vote frequently feels futile, as if our voices are inconsequential. It resembles the deprivation of an American right. The popular vote ought to prevail, not the Electoral College. Al Gore experienced defeat, notwithstanding his triumph in the popular vote. The article "The Indefensible Electoral College" notes, "...according to a Gallup poll in 2000, taken shortly after Al Gorethanks to the quirks of the electoral collegewon the popular vote but lost the presidency..." This underscores how it diminishes the significance of individual votes. The same article reveals that "...over 60 percent of voters would prefer a direct election to the kind we have now." Should votes *truly* matter, the populace should vote on whether to retain it.

Certain individuals posit that the Electoral College preserves order, thereby averting voting irregularities. But is this indeed accurate? Every system possesses shortcomings, but the Electoral College does not constitute the remedy. The United States was established upon the principle of heeding the people's vote, a principle I aspire to witness realized in earnest. I aspire to vote directly for my preferred candidate, rather than voting for someone else to cast the vote.

In summation, the Electoral College: unjust, flawed, un-American. It thwarts our capacity to vote for our desired candidate and mandates voting for electors. If one subscribes to American values, the inherent flaws of this system become apparent. It represents an unsound method for managing votes; the American populace should hold the power to decide!

Respected Senator,

Why are we still using the Electoral College, some ask? People claim it disrupts true democracy, arguing that we're choosing electors, not our preferred leader. But in actuality, it functions well! It aids in clearly identifying the winner and ensures a president for all.

Its existence is based on providing a clear victor. Voting solely on popularity might cause more issues! Richard A. Posner notes that the victor nearly always gains more Electoral College votes than popular votes. This reduces the likelihood of result disputes. He's correct; those Electoral College votes simplify matters. Additionally, it prevents ties, such as a Nixon-Clinton tie in '92. The Electoral College offers a clearer victor due to its higher vote counts.

It also forces the president to consider all citizens. Candidates need votes nationwide, not just in supportive areas. We don't want a president focused on only one region! Envision Republicans favoring the South. If a Republican only focuses on the South, it's unfair to others. Bradford Plumer argues candidates ignore states they'll likely lose. But those states might be decided already! Why would a Democrat try in Republican Texas? Or a Republican in Democratic California? The voters likely decided! Old arguments like Plumer's might be outdated. Times change! Opposing the Electoral College needs current info. Look at recent elections! Opinions in 2004, 2008, and 2012 depended on current events, promises, and youth voter turnout. So, the Electoral College ensures presidential concern for the entire nation.

Therefore, keep the Electoral College! It confirms the winner and aids the president in representing everyone. Electoral College votes tend to be higher, clarifying results. It also forces the president to consider the whole country, not just specific regions. Senator, please retain it! It has worked well recently, suggesting future success.

Way back when, folks hoofed it everywhere – shops, meals, and kin time were all on foot. But now? Why the car craze? Everywhere you look, cities are nudging us to ditch those four wheels.

Cars? Environmental headache from day one. But hey, we're smarter now! So, rules are popping up, clamping down on car use. Check out Vauban, Germany: residents live car-free for dear old Earth and guess what? They dig it! As Heidrun Walter, a mom there, said, "Cars stressed me out. Now? Way happier!"

Paris, the "Love City," axed cars, blame the yucky air. Get this: Traffic jams plunged a huge 60 percent in just five days!

Paris had Europe's grossest air, a real smog fest, so they had to do something. The ban worked like a charm, clearing the air and kicking smog to the curb.

Big apples like New York, with its tiny streets, get seriously car-clogged. Buildings are squished tight, so walking or biking? Way faster than driving. New York's bike program? Proof in the pudding. Plus, carpooling's the new cool, where folks share rides, cutting down on cars. Less pollution, less jams, more cash in your pocket from gas saved.

Bill Ford, Ford's big cheese, dreams of a future where owning a car's a pain. He wants to buddy up with phone giants to build cities, or city webs, where "folks walking, bikes, cars, trucks, buses, all play nice, saving time, energy, cutting pollution, boosting safety." We can't just ban cars everywhere – they're often handier than trains or feet – but less is more. Too many cars? Pollution nightmare! How about one car-free day everywhere, and trying other ways to get around? Biking? Awesome exercise and faster than walking, so get fit and arrive sooner!

The Electoral College makes presidential races a real tug-of-war. Some say it's a goner, others swear it's all good. It's like mixing a people's vote with a congressional one. Past elections had hiccups, and future ones could be even wilder. Why can't we just directly elect the president in a democracy? We need a better way, and the Electoral College should be ditched.

Here's the kicker: we pick electors, and they're the actual deciders of who gets to be president. Even if the popular pick wins, there are still issues like the "winner-take-all" deal. "The Indefensible Electoral College" says this system's a bummer for voters because of its setup. Candidates skip states they can't win, zeroing in on tight races in swing states. They camp out in certain states, leaving others in the dust. In 2000, candidates didn't even swing by 17 states, proving how messed up the Electoral College is.

Another worry is "the disaster factor." Bradford Plumer mentioned we dodged a bullet in 2000, but the system could have way bigger fails. In Louisiana, some politicians almost swapped out Democratic electors with anti-John F. Kennedy folks to mess with the vote. It shows how shady the system could be if they got away with it. We could also see states sending different electors or a tie. Even with ways to fix these, such possibilities scream for a reliable system.

Richard A. Posner defends the Electoral College, believing it keeps things fair. He says it gives smaller states more say to balance out the bigger ones. But it doesn't balance; it favors smaller states. Posner also thinks swing state voters are more engaged. Plumer disagrees, noting campaigns ignored places like Rhode Island and South Carolina. Many voters in big media markets didn't see any campaign ads. The Electoral College is easy to rig, and we should dump it.

I'm still convinced the Electoral College has to go. People should pick their president. Even if tough, the current system is clearly crooked. Some say the Electoral College is just fine, but that's just not the case. We need a total replacement or some heavy-duty changes.

Dear Florida State Senator, Some see the Electoral College as a solid way to pick presidents, while others claim it’s too flawed and unjust to keep around. We need something better to fix these issues. A true democracy should have a voting process that truly shows what democracy is all about.

Those who like the Electoral College might say ties don’t happen often, or that electors usually do what voters want. However, just because a problem is rare doesn't mean we should ignore it. Small flaws in the system can grow bigger, like a tiny tear in fabric. If we don't fix the Electoral College now, the problems could get too big to handle later. Electors might think about what people want, but they can also decide on their own.

The Electoral College really cuts down on the power of individual voters. Even though you vote, the electors are the ones who really pick the president. The way electors are spread out unevenly across states makes it seem unfair, like some votes matter more than others. This can turn people off from voting because they might think their vote doesn’t matter. Also, candidates often ignore states they don’t think they can win and instead focus on states with more electoral votes. This makes people feel disconnected from the election and might make them less likely to vote.

Besides weakening the people's power, the Electoral College has some obvious problems. Electoral ties are rare but still possible. If that happens, the House of Representatives gets to decide. This could lead to a choice that doesn’t match the popular vote. Plus, you can win the popular vote and still lose the election because of the Electoral College, like Al Gore did in 2000. All these issues show it's not a good system. We need to make changes before we use the Electoral College in future elections to stop more problems.

The Electoral College works, but it doesn't really show what the people want. The electors, not the citizens, pick the president. In a country that believes in the power of the people, we don't have much say. We need a better system that truly fits with what democracy is all about. We should speak up against the overrated Electoral College and push for a system that truly represents what America should stand for.

Cars offer easy travel for many. Yet, vehicle fumes seriously harm our world. Thankfully, using cars less is a good fix, bringing many wins – like less greenhouse gas, and less smog, plus it saves stuff we need!

First off, using cars less means way less greenhouse gases go into the air. Think about biking, trains, or even just walking! Car fumes really pump out those gases, which are bad news. Source 1 says regular cars cause almost half of those fumes. So, fewer cars directly helps our planet feel better.

Also, using cars less really cuts down on smog. Smog messes up both nature and our health. Think of smog as nasty air, and it can make people sick. Lots of cars make smog way worse in some towns. Source 2 noted that Paris almost had worse air than even China, which is known for bad air. When we drive less, the air gets cleaner overall. Proof? Just five days of fewer cars in Paris dropped smog by a huge 60%! Cleaner air is a big win when we use cars less.

Finally, using cars less helps save resources. Source 1 says that less car use could mean we build smaller cities. That means less land is used for roads, malls, and homes. Source 4 claims this would save tons of building stuff, plus natural resources. Basically, less driving is better for everyone's lives.

In short, leaning less on cars helps our earth, makes us healthier, and keeps resources safe for later.

Even though many folks wanna get rid of it, the Electoral College actually has more good stuff than bad. The Constitution MADE this thing on purpose to make sure neither Congress nor the people had ALL the power when choosing a president. This way, everyone in the country gets a say, and the leaders of each state can stand up for what their people want. Sure, some people complain, but the Electoral College is still a pretty good way to pick the President, if you ask me.

Because of the Electoral College, people who want to be president gotta try to get folks all over the country to like them. Like, if you're only popular in the South, you can't win, 'cause that area don't have enough electoral votes. So, candidates gotta work on winning over places that don't really like them much. This is a good thing 'cause we WANT a president that lots of people like, 'cause that makes them better at the job. Candidates really focus on the states where the election could go either way, which makes voters there more interested and knowledgable. These smart voters are perfect for deciding who wins!

Also, we should keep the Electoral College 'cause it usually picks who the people want anyway. Yeah, Al Gore got more votes than George W. Bush in 2000, but Bush won because of the Electoral College; however, this hardly ever happens! It last happened in 1888. The folks who vote in the Electoral College are chosen by the parties, and they're supposed to vote for their party's candidate, which they almost always do. Switching to just counting up the votes might make MORE problems than the Electoral College fixes. Plus, what if there's a TIE? That's even numbered votes, yikes! Of course, that's super unlikely, and it's never happened before. The Electoral College also handles things if no one gets a majority of the votes.

Some folks say the Electoral College makes people not wanna vote 'cause they think their candidate doesn't have a shot in their state, like Republicans in California. But every vote helps decide who gets the state's electoral votes! People vote in presidential elections to show who they like, not just 'cause they think their one vote will decide everything. The Electoral College has worked pretty well for over a hundred years, and it'll keep doing so if we keep it around.

Want to save Earth? Easy peasy! Just drive less. Check this out: a study in Europe says cars make about 12% of greenhouse gases. Giving up your ride sounds tough. But hey, tons of choices! You can carpool, bum a ride, hop on a bus or train, grab a cab, or even bike around! So, it's not about choices, but just wanting to save the planet.

Cars are bad news for Earth! Cities are fighting back with fees to lower car use. Paris did just that. After a smog event, they banned cars for a bit! This shows the world can get better! Beijing was super polluted like Paris, until the government stepped in. Bogota also bans over 7.2 million people from driving on "Day Without Cars." If you break the rules, you could get a $25 ticket or even jail time. Andrew Selsky said that this "program set to spread to other countries, millions of Colombians hiked, biked, skated, or took buses to work during this car free day". It's not just cleaner air, but people hanging out in parks together.

Fewer cars are good for you, for real! Less traffic means cleaner air to breathe. More walking means less heart issues, obesity, and more! Car companies might not like this one bit. Ford, Toyota, Honda, and others might lose cash if we buy less cars, but most folks won’t stop driving. But, less car production could help the economy by saving money on gas. Guess what? People are driving less each year! Experts think this is a great thing for Earth, even if car companies don't agree.

Check this out: Car use is going down! Young adults aren't buying cars. A study in 2013 found a 23% drop in driving among young people from 2001 to 2009. Experts aren't sweating it, but the drop in sales makes some wonder if the U.S. has reached "peak driving". What's next? Good or bad? Who knows! Obama’s goals to cut greenhouse gases seem to have worked. Studies now show that "Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses as each year goes by".

So, Senator, this Electoral College deal is kinda crazy, right? Should we stick with it, or go for a system where the person with the most votes wins? I'm really feeling that popular vote thing. I mean, think about it: a bunch of people don't even bother voting now. Like, maybe half of us do, if that. Could be 'cause they think their vote doesn't matter in states like California or Texas, where it feels like the winner's already picked. "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23 has more on that. Also, right now, candidates only care about those "swing states," forgetting about everyone else.

Here's another thing, Senator: "only half of eligible Americans voted in 2012," says "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23. I think people just don't see the point if their candidate's got no shot in their state. And get this: some folks even tried to mess with the Electoral College back in the day. For example, some segregationists tried to screw over Democratic Electors to hurt Kennedy—read about it in "The Indefensible Electoral College" Section 11—which shows how shaky this whole thing is.

I get that there are reasons to keep the Electoral College. Some say it's a compromise the Founding Fathers made to make sure everyone gets a fair shake, giving "qualified citizens in congress" a say, according to "What is the Electoral College" Section 1. Maybe the Electoral College helps because people still get to influence the electors' choices, and history matters, says "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 15. Ideally, electors are good people who'll vote for whoever won the most votes, according to "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16. But even if the electoral votes are right, the popular vote can still be higher, even though it doesn't happen often—"In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16 says that.

Senator, we gotta talk about how unfair the winner-take-all thing is. Candidates spend all their time and money in the big states, running tons of ads, and they forget about the smaller states they think they've already won. And those "swing states" get, like, all the attention. Check this out: "Seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't see a single campaign ad," says "What is wrong with the Electoral College" Section 13. It shows how much they care about those big states and "swing states." That's gotta bum out voters in the states everyone ignores.

So, Senator, I think we should switch to a popular vote system. It would make people want to vote, knowing their vote can actually make a difference and help pick who runs their state and country. We should always remember: "Power to the People." That's what really matters.

Woah, cars are taking over! No one seems to walk or bike anymore; it's all about driving. This changes everything. But is this normal? Rosenthal, Duffer, and Selsky get it! They all want fewer cars, but for different reasons. After reading their stuff, it's clear: fewer cars mean more time, better business, saved resources, and a weaker "car culture."

Late because of traffic? Maybe walking or biking is faster! Selsky noted that Colombia had a car-free day. Millions "hiked, biked, skated or took buses," so the streets were "eerily... devoid of traffic jams." Less traffic, less waiting! Walking helps the planet and saves time, too. Businesses can also thrive with fewer cars, as Selsky pointed out, with "new restaurants and upscale shopping districts" popping up, and old sidewalks becoming "broad, smooth sidewalks." More walking means people see more stores and spend more cash!

Saving resources is another huge reason to ditch cars. Rosenthal says cars cause "12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe... and up to 50 percent in some car-intensive areas." Not good! We *have* to drive less; car fumes are killing the planet. Some cities even try "no-driving" days. Duffer mentioned Paris having a "partial driving ban" after "days of near-record pollution." Drive less, less pollution, traffic, and smog! Awesome.

Car culture might be dying. Studies show that young Americans are driving less. Rosenthal mentions a "23 percent" drop in driving among young folks. Maybe it's tickets, parents, or caring about the planet. But Rosenthal also said experts think this will help lower "carbon emissions and the environment." Because of this, car companies are changing, and commute times are shifting due to tech.

Bottom line: less driving means more time, better business, saved resources, and a weaker car culture. Next time you travel, think about driving less. It's way better for the planet.

Listen up: the Electoral College? Super important, can't get rid of it. Some say it's old, whatever, but it's still the best way to pick a President, end of story. Just imagine: elections cause way less trouble than if we just counted all the votes. No do-over needed, either. And most importantly, it stops the big states from running the show. Keep the Electoral College? Key to stopping chaos.

Electoral College wins hardly ever cause the kind of crazy fights you'd see if we just counted up every single vote. A lot of times, candidates get way more electoral votes than their actual popular vote percentage would show. Remember Obama? He snagged almost 62% of the electoral votes, even though only about 51% of voters chose him. Because of the "winner-take-all" thing in most states, figuring out who wins each state's electoral votes is easy when someone gets the most votes, even if it's not a majority. A tie in the Electoral College? Could happen, sure, but super rare, even with an even number of electoral votes total. Counting popular votes? That'd probably cause way more problems.

The Electoral College actually stops us from needing RunOff elections. Those happen when nobody gets more than 50%. Like, "Nixon in 1968 and Clinton in 1992 both only had 43 percent of the popular votes, but they still won a majority in the Electoral College with 301 and 370 Electoral Votes". The Electoral College picks a clear winner, saving us the stress of a second vote. Because of it, RunOffs are a total non-issue.

So, basically: the Electoral College really stops big states from having too much power. Honestly, I think we should keep using the Electoral College, just like always. Sure, maybe there are better ideas out there, but the Electoral College is the best system we've got working right now.

Click! Mom snaps a pic on her iPhone as my grin stretches wide. This little plastic rectangle, feels like pure freedom. Ah, childhood! Lying on my belly in the kitchen, a toy car nearby as Mom makes dinner, the garlic scent fills the air. I finally have my driver's license! Huge for us teens, sure, but let's not forget the downsides. If we ditch the car more often, the air would be cleaner, and we'd all breathe easier.

Now that I have my license, I hate waking up to smog so thick I have to squint on the way to school. And my car? It makes it worse! Car fumes are a major cause of smog, pumping greenhouse gasses into the air. Cars in the U.S. are responsible for about half of these emissions (Source 1). And making gas? Even more pollution! It wastes energy and spits out carbon. So, fuel production releases greenhouse gasses, and then our cars release even MORE! Thanks to AP Environmental Science, I know too many greenhouse gasses are bad news. They trap sunlight, even UV rays, and then—bam!—global warming. Plus, smog just looks awful. Paris was so choked with it, they banned certain cars to clear the air. One car-free day, and the smog vanished! People could breathe again (Source 2). Smog also hurts Paris' tourism. Who wants to visit Versailles if all they see is pollution?

Less car use is good for our bodies and our minds, period. Take Vauban, Germany. They went completely car-free. Over half the new residents sold their cars, and they're happier for it! Heidrun Walker, a media trainer with two kids, said, "When I had a car, I was always stressed. I'm much happier now." (Source 1). Walker's story shows that ditching the car is good for mental health. And your body benefits, too! People walk, run, bike, or even skate to get around. Bogota, Colombia, holds a "Day Without Cars" every year, encouraging walking in the big city (Source 3). Walking is great for your heart! If more places had car-free days, maybe fewer people would suffer from heart problems.

So, basically, driving less has tons of upsides: cleaner air, healthier bodies, and happier minds. Next time you reach for your keys to drive a short hop to a friend's house, stop and think. Think about your own health, think about the planet's health, and maybe try walking instead.

Elections? They're a HUGE deal, not some everyday thing! When you vote, your voice rings out loud, helping shape our nation's course. Picking the president should be as easy as counting votes; a clear path to our leader. We need to vote for who we really back, no strings attached. The popular vote's simple charm beats the Electoral College's confusing mess.

A lot of folks say the popular vote is more fair, 'cause it's simple: vote, count, winner. Candidates usually hit up a lot of states to campaign, if they can find the time. But, Bradford Plumer pointed out that with the Electoral College, "candidates don't hang out in states they know they can't win, just focusing on the close races in "swing" states." Seems wrong, since every state should meet the candidates, hear their plans. Some candidates forget whole states. Plumer notes that "during the 2000 campaign, seventeen states didn't even SEE the candidates, like Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the biggest media markets didn't see a single campaign ad." How can people know who to vote for if they don't see the candidates, hear their ideas?

Also, it's clear the popular vote is way less of a headache than the Electoral College. It can really screw things up. Popular vote? Easy peasy. Vote, count, winner. Electoral College? Well, Plumer says if there's a tie, "the election goes to the House of Representatives, where states vote on the president. The Senate picks the vice president. Since each state gets one vote, Wyoming's single vote, for 500,000 people, has the same say as California's 55 reps, for 35 million voters." Picture a HUGE mess, one vote for so many! The popular vote keeps it simple: everyone votes, most votes win. No need for reps to jump in and maybe screw it up for us.

Basically, the popular vote is better because it's more just. We pick who we want, no interference. And it's way simpler than the Electoral College. A tie could mean reps vote, and they might not even know what we want. Voting is serious, so let's do it right. We all should vote for our favorite, nobody else deciding, not even reps. Voting is super, super vital.

So, the Electoral College is how we vote, right? The Office of the Federal Register explains it: We pick electors first. They meet up and vote for the President and Vice President. Then, Congress counts those votes. Essentially, we pick people to pick the President. Seems okay, doesn't it? So why all the hubbub?

A lot of people want to get rid of it and just do a straight popular vote. Bradford Plumer asks, "Will electors even vote how we want them to?" He says, "Probably not." Well, maybe it happens sometimes, but we still pick electors we THINK will pick the right person. You have to trust them. Even if we voted directly for the President, we'd still have to trust THEY'D do a good job. It's pretty much the same, just a step more.

Richard A. Posner makes a good point: "Why dump a despised system?" He thinks the Electoral College evens things out, since the Senate helps smaller states. Right! Big states don't get much Senate power. So, with just a popular vote, those big states would get even MORE attention because they have tons of people.

Plus, the Electoral College keeps things from going crazy if no one gets more than half the votes. Think about 1968. Nixon got the most votes (43 percent), but no majority. Clinton had a similar thing happen in 1992. But they both won the Electoral College, so no runoff election was needed.

I say, keep the Electoral College. It works and stops states like Texas from bossing around little states like Rhode Island. And it helps us avoid runoffs. Some might say it's old, but I think the Electoral College is a pretty good voting method.

Okay, here's my attempt at rewriting the text with lowered perplexity and heightened burstiness, while adhering to all your specified constraints:

It's obvious: the electoral college is a bad system – old, unfair, and makes no sense. Some try to defend it, but those arguments don't hold up. Worried about direct voting? Don't be! Dole had it right: get rid of the electoral college. Why? Even its best defenses are simply wrong. Lots of supporters forget electors can't be trusted. You might think voting for an elector means they'll vote as they promised, but that's not a sure thing. Think about it: Nixon, Carter, Dole, the U.S. Chamber, and the AFL-CIO all wanted it gone at some point. People want the person with the most votes to win, right? But we vote for electors, not directly for the president. And if it's a tie, the House decides, but their votes aren't equal!

Voting for electors? It's NOT the same as voting for the president. Can you bank on electors keeping their word? Nope! Can voters pick the wrong electors? Absolutely. The electoral college? It's a mess, totally broken. People believe they're voting for the president directly, but surprise! That's a lie. We don't even get to pick the electors! So why assume they'll be loyal? Back in 1960, Louisiana almost swapped electors to block Kennedy. Imagine that! This system? It's easy to rig. The electoral college is a weak base for the U.S. "Faithless" electors have stabbed their party in the back. It could happen again. It's crooked! Ditch the electoral college. Let the people vote directly.

If the electoral vote ties, the House picks the prez and VP. Their process? Just as awful! It’s unfair and undemocratic. Each state gets ONE vote. So, Wyoming's single rep, with half a million people, gets the same say as California's 55 reps, with 35 million! Does that compute? NO WAY! That's just how it is. People often split their tickets, voting one party for president and another for Congress. So the House's choice might be totally off base. Think ties are rare? Nope: 41,971 votes in '68 could've done it. 5,559 votes in Ohio and 3,687 in Hawaii in '76 would have been it. Small shifts, HUGE problems. "Your vote matters?" Really? With this system? It's insane the Electoral College is still around. People just want a simple popular vote.

Bottom line: dump the electoral college. Direct presidential voting is the way to go. Electors are unreliable, and the House is unequal in a tie. Let people vote fairly, without tricks! Let us vote! A president "For the people" should be picked "By the people."

Driving all the time is bad for the Earth, so let's drive less, okay? The planet is getting wrecked, and cities and people suffer. Cars are a problem everywhere, from here to Central America and even Europe. This problem isn't just in one place. Burning gas makes the planet warmer, so how about we cut back on driving?

So, paragraph 10 from the "Paris bans driving due to smog" article says, "After days of near-record pollution..." Okay, but so what if Paris is very polluted, with super high pollution levels? So terrible! The "City of Love" is in a smog crisis! If Paris is getting smog alerts, what about every other big city? We get more of everything like factories and cars. What can we do? It can only get worse, so if we do not fix it, the future will be scary, so wake up!

David Goldberg said something really good in "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph seven: "All of our development since World War II has been centered on the car..." Why are cars more important than being healthy? What's the big deal? I get it. They are fast, but what do we lose because of cars? Let's not talk about gas; what about our lives? Our taxes go to the roads, not parks, and we see malls, not nature! Trees make clean air, but pollution is killing them. No one talked about smog before, but now cars are all anyone talks about!

Even though we need to drive less, we can still fix this mess. Let's start with no-car days. If you drive, you get a ticket! Bogota, Colombia, does that! The article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" mentions this, and hey, it might just work! Cali and Valledupar are trying it out!

Okay, last summer marked my first ever trip to the West Coast, specifically California. I pictured it as super diverse, you know, with surfing, mountains, and all that jazz. So, when I finally rolled into Los Angeles, I was expecting a real vibrant city, kind of like Miami, but boy, was I surprised! Honestly, the smog and crazy traffic just took away from California's natural beauty. It's a pretty common thing in cities that are so reliant on cars, but cutting down on car use would make a huge difference. Seriously, cleaner air and safer streets? That'd be a win-win for everyone!

In source 1, Elizabeth Rosenthal points out that "Passenger cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas emissions... and up to 50% in car-intensive US areas" (Rosenthal, paragraph 5). But hey, it's not just cars; delivery trucks are adding to the problem, too. Now that everyone's more aware of how harmful greenhouse gases are, there's a real push to ditch the cars. You've got places like France, Colombia, and even the US jumping on board. And guess what? Source 4 says these efforts are actually working! Rosenthal also mentions that "Recent studies suggest that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses as each year goes by" (paragraph 29). Okay, cars aren't going to disappear completely, but just cutting back on how much we use them can really help out the planet.

I mean, think about it: if we limited cars in super crowded areas like New York City, it could seriously cut down on accidents. Did you know that in the US, there are around 500 car accidents every single day? And worldwide, we're talking over 5000! Car accidents are a huge killer, but a lot of them could be prevented. And let's be real, driver overconfidence and people glued to their phones don't help. But here's a cool thing: greenhouse gas reduction programs also help decrease traffic accidents. According to Duffer, "Congestion was down 60 percent in the capital of France" (Duffer, paragraph 14). Plus, Source 3 points out that Bogota's Car Free Day is inspiring other Latin American countries to reduce traffic and make things safer for people walking around.

Basically, people are starting to get that using fewer cars is a big deal. Less car use equals cleaner air and fewer accidents, plain and simple. After I finally ditched the city and the highway on my California trip, I finally got to see all the amazing nature!

Ever wondered where air pollution comes from? Cars are a big part of the problem. Driving less has major upsides! Think cleaner air, more cash in your pocket, and safer roads. Let me explain.

First off, fewer cars on the road means less pollution in the air. Pretty simple, right? Take Paris, for example: they limited cars when pollution was bad, and the air got better fast! Basically, less cars, cleaner air. Plus, driving less means oil companies burn less stuff, so less bad stuff goes into the air. Bikes, walking, buses, skateboards, and subways are all much cleaner ways to get around.

Also, you save a bunch of money! Seriously. Getting caught driving when you shouldn't, like with those "25 fines" some people got, can cost you big time. And owning a car is expensive: gas (which is getting crazy expensive!), tickets, and upkeep (tires, lights, oil). Driving less means more money stays in your wallet over time.

Last but not least: safety matters! A report said mixing up how people get around – walking, biking, driving, buses – could "improve safety." Less traffic jams means fewer accidents and fewer people dying. Plus, less cars makes it safer for motorcycles, bikes, and people walking. And it cuts down on drunk driving.

So, yeah, driving less is a win-win-win. Cleaner air, more money, safer roads. What makes you choose to leave the car at home sometimes?

Lots of folks think cars are a must-have nowadays. Even though cars are super common for getting around, they're a big problem for the earth and make walking or taking the bus less appealing. But guess what? Cutting back on car use is a great idea! Other countries are already doing it, showing it's totally doable. If we had fewer cars, our planet, neighborhoods, and public transportation could get way better.

Cars are seriously messing up the environment. Check out this article called "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars" by Elisabeth Rosenthal. It talks about how Vauban, Germany, is trying hard to cut down on greenhouse gases. The article points out that cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas mess, and in the US, where everyone drives everywhere, it's even worse, like 50%! That pollution is a huge worry, making cities gross. If we didn't rely on cars so much, those numbers would drop big time. Sometimes, the government steps in to deal with car pollution. For example, Robert Duffer wrote an article called "Paris bans driving due to smog." He says Paris, France, banned cars because the smog was so bad! "After days of super bad pollution, Paris said only certain cars could drive to help clean up the air. People with even-numbered license plates had to stay home or pay a 22-euro fine. The next day, it was the odd-numbered plates' turn." And it worked! "Traffic went down by 60% in Paris after five days of smog!" See? Even small changes in how much we drive can make a big difference.

Cutting back on cars also makes cities way nicer. Andrew Selsky wrote an article called "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." It's about how Bogota, Colombia, got better by using fewer cars. "The car-free day is part of a plan that started in Bogota in the mid-90s. They built a ton of bike paths, the most in Latin America. Parks and sports places popped up everywhere. They fixed the broken sidewalks, and now they're smooth. Rush-hour traffic is way better, and new restaurants and stores opened up." Cities are starting to think about people who don't drive, because they know cars are a pain and want to make things better for everyone. In "The End of Car Culture," Elisabeth Rosenthal talks about how the US is trying to drive less too. Bill Ford, who's in charge of Ford Motor Company, wants to work with phone companies to build cities where "walking, biking, cars, buses, and trains all work together to save time, use less gas, make less pollution, and be safer." Lots of people are trying to make public spaces better for eco-friendly travel.

More and more, folks are realizing we don't need cars as much, so they're using them less. In "The End of Car Culture," Elisabeth Rosenthal says fewer people are getting licenses and buying cars. "America's obsession with cars is fading. The number of miles people drove peaked in 2005 and has been going down since, according to Doug Short from Advisor Perspectives." The article also says you don't have to own a car all the time anymore. "The Internet lets you work from home and hang out online. Cities are getting cooler, so people are moving back from the suburbs. Phones and carpool apps make it easier to get around, like shared vans for commuting.
