So, people who stopped driving because of the recession might not start again." Now, it's easier than ever to get a car when you need it, so you don't have to own one all the time.

Driving less is a smart move. If we have fewer cars on the road, we can fix big problems like pollution. Cities are changing to make public spaces better for everyone. Relying less on cars is super important for making people's work, time, money, and lives better, both where we live and around the world.

Alright, so I'm just a kid who hasn't seen much, but this Electoral College thing has been around forever. I know a 14-year-old's opinion might not matter since I can't vote for years. But listen! I really believe we should keep the Electoral College. A president chosen by popular vote could be a total disaster. A popular vote might mean one area loves the president, but everyone else hates them, which would be a big problem.

If we elect our president by popular vote, we could get stuck with someone terrible, no kidding. Article three, paragraph 20 says, "The winner-take-all thing makes candidates focus on tossup states." These states are crucial because they decide the whole election, so people there are informed. But with a popular vote, anyone over 18 can vote for silly reasons. Young voters might pick who their friends like, or just vote for their party. Many would make dumb choices without thinking, which the Electoral College prevents.

Also, a popular vote might elect a president just because they're famous in one place. Article three, paragraph 19 says a candidate with only regional appeal won't be a good president, and no region has enough votes to elect someone alone. If the popular vote decided everything, imagine that. Say Rodney is super popular in the South. Rodney doesn't need to campaign there since it won't get him more electoral votes. But if it was all about popularity, voters in other areas might feel ignored, and the president wouldn't care about them. Even if he won, they'd feel he wasn't "their president." The Electoral College stops that. We vote for electors, then they vote for president. The person with the most votes wins all the electors in that state, which is fair. A popular vote would unfairly hurt those who didn't vote for the winner.

Some people disagree, I know. Article two says, "Candidates don't spend time in states they can't win... In 2000, seventeen states didn't even see the candidates!" So, candidates ignore states they think they'll lose, and I see how that's an argument for a popular vote, where candidates would have to campaign everywhere. But there are good reasons not to do that. A candidate would have to visit all 50 states, but that doesn't mean they'd win. The Electoral College makes sure the majority in a state matters, not just overall popularity. It's like high school: the popular kid isn't always the nicest, and someone less popular might be really kind. Picking a president based on popularity could really hurt our country.

So, Mr. State Senator, I think the Electoral College is super important, no doubt. A popular vote could mean we get a bad president, or someone who only wins because one area likes them a lot. Please think about my letter when you talk about this, okay?

If folks chose to drive their cars less, perhaps the world would be a much better place. It could become a safer and healthier planet for everyone!

Seriously, just imagine if we all used cars less. The air around us would actually become so much cleaner! This means it would be way easier for everyone to breathe fresh, clean air and not be so constricted!

Of course, ditching the car might not be cool with some, but in the big picture, it helps everyone. Picture yourself breathing in dirty air all the time; that can't be good for you, especially for your lungs. Plus, less driving also means fewer trips to the gas station. Who wouldn't love to save money? More money can go towards anything you want!

Having a car isn't cheap! Think about paying for car insurance every month, then the constant need to fill up the gas tank every week... Gas prices might be a little lower these days, but they could shoot right back up and totally drain your bank account.

So, is driving *really* worth what it does to our earth, let alone what it does to your bank account?

Wouldn't life be a whole lot easier if we relied on our cars a bit less?

Ultimately, the choice is yours!

Have you ever stopped to think about all the pollution your car spits out when you drive it? Probably not, though. You're probably too busy thinking about getting where you're going or sending off a text message, instead of what’s going on with the environment. I get it, the environment isn't always top of mind! Usually, we only pay attention when some big, terrible news story about air quality pops up.

Because car pollution goes up a little at a time, it’s easy to ignore how bad it actually is for the planet.

If we keep going like this, plants and even people might wind up in the hospital just from having trouble breathing!

Think about how nice it would be to not stress about car payments or always needing to fill up the tank.

There are countless individuals and families that are constantly stressing about money, just trying to pay bills and have enough for gas to get to work.

What if, instead of having your car drink all that gas, you walked or biked to work? Imagine the cash you could keep in your pocket!

Scrapping the Electoral College? Bad idea, really. The Founding Fathers stuck it in the Constitution to keep Congress and the average Joe in check. Some folks whine it's rigged now because electors pick the president, not us straight up – like we're just choosing some electors. But the fear that electors will ditch our votes? Rarely a thing, and usually just drama. So, even though some say it's unfair, let's stick with the Electoral College. It keeps things clear-cut, avoids extra votes, and gives smaller states a louder voice.

Okay, the Electoral College can get dicey, like back in 2000, but that's rarer than a brawl over the popular vote count. It's a fact, someone could snag the electoral vote but lose the popular vote. In 2012, Obama grabbed 61.7% of the electoral vote with only 51.3% of the popular vote. The winner-takes-all setup in most states can make a slim win look massive.

Plus, the Electoral College stops elections where nobody gets more than half the votes. Think 1968 and 1992, where the winners only scored about 43% of the vote. This could've meant ties and runoffs! But the Electoral College picks a clear victor. Some even argue it makes folks skip voting in states where their candidate's doomed, like Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. Why bother if it seems pointless, right?

Still, the Electoral College levels the field, giving dinky states a chance against the big boys. Back in 2012, Florida was neck and neck, and its 29 electoral votes swung to Obama. Candidates hustle harder in big states because they matter more in the Electoral College. People often gripe about the winner-take-all deal, but candidates focus on swing states instead of wasting time where the outcome's a done deal.

Sure, some might not vote thinking their vote's a drop in the bucket, but one vote never decides an election. People vote to shout out what they believe, not because they think they're the only one calling the shots. That's why the Electoral College, not just the popular vote, should still pick our leader, plain and simple.

The electoral college? It feels quite off. Richard A. Posner penned "In Defense of the Electoral College: Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing the President," where he discusses Florida in 2012. Obama grabbed all electoral votes, yet around 62% of folks backed him. What about the remaining 48%? Their votes meant squat! It's a win-or-lose setup, even if the race is tight. Swing states hog all the spotlight, Bradford Plumer contends in "The Indefensible Electoral College: Why even the best-laid defenses of the system are wrong." Way back in 2000, candidates largely ignored seventeen states. A select few swing states mattered most. Under a popular vote, everyone gets equal attention. Each vote carries the same weight, regardless of where you reside.

Further, the electoral college can trigger a significant debacle. Plumer points out that electors sometimes stray from their designated votes. Although infrequent, it remains a possibility. One individual could derail everything. Also, imagine a tie! Each state then gets a single vote. Terribly unjust! Millions in populous states? Their vote holds equal value to a handful in smaller states. A popular vote resolves all these issues.

Richard A. Posner defends the electoral college, but his arguments fall flat. He suggests a popular vote would spark more disputes? Where's the evidence? He argues candidates would stick to their own areas? Perhaps, but that's a losing strategy. He claims the electoral college educates swing state voters? Seriously? A popular vote would educate *everyone*. He believes it benefits bigger states? Wrong. Candidates will target large populations regardless. Runoff elections are his sole valid point. They could be tricky. Yet, it's still superior to being unjust or perilous!

So, defending the electoral college is tough. The evidence for its unfairness and danger abounds. A national popular vote? It's essential. When you back the electoral college? You're endorsing unfairness, possibly even chaos. Nobody wants that!

America loudly proclaims the promises of life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness, with a side of impartiality and equal opportunity for all! But, uh oh, there's this thing called the Electoral College, and it's a real head-scratcher. We're talking about electors from each state throwing their votes in the presidential ring. Is it fair? People are always arguing about it, and a whole bunch see it as a total bummer, and for some pretty good reasons too.

Imagine this: you pick an elector who's all about your favorite presidential candidate, but then bam! They switch their vote! You end up with someone you totally don't like. It feels kinda unfair, right? You don't have total control over what these electors do. Wouldn't a simple popular vote be better? Then, the person everyone actually voted for would win, not someone they were against. It's even happened before: the person who got the most votes didn't win because of the Electoral College. So, your preferred person didn't end up in the White House. Fair voting is like, a basic right, right? We have fought so hard for it.

Now, besides shady votes, the Electoral College can cause even more trouble. A tie in a presidential election seems like, super unlikely. But hey, history's had some really close races. So, what if it's a dead tie? Some might say the Electoral College saves the day, but that might not be true. A tie might be easier to happen because there are fewer electors in the Electoral College. Just one elector changing their mind is like thousands of votes switching. Also, these electors get rewarded when their candidate wins, even though their whole state, or even the whole country, really helped them win and should get some credit.

So, yeah, the Electoral College makes you wonder about fairness and trust. Americans don't have total say over their electors, and a big tie is more probable. Seeing as America is all about equality and opportunity, direct and fair voting is a must.

Have you stopped to think about what happens because we love cars so much? Think about the dirty air, the sky covered in yucky stuff, and using up things we can't get back. What if, in the future, we all need masks just to breathe because of so much smog? If we use "green" choices and don't drive as much, we can fix up our towns and protect the world. We could have cleaner air, better places to live, and ways to get around that are way better. Trust me, once you try going green, you'll love it!

Smog really stinks if everyone's driving, especially in big cities. All the junk from cars gets stuck in the air and makes it bad, so it's hard to breathe. Paris even stopped people from driving cars once because it was so bad. They only let people drive on certain days, and the smog got way better! It was as bad as in Beijing, China. But when the smog was gone, the cars came back, and everything went back to being yucky.

Awesome towns don't have many cars. There's this town named Vauban in Germany where cars aren't allowed. About 70% of the people there don't have a car, and about 57% sold their cars to move there! One of the people who lives there, Heidrum Walter, said, "When I had a car, I was always stressed. Now I'm much happier." Bogota, in Colombia, is trying to get people to drive less too. These lifestyles without cars are good for us and the Earth!

Cars aren't going away, but there are new ways to get around. In NYC, people are using bikes that you can share. People are also carpooling to have fewer cars on the road. Studies show that young people in the US aren't getting their driver's licenses and buying cars as much. We could live in a world with less cars!

So, to end, driving less will really help the Earth. It can stop smog and make things greener!

According to Mr. Goldberg at Transportation for America, a group pushing for lifestyles without cars, the infrastructure we built after World War II was all about cars, and he thinks we need to rethink that. The world's changing, and cars, once a big deal, aren't as important anymore. Let's look at why people are moving away from owning cars.

This change is happening faster than expected, like in Germany. Vauban, a town that started in 2006, is trying out "smart planning." Basically, they're making a community where you don't need a car. More and more, people are walking, biking, or taking trams to work or school from the suburbs. "Smart planning" means designing towns so you don't have to drive as much. In Vauban, a whopping 70% of families don't have a car and are happier without the stress of driving every day. Get this: 57% sold their cars just to move to Vauban! Car-free towns are popping up all over Europe, the US, and other countries, making cities and suburbs more crowded so people don't have to drive so far for everything.

A big reason for this is that we need to stop global warming. Cars make a lot of greenhouse gases, about 12% of the world's total, and even 50% in places like the US where everyone drives.

Paris had really bad smog, so they banned cars with even-numbered license plates from driving, and if you did, you got a fine. It really helped with traffic. The smog was mostly because people were using diesel fuel, which was cheaper than gas. After it worked so well, Paris banned cars with odd-numbered license plates too, because they were a capital city with a lot of smog.

Cars have really changed how we live. But studies in the US show that people are buying fewer cars, driving less, and even getting fewer driver's licenses. Even though America used to love cars, more and more people want a greener planet and see the good things about not having a car. It's a pain to deal with insurance, payments, repairs, safety, and just the stress of owning a car. Cars are convenient, but their downsides are becoming clearer. Fewer teenagers and young adults are getting licenses, and more people are just not buying cars at all. So, you have to wonder: why even own a car?

All over the world, people are thinking about the good and bad things about cars, realizing that driving less is good, and changing their lives because of it. One of the most important inventions in history is starting to take a backseat.

A global shift is happening now. People want to lower greenhouse gasses a lot. Many are trying to stop using cars, or at least drive much less. It might sound weird, but some places are already doing it and it's working. They are making car pollution much lower.

Take Vauban, Germany, near Freiburg, for instance. There are no parking spots, driveways, or garages there. Instead, people take a tram that goes right to Freiburg. Heidrun Walter, who lives there with her two kids and teaches media, loves it. Vauban, finished in 2006, is a trend in Europe and the US: living in the suburbs without needing a car all the time. It's about one square mile, and its 5,500 people can easily walk to stores and services on the main street.

Europe makes about 12 percent of the world's greenhouse gasses, but the US makes 50 percent! This was clear in Paris when bad smog caused really high pollution. So, the city banned some cars. One day, cars with even numbers on their plates couldn't drive. The next day, it was odd numbers. People who broke the rules had to pay. Some drivers were mad, but the city made public transport free for the week.

Bogota, Colombia, even has a "Day Without Cars" to get people to drive less. Millions of Colombians found other ways to get to work. The city, with seven million people, made people try different ways to travel to fight smog. Mayor Antanas Mockus said even rain didn't stop people from joining in. That year, two more Colombian cities, Cali and Valledupar, also had a "Day Without Cars." Enrique Rera, the mayor of Asuncin, Paraguay, said, "These people are starting something big, and it's catching on!" Besides the "Day Without Cars," Bogota has made rules for rush hour, better sidewalks, and new places to eat and shop.

Vauban's no-car design, Paris's car ban for a bit, and Bogota's day of no cars show how we can drive less. People everywhere want to change to lower greenhouse gasses. In the US, fewer people are registering cars, and fewer young people are getting licenses. It's down 23 percent from 2001 to 2009. The goal is to save stuff, lower greenhouse gasses, and be safer. Many cities and towns are doing this, so it seems like we can do it.

Driving less has many good results. First, when we don't drive as much, we send fewer bad things into the air. Cars make a lot of pollution, maybe 12% or maybe even 50%, it depends on where you are. Therefore, when we use cars less, we help keep the air clean and make the world healthier!

Plus, if you don't drive as much, you may have less stress! Getting to work or school often needs a lot of thought, and just thinking about traffic can make people worry. Interestingly, when some places changed things during rush hour, traffic got better. As a result, using other ways to get around or simply driving less can make life calmer.

Also, young people seem to be driving less these days. Numbers show that fewer young people are getting their driver's licenses. One study shows that nearly 25% fewer young people are driving now. Thus, fewer new drivers could mean fewer car crashes, making things safer for young people.

In short, there are many good things for everyone when we drive less. From making less pollution and stress to making a safer place for young people, finding other ways to get around instead of driving is good for better and more sustainable communities.

It seems like something is occurring everywhere. Politicians as well as your average Joe, are attempting to drive less. Check this out: There is this German town which is almost fully car-free. The town's residents appear to be very happy about it! Germany isn't alone in trying to get cleaner air and reduce car stress. Even Paris, France is trying out this "no-car" thing. Paris will sometimes stop cars from driving when the air is extremely polluted. And wow, a week later, the air is significantly cleaner! Vauban (Germany) and Paris are attempting different methods to reduce the amount of cars. But wait! Colombia is home to three cities attempting to do the same! Once a year, they have a "no car" day.

Let us take a closer look at Vauban in Germany. Why would anyone construct a city with very few cars and parking? The majority of suburbs have homes which are far away from one another, almost no shops or interesting places close by, and a lot of cars. A lot of suburbs are like this. Although, this also results in a lot of pollution. Vauban fixed this issue by restricting the use of cars. Elisabeth Rosenthal penned an article entitled "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." The article mentions that cars account for approximately 12% of the greenhouse gases produced in Europe. However, in certain areas of the U.S., that number is close to 50%! Vauban is contributing to cleaner air simply by reducing car use. They constructed walkways and stores instead of simply building roads. This makes it a lot easier to get around. The money that they saved on roads is used for other methods of transportation. The article stated that eighty percent of transportation funds are typically spent on highways. Only twenty percent is put towards other options.

Paris is trying very hard to bring down the quantity of cars in use. There is a lot to see in the city and just about every place can be reached by bike. No one wants to pollute even more by driving! That is why Paris sometimes bans cars. The city's pollution can be just as bad as Beijing, China which is infamous for pollution. Robert Duffer wrote "Paris Bans Driving Due To Smog." Paris initially desired to ban cars altogether in order to get rid of pollution. Although, they were aware of the fact that this wasn't possible due to the number of drivers. In order to find a solution, they made a compromise. On some days, vehicles which had even license plate numbers could not drive. And on the following day, cars which had odd numbers were banned. This went on for a week. If you broke the rules, you were forced to pay a fine of twenty-two euros. Duffer's article stated that almost four thousand drivers ended up paying the fine that week. In spite of the fines, traffic was reduced by 60%!

Bogota, Colombia is yet another city that is attempting to limit cars. Bogota has nearly seven million people. Each year, they celebrate a car-free day. During this day, residents walk, bike, skate, and ride the bus. Andrew Selsky wrote "CarFree Day Is Spinning Into A Big Hit In Bogota." The article states that the city is attempting to reduce pollution, traffic, and accidents by encouraging the usage of taxis, buses, and other options. After three successful years, Cali and Valledupar (two other cities in Colombia) joined in. The car-free day was part of a plan to improve Bogota which began in the mid-1990s according to Selsky's article. Thanks to this plan, Bogota managed to construct 118 miles of bike paths. They also constructed various parks and sports centers.

Even in the U.S. (where people drive very often) change may be coming. Elisabeth Rosenthal penned "The End Of Car Culture." According to the article, fewer young people have been getting licenses and driving for the past 8 years. A greater quantity of people are walking, biking, taking buses or taxis. Because traffic is getting worse, these options tend to be faster than driving. When people do drive, they carpool even more. This contributes to a reduction in air pollution.

You know, lots of folks these days are itching to ditch the Electoral College altogether. Back in 2000, a Gallup poll showed people just wanted a simple, straight-up popular vote. As Americans, we've gotta weigh all our choices carefully. Maybe the Electoral College had its day, but it seems like a change is overdue.

The Electoral College uses a really complicated way of picking electors, and it takes months of planning. First, states have to pick their electors, then set up and run all those meetings. Deciding who gets to be an elector and why can be a real headache on election day. After that, the electors vote for the President and Vice President, and then the votes get counted. There are 538 members in the Electoral College, and you need at least 270 votes to win the presidency. A direct election could make this whole thing way easier.

If we had a direct election, everyone's vote would count, which could bring the states closer together. People want to feel like they matter. The Electoral College is losing popularity fast, and a lot of folks think a direct vote is a much better way to pick the President and Vice President.

Each candidate picks a group of electors in each state, usually chosen by their political party. So, if a voter doesn't like the electors that were chosen, they might feel like their vote doesn't matter and that they don't have a say in who becomes president.

Basically, people aren't big fans of the Electoral College anymore. They see it as clunky and slow. Some think government officials are holding onto it because they don't want to lose power. A direct election would clear up a lot of the confusion and make it simpler to choose our leader.

Since the beginning, people have tried to make life easier by making tools to hunt or building carriages. Back then, a big problem was horse poop. People had to clean it up. Then, cars came along. They were great, but they made smog.

Paris recently said no to some cars because the smog was too bad. This has changed Paris's weather. Nights are cooler, and days are warmer. Some blame diesel, but really, anything that burns fuel makes smog. But there's Vauban in Germany. It's a town with no cars! People there don't own cars because the public transportation is so good. People in Vauban say they are way less stressed than when they had cars. Kids can play safely without cars, so they might spend less time on screens. Vauban might seem extreme, but Bogota, Colombia, has a car-free day every year. Only buses and taxis are allowed, and if you break the rules, you pay a lot. Even in the U.S., young people are liking driving less. From 2003 to 2009, it went down by 23%. Driving is becoming less cool because there are so many other things to do. Is this the start of a change? In April 2013, people drove as little as they did in 1995 when fewer people had cars. New York City even has a carpool program!

But in some places, like Florida, public transportation isn't great. If you wanted to go from Homestead to Miami, you might have to drive to a bus stop, take a bus to a train station, ride a train for an hour, and then take another bus for half an hour. That's three hours each way, or thirty hours a week! Driving only takes an hour, so it seems like you have to drive. Even just doing errands in downtown Miami or Doral from Homestead can take all day by car. If you don't have a car, you could waste days, or you'd have to wake up super early.

If you look at Vauban, people seem happier than people who rely on cars. They talk to each other more because they walk around. The air is clean, unlike cities with smog. Cars make about 12% of Europe's pollution and over 50% in the United States. If Paris had done something sooner, it might not have so much smog. After World War II, we spent a lot of money on cars. Imagine how much better medicine or other fields could be if they had that much money. In the U.S., we spend more on highways than on public transportation. Vauban and Bogota work because shops and homes are close together, and buses can get around easily. Giving up cars completely might be hard because we're used to them, but we can change little by little.

Hey everyone! I'm really worried about all the air pollution in our country. But guess what? I have a thought: what if we all drove our cars less? Seriously, this simple thing could really cut down on pollution – it's like hitting the problem head-on, especially since cars are a big cause of it. And on top of that, get this, it could bring us closer as a community and make our towns way more fun.

Like I said, cars are a major source of air pollution. So, if we don't drive as much, there would be fewer gross pollutants going into our air. Less exhaust means less carbon messing with our climate. And guess what? That means easier breathing and better health for everyone. We've already seen the good stuff happening in places where there are fewer cars. And check this out: "Car-Free Day" is getting popular in places like Columbia and other countries in Central America. Less exhaust also means less smog – that nasty stuff that makes the air gross. If we cut down on car fumes, we can avoid the big pollution problems that cities like Paris have.

But wait, there's more! Driving less can actually make our social lives more interesting. Imagine walking around, chatting with your neighbors, or biking to the park. People in car-free areas, like Vauban, say they're way less stressed without the hassle of cars. And here's the cool part: young people don't even care that much about cars! They'd rather take a bus or carpool, which means more time with friends and family.

Just think about this: because we're always driving, sidewalks and bike paths often get ignored. But if we stop driving so much, those broken sidewalks and bumpy roads will get fixed quickly. In Columbia, "Car-Free Day" led to sidewalk repairs and tons of new bike lanes – over a hundred miles! Also, they added more parks, sports fields, and cool shopping areas. So, less driving can help us clean up our towns, make more parks, and build more fun places to hang out. And, it can keep us safer by lowering the risk of car accidents and making our streets safer overall.

So, everyone, please think about joining me in driving less. The benefits are huge – cleaner air, better health, and stronger communities for all of us!

If we ease up on car use, even if it means mixing up our travel habits, we score big. Fewer cars on the road? Fantastic! Cities choking on smog, like L.A. and Beijing, start to breathe easier. Drivers chill out, feeling less frazzled, and wallets get a little fatter. Who wouldn't jump at the chance to save some dough?

Smog's a serious nasty in packed, grimy spots. You can practically taste the smog in L.A., and Beijing? Forget about it – often buried under a blanket of bad air. Cars pump out a ton of pollution. Over in Europe, they're responsible for 15% of the airborne gunk. But here in the U.S., buckle up – it's a whopping 50%! So, fewer cars? Less pollution. Can't ditch the car completely? Snag a hybrid, like a Prius, to soften the blow. Some cities, like Bogota, throw "Car-Free Days." Big hit, and more places are hopping on board. These days give smog a serious smackdown. Paris even banned cars when the smog went bonkers. Hybrids and carpools got a pass. That just goes to show you how gnarly smog can get.

Driving can crank up your stress levels to eleven. Traffic jams? Always running late, always ticked off. Drivers get their hackles raised when someone cuts them off and sweat bullets over accidents. Imagine ditching that daily dose of drama! In Vauban, Germany, they've practically banished cars to simplify life. Folks own cars, sure, but they're parked in a pricey garage outside town (think $40,000!). You can hoof it everywhere – shops, grub, you name it. Cars are mostly for those long hauls. Strolling to grab your mail or soaking up nature without dodging cars melts away stress. Plus, no need to play Frogger to get to your favorite burger joint! And with phones and the internet, staying connected without driving's a piece of cake. Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram keep you plugged in, so driving's less of a must.

Finally, fewer cars equal fatter wallets. In car-light havens like Vauban, you might not even need a car at all. A decent car can set you back $30-40k. Just imagine what you could do with that kind of cash! A killer house, an epic vacation, or maybe foot the bill for your kid's college. And everything's still close enough to stroll to. Not a bad deal, right?

Although the electoral college and a plain popular vote both try to select an election winner, a simple popular vote could cause many problems. The electoral college, with its easier way, makes choosing a president much simpler, stopping issues early. For sure, it's not perfect, but really, the good parts are more important than the bad parts.

Now, a popular vote does have some good points. As Richard A. Posner says in "In Defense of the Electoral College," the electoral college uses electors, not just regular people, to decide ("source 3 a15"). But think about this: a popular vote gives the power right to the people, without anyone in between. But, most voters don't know who these electors are that they are voting for. Bradford Plumer, in "The Indefensible Electoral College," says that states pick the electors, so voters don't get a say ("source 2 a 10"). That's not much of a choice in who gets elected!

Even if voters feel like they don't matter, electors usually do what they are supposed to, voting how their state wants. Also, the electoral college makes it easy to say who won. Ties? Not likely. Posner says that "The winning candidate's part of the electoral college is always more than their part of the popular vote," so even a small popular vote win means a big electoral vote win for that state ("source 3 a 15"). Easy to see, right?

Besides the organization stuff, the electoral college makes candidates talk to different groups across the country. Posner says, "No one area has enough votes to win an election, so the president has to be liked by other areas too" ("source 3 a 19"). Basically, to win, a candidate needs to be liked by everyone. And don't forget swing states, where voters who know a lot decide things. Posner thinks "People in swing states decide the election and are the smartest voters" ("source 3 a 20"). Candidates have to pay attention!

Yes, some might like the popular vote, but the electoral college makes things organized and better for presidential elections. It makes sure things are done in a set way and makes candidates talk to real people by visiting different areas.

Dear Rick Scott, lately, the Electoral College has caused quite a stir in our elections. This body casts 538 votes, and a candidate needs 270 to secure a victory. States with larger populations wield more influence; for example, California boasts 55 votes while Rhode Island has a mere 3. Since Al Gore's loss in 2000, despite winning the popular vote, the Electoral College has faced scrutiny. Although some advocate for its abolishment, I disagree, asserting its crucial role in presidential elections as it ensures a clear victor, elects a president for "Everyone", acknowledges educated voters in swing states, and prevents runoff elections.

Without it, choosing a winner might become a real headache. The system is set up so a victor is always picked. Even if it's a super-close tie, the Senate breaks it. Each senator gets a vote, so smaller states have a bit less power. It also forces candidates to try and appeal to different parts of the country. That way, the president speaks for the entire nation, not just one small area. And, swing states have a lot of pull. States like Florida, New York, Texas, and California can swing the election. Getting these states is a huge step toward winning the presidency. These states are important due to their populations, but also their informed voters. In Swing states, voters are more educated and they know what the candidates stand for. The Electoral College also prevents a situation where no candidate gets a majority of the votes. Bill Clinton, for instance, won with only 43% of the votes but got 370 electoral votes. This mainly removes the need for a runoff. Even though some people claim that the Electoral College should be eliminated because voters vote for electors, it makes certain that there is a clear winner, which is essential.

Thus, let's retain the Electoral College. With its 538 votes, the requisite 270 for victory, and tie-breaking mechanisms, it ensures a conclusive outcome and elects a president for "Everyone", acknowledges educated voters in swing states, and prevents runoff elections.

Globally, cars profoundly shape lives, no doubt. Whether you acknowledge their significance, reducing car dependence would greatly benefit us and our planet. Smart urban planning that prioritizes alternatives can usher in improvements, from air quality, safer, and calmer streets, to a generally more relaxed atmosphere.

"Cars produce 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases… but it can rise to half in car-heavy US areas." Cars are significant pollution sources, particularly in congested urban centers. Someone observed that "...new suburbs might resemble Vauban, not only in rich nations, but also in developing ones, where growing middle classes own cars and worsen pollution." Experts generally agree that less car usage curbs air pollution. For example, Paris, "After days of severe pollution, Paris restricted driving to improve air quality." Fewer urban cars mean less smog and healthier air, benefiting everyone and the environment.

Tragically, car accidents are constant, often deadly. While accidents can happen, some are preventable. Think of lives lost in crowded cities or due to stressed, aggressive drivers. One driver shared, "When I drove, I was always stressed. I'm much happier now." Driving is stressful for many. Less car reliance could yield more relaxation. Picture a better environment filled with kids playing and bikes, rather than blaring horns, engines, and crashes.

Roads demand extensive space. Increased walking, biking, and less driving reduce the need for roads and allows for larger sidewalks. Cities are actively trying to decrease car dependency, "...trying to make suburbs smaller..." One approach involves concentrating shops along main streets, encouraging walking over driving to distant malls. This is a safety improvement for all.

Overall, reduced car usage is beneficial. It decreases pollution, accidents, and fosters nicer, quieter living spaces. If cities and nations focus on lowering car usage, the world would improve significantly, and daily life would be positively impacted.

Do we use cars too much, perhaps?

Like in "Paris bans driving due to smog" from Source 2, the city limited which cars could drive due to bad air. The smog had been really bad for days, almost the worst ever! Using our cars so much makes the smog worse and hurts the planet. But things can change! Driving less has lots of good things about it.

Could we live without cars? VAUBAN, in Germany, is a town without cars! They are seeing if not having cars makes people happier and healthier. Going to work and back every day can be a pain, with lots of traffic, noise, and crashes. Sources 1 and 3 say, "So, 70% of families don't have cars, and 57% sold their cars when they moved there. 'When I had a car I was always stressed. I'm much happier now,' said Heidrun Walter." This shows that you can be happier without a car.

Another good thing about driving less is that there is less smog. After the air got really bad, Paris stopped some cars from driving to fight the smog. Almost 4,000 drivers got tickets, and some lost their cars for a bit because they got mad about the tickets. But it worked! The amount of cars in Paris went down by 60% in just a few days. The air got better, so the French government let some cars drive on certain days.

Are Americans getting tired of cars? Or, is new tech making us not need to drive as much? Elisabeth Rosenthal says "the internet lets us work from home and talk online. Cities are getting better, so the suburbs aren't as nice as they used to be. Also, phones and ride-sharing apps make it easy to get around, like shared vans for going to work" (source 4, 35). Because of tech, meeting in person isn't as important, since we can talk using apps, calls, texts, and video.

Thinking about all of this, I think that driving less is good for us. I hope you think so too!

Cars, cars, they're everywhere you look! Red ones, blue ones, even those plain black ones, beeping and honking non-stop. They move people around, but what if they just vanished? Could giving up driving actually be a good thing? Well, it seems that saying goodbye to our cars could really help the Earth and our minds.

First up, fewer cars mean a healthier planet, seriously! Cars are major offenders, releasing loads of greenhouse gasses. In Europe, cars produce 12% of these gasses. But in some US areas where everyone drives? A crazy 50%! Paris once tried banning cars for a short time to fight smog. Guess what? Traffic went down by 60% after just five days! So, less cars equal less pollution, which means a safer planet for everyone. Also, young people drove much less between 2001 and 2009—a 23% drop! Experts think this will continue, maybe even cutting carbon emissions, since transportation causes a ton of pollution in America.

But hold on, there's even more good news! Getting rid of cars can make us feel great, both physically and mentally. Think about Vauban, a town in Germany where nobody drives. One person there said they were always stressed with a car, but now they're much happier. Less traffic, less road rage? Sounds like a sure way to feel less stressed. Bogota, Colombia, has a "Day Without Cars," and people love it. One person even said it’s an awesome way to chill out. Less stress equals better mental health. And that’s not all. In Bogota, a car-free day makes people walk, bike, skate, or take the bus to work. Exercise makes you happy. That’s because your brain releases feel-good chemicals. So, fewer cars could mean more exercise, which means people feeling awesome and staying healthy.

So, ditching cars could lead to a happier planet and happier people. Sure, cars are useful, but they have a downside. Next time you reach for your keys, think about leaving the car behind. Remember the clean air—cars take it away. And remember how cars can make you angry and stressed.

Ever stop to think why traffic is always so bad, especially around 5 PM? It's simple: we drive way too much. Less driving is great for many reasons and can really make our lives better.

At first, cars seem like a fast way to get around. But this one good thing doesn't make up for all the bad stuff. One big problem is that driving all the time makes us lazy. Walking or running is good exercise, but we miss out when we always drive. My friend Walter said having a car made him stressed, but he felt much happier without it. We should try to drive less and find fun, easy ways to get around. Walking might take longer, but it's worth it in the long run. No more traffic jams, and you can go to places cars can't. Plus, you can take different routes, not just roads. Rain might stop you from walking, but Antanas Mockus, who used to be the mayor of Bogota, said rain shouldn't stop us from going outside. Cars keep us dry, but they're also more dangerous than walking. Cities like Germany, Paris, and Bogota are trying to be "car-free" because of these problems.

Also, less driving could mean less road rage. People get angry at each other when they drive, usually because they're stressed. If we drive less, we'll be calmer. Car crashes are a big deal, and lots of people die in them. If we drive less, we could save lives. Cars are more trouble than they're worth. Walking and biking are easier and safer. This would also make traffic better and help us get around more easily. Drunk driving is a huge problem, and it kills many people. A drunk person walking isn't as dangerous as a drunk person driving. Really, think about the bad things about driving too much.

Finally, Barack Obama said we should drive less to help with pollution. Reports say that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less, and not getting licenses as much, which is a good sign. Paris banned cars because of bad smog, stopping cars from going to certain areas. Diesel fuel made the smog worse. In France, 67% of cars use diesel, but in other Western European countries, it's only 53%. Bogota wants to "make other ways of getting around more appealing and reduce smog." If you break the rules, you get a fine. They learned that less driving could make people less stressed and clean up the air. Bill Ford wants cities where "people walking, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses all work together to save time, use less gas, reduce pollution, and be safer." These are just some of the good things about driving less. Going car-free or driving less would be a big change. Let's stop pollution, prevent crashes, and stop underage driving. Think about the bad things about driving too much compared to the good things about driving less.

For quite some time, we've been battling pollution and global warming. You may wonder, "Can't we fix this quickly?" Cars are a MAJOR culprit. People simply need to use their cars less often. Some places are taking action. Take Vauban, Germany: cars are completely forbidden! In Paris, France, only specific license plates are allowed on certain days. Meanwhile, Bogota, Colombia, is also doing its part.

Consider Vauban, Germany, which features cities devoid of driveways or parking. As a result, people must pay a lot for garage space. According to some sources, cars produce roughly twelve percent of Europe's greenhouse gas emissions. It might not seem like much, but it's significant. If we construct these car-free cities, pollution will decrease because of less driving. As Heidrun Walter stated, "When I had a car, I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." This proves that life is less stressful without a car! Reduced gas and repair expenses translate to less financial burden, simplifying life. Thus, cities with costly parking are beneficial for both the environment and people's well-being.

Another option? Adopt Paris, France's strategy: odd-numbered plates on Mondays, and even-numbered plates on Tuesdays. The pollution was severe, even worse than Beijing! Yet, remarkably, pollution decreased by sixty percent in just FIVE DAYS after implementing this. Incredible, isn't it? This demonstrates that such car restrictions are effective and yield rapid results!

Then there are "no car days," like those in Bogota, Colombia. Carlos Arturo Plaza aptly noted, "It's a good way to take away stress and lower air pollution," highlighting the clear relationship between cars and negative impacts. The source also reveals that Cali and Valledpar, other Colombian cities, are also participating. Furthermore, leaders from other countries are observing and seem impressed. This suggests that the concept could spread globally! Clearly, this is another highly effective method to reduce car usage.

In conclusion, cars pose a significant problem. Yet, many individuals continue to drive without considering the accumulated pollution. Effective solutions include cities with paid parking, odd-even license plate systems, and implementing "no car days."

The U.S.? It is ours, we run it, and it exists solely for us! We select our leaders via voting – appearing to be the fairest method for choosing the President and Vice President, agreed?

However, the electoral college? Debatable. Yes, electors may possess extensive knowledge of the country and believe they know what is best. Yet, the truth is, their lives differ from ours. Average people understand the issues they encounter. As Bradford Plummer stated, "the best arguments supporting it lack real-world basis" (source 14). They may grasp Washington's complexities, but frequently overlook the struggles of ordinary individuals.

The populace must have governmental input. If the electoral college alone chose the President, people would feel excluded. Maintaining peace requires consent. Without it, chaos could ensue. Richard A. Posner notes that citizens would "feel disenfranchised, believing the new president disregards their interests, and isn't truly their leader" (source 19). Consider: if people are upset when their candidate loses NOW, imagine the frustration if they lacked a vote entirely!

The electoral college? Simply unjust. Richard A. Posner noted some advocate for it due to "its undemocratic origins" (source 17). The popular vote functions adequately. So, why complicate matters? Change can be positive, but not this time.

In conclusion? Eliminating the popular vote would be erroneous, fundamentally unfair, and harmful. It contradicts the core principles of "we the people."

Even though it has issues, there are still reasons to keep the Electoral College. Each state gets a number of electoral votes based on how many representatives they have in Congress (House plus Senate equals Electors!). All together, there are 538 electors in the whole country. To win the presidency, you need more than half of those votes, which is 270. People say it’s good because it makes sure there’s a clear winner, the president represents a lot of different people, it affects what happens in states that could go either way, it gives more power to bigger states, and it stops elections from needing to be redone.

Basically, the Electoral College is there to make sure the presidential election is fair and that outside forces don’t mess with it. It’s the final say in who wins, but it’s not perfect. The “winner-takes-all” approach, where the candidate who gets the most votes in a state gets all of its electoral votes, can seem unfair. Candidates might focus on big states because they have more electoral votes. Also, someone can win the popular vote but still lose the election because of how the Electoral College votes. Getting the Electoral College on your side is what matters, and that’s not always a sure thing, even if you have a lot of support from voters.

One reason people like the Electoral College is that it’s supposed to make it clear who won. It’s thought to reduce arguments about the popular vote, as long as the electors vote the way they’re supposed to. It also makes presidents think about what different parts of the country need, because no one region can decide the election by itself. The effect on swing states is another thing people bring up, because the winner gets everything. It's said that this also helps the big states through more influence. Since the population is bigger, states get more elected officials. Because of this, candidates tend to focus on what those states want. Finally, this makes it less likely that no one gets enough votes, which prevents run-off elections.

Even though the Electoral College has some good things going for it, it also has problems. Still, some people think it’s the best way to choose a president. They warn against only using the popular vote, saying that people might not be informed enough to make good decisions. Even though there are rules for elections, those who support the Electoral College believe that it’s the best thing for the country. So, it’s important to think carefully about the reasons why people want to keep the Electoral College.

Dear State Senator, a question: Could we rethink how we pick our president? Let's chat about chucking the old Electoral College for a straight-up popular vote. Plain and simple: the people, not some fancy electors, should call the shots on who leads us.

Lots of big names want the Electoral College gone, you know? Nixon, Carter, Dole – even the Chamber of Commerce! There's a worry hanging in the air. It just feels wrong that electors, with their own opinions, could change who wins the White House.

Remember Obama versus Romney? Obama crushed it in the Electoral College, like, 29 to only 3 for Romney. This shows how rigged the system is, blocking what the people really want. A popular vote? Fair for everyone.

Sure, the Electoral College can be handy when nobody gets a big win, like Nixon in '68 or Clinton in '92. Both had just 43 percent! It kept things from going wild. But it gives too much power to things other than the popular vote. Me? I think the American people should be in charge.

Thank you, Senator, for thinking about my idea: Should we stick with the Electoral College, or switch to letting the popular vote decide?

Respected Senator, some like the Electoral College. They say it makes candidates talk to swing states and get support from small states, like Source 3 says in "In Defense of the Electoral College: Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing a president." But I disagree strongly. I think it's a bad system and not fair to people.

Source 2, called "The Indefensible Electoral College: Why even the best-laid defenses of the system are wrong," shows how unfair it is to voters. Most states do winner-take-all, so candidates only care about close races in "swing" states. Other states get ignored. A candidate could be losing overall but still win a state because of the Electoral College. Voters might not even know what the winner believes until they're sworn in! This means the winner might not really care about that state. Source 3 also says, "The Electoral College requires a presidential candidate to have transregional appeal. No region has enough electoral votes to elect a president. So a solid regional favorite, such as Rodney in the South, has no incentive to campaign heavily in those states, for he gains no electoral votes by increasing his plurality in states that he knows he will win." If Mitt Romney ran against Barack Obama, Obama's strength in the North would hurt Romney, which is what the Electoral College wants, supposedly.

So, I still think the Electoral College is a bad and unfair way to pick a president. Source 2 backs me up, saying, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational. The best arguments in favor of it are mostly assertions without much basis in reality." This means the Electoral College doesn't fit with today's democracy and might not even work well.

A lot of folks don't like the Electoral College, claiming it's just not fair and wanting it gone for good. Instead of directly picking the President, we vote for "electors," who then do the choosing based on who got the most votes. This makes some folks think our leaders aren't really being chosen by the people themselves. You can even win without getting the most votes, since those electors are supposed to vote how the people want anyway. Many people think this whole thing is strange and that we should just get rid of it altogether.

Plus, some say that many voters don't even know enough about the electors they're voting for. People in some states don't see as many campaign ads or news stories, which makes it hard to make a good choice. Presidential candidates often focus on states they think they can win, trying to get attention and support. While a president should try to appeal to everyone, this focus can make folks in less important states feel like their votes don't matter as much. Even though people think they're voting for a candidate, they're really voting for electors. So, winning the popular vote doesn't mean you'll be president, which makes some people want a more direct way of voting.

Political parties pick the "electors" who will vote for their candidate. Even though it doesn't happen often, it's still possible for the Electoral College winner to lose the popular vote. Like in 2000, Gore got more votes, but Bush won, which hadn't happened since 1888. This could happen again, meaning you could win or lose no matter how many votes you get. This can cause arguments, since most states give all their electoral votes to whoever wins the state. Still, it helps avoid situations where no one gets a majority, which could mean more elections. The Electoral College makes sure there's a clear winner. People want their votes to matter, but that's often not the case.

A lot of folks see the Electoral College as a weird, messed-up way to choose leaders. It's supposed to get people involved in voting, but it can mess up democracy by making individual votes less important. In a democracy where voting matters, it's super important to make good decisions. The system should make sure that voters feel like their vote really counts.

Esteemed State Senator, I pen this missive to champion the preservation of our Electoral College. Ditching it for a popular vote system in presidential races seems, well, not needed. It’s valuable because it lets the House of Representatives play a part in picking the president, which boosts fairness and keeps things from getting too messy. Plus, it helps everyone understand how we elect our president.

The House of Representatives' involvement is a huge win for the Electoral College. Source One, paragraph three, explains that each state gets electors based on its congressional size: one for each House member, plus two for the Senators. This makes sure lots of folks have a say. Source One, paragraph five, points out that "The electors are usually picked by the candidate's political party," showing the House isn't the only one at the elections table. The government has a very significant role in the Electoral College, due to its highly important votes.

Electoral fairness is another feather in the Electoral College's cap. Source Three shouts out that "The Electoral College fixes the problem where big states get too much power because the Senate isn't fair..." This calms worries about how we elect the president. Source Three also says that "So, if everything else is the same, big states get more attention from presidential candidates than small states do..." The Electoral College is beneficial, especially for larger states.

Importantly, the Electoral College is a safety net against electoral messes. Source Three, paragraph twenty-two, stresses that "The Electoral College stops problems in elections where no candidate gets most of the votes." This makes the voting process way easier. The same source and paragraph also indicates that "There's no need for extra elections when no one wins most of the votes; the Electoral College makes sure there's always a clear winner..." It ensures a clear winner, boosting trust in our elections.

Sure, some might hate the Electoral College because their favorite candidate might lose, but that’s not a good enough reason to scrap it. It keeps order in presidential elections. Source Two, paragraph Ten, says that "With the Electoral College, we don't vote for the president, but for electors, who then pick the president." Some people feel the Electoral College is unfair when they do not get a vote directed towards their candidate of choice. But electors vote for who the state wants. Source Two also says, "Basically, the Electoral College is unfair. Because of the winner-takes-all system, candidates only focus on close races in 'swing' states." Even with the worries, the Electoral College does more than count votes; it helps the government pick the best president.

To wrap it up, I’m all for keeping the Electoral College around. It’s a win-win for both citizens and the government. It makes elections fair, stops problems and confusion, and, most importantly, lowers problems, so folks are happier with how things turn out. The Electoral College makes elections clearer and more organized every year. Despite substantial governmental involvement, the importance of individual votes remains undiminished. Individual votes are essential to the Electoral College, as are government votes.

This here Electoral College? Antiquated, I say! Back in the day, with tech that wasn't so hot, it was used to tally votes fairly across all the states. The states sent their people to vote, and it worked well enough then. Now, though? Tech is way better, so it feels clunky and easy to mess with. If you're a Republican living in a state that's all about the Democrats, your vote might feel like it doesn't even matter, right? A national vote could be the way to go; then even a small voice like yours would be heard. Plus, this winner-takes-all deal in most states makes those big states super important. Smaller states end up with less info, so voting is hard because they don't know what's what.

When this whole thing kicked off, the telegraph was the cutting edge! Instead of relying on states to count everything up and send electors (who are supposed to be fair) all over the place with the votes, folks needed a better plan. Imagine people on horses hauling paper votes! Risky stuff! Papers could get lost, riders hurt, entire areas missed. The Electoral College was a smart solution, necessary even. But like that telegraph, it's just out of date now. Nowadays, your cell phone can hold millions of votes. Computers can do math super fast, spitting out facts, answers, even ideas. We can send votes in a flash, so even the smallest mistakes can be found and fixed. A much better, safer, and spot-on method? A computer program to count it all up! Every place sends the info to one big computer, it's checked a bunch before the final result. And to stop any cheating, you can use a bunch of computers. So, it's just a waste that some politicians just won't let it go. It's old!

Seriously, it's not fair to a lot of people. A Democrat who lives in a Republican state casts their vote, but the Republican still wins. That Democrat's vote, the vote of their family, and all the other Democrats in that state? Worthless. Gone. Every single election. A popular vote, on the other hand, means all those votes help their candidate. Then they are worth something! Winner-takes-all just makes it worse. You win California by a hair, and you get ALL their votes, ignoring almost half the people who voted the other way! That's not what most folks want. Big states like California, Texas, and Florida win out because they have the most votes. Small states like Alaska and Hawaii? Not so much. Politicians focus all their time on the big ones, ignoring the little guys. Voters in places like the Dakotas or Montana might not be in the know, so they just pick a party. All the big fights and ads happen in the big states. Sure, those ads are biased, but voters still learn something. Swing states get bombarded with info as candidates fight for their votes. Small states, or the ones that always vote the same, get skipped over, leaving voters in the dark, and their votes might not even matter. A popular vote means candidates have to go everywhere, putting an end to the big-state bias and making voting fairer for all.

The bottom line is this: The Electoral College might have been the best way to vote back in the 1700s and 1800s, but today? It's old news and not fair at all. Talking and traveling are easy now, so it's not just out of date, but it's also slanted, useless, and bad for our democracy. It's time to move on to new tech and ditch the Electoral College!

Cars are used less around the world! Giving them up is a good idea, as it cuts pollution and saves money. People want to make changes or help their area! Places such as China, Spain, and the U.S. are all using less cars. Not everyone is happy, but many like it because pollution is not good. Just think of cleaner air if the world cut cars by half. Cars are very important, but they also make problems!

In Vauban, Germany, cars aren't very common! You can own one, but where to put it can be hard and costly! Forty grand to park at home? Wow! Many leave their cars somewhere else. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote about it: "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." Seventy percent of families don't have a car, and over half sold them when they moved. This shows why going without cars is good and bad. Saving money? For sure! No car payments, no parking fees, and no gas. Plus, cleaner air too.

Paris, France? Very polluted, so they banned some cars. Robert Duffer stated that if your license plate ended in an even number, then stay home! Or you would have to pay a €22 fine. The next day, odd numbers. Pollution decreased! Very impressive in a big city! Their goal? Cleaner air and healthy lungs. Duffer noted, "4,000 drivers were fined," and "27 people had their cars impounded." Traffic went down sixty percent! The ban ended when the air was cleaner.

Bogota, Columbia, had a small idea that became huge. Andrew Selsky tells the story: "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." It started small, but now it is trending.

One day each year, no cars allowed! But buses and taxis are, of course. Seven million people are trying to breathe better. Alternative transport and less pollution is the point. Break the rules and you get fined! Selsky says Bogota has "118 MILES of bicycle paths!" Which means lots of bikes. Parks were improved, sports got cleaner, and the air was fresher. Now, other cities are copying them.

Rosenthal writes about the U.S. too. "Recent studies suggest that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses." Rosenthal 29. Which helps "Obama's ambitious goals to curb the United States' greenhouse gas emissions!" Rosenthal 29. How about New York City? Many taxis and subways exist! Parking is hard, so people don't use cars. Think of the money saved! Traffic is still bad, but think of more cars. The city uses public transit. But this is expensive, and without it, the air would be worse.

So, less cars? Great for everyone to enjoy. Fighting pollution, making cleaner air, and saving money. Giving up the car is a win-win for all! Walk if you can! It is good for your health, wallet, and the planet. Cars are fast, but think about the cost in money and pollution. Try a life without cars! See what happens and enjoy it too.

Car companies are doing great, as the auto industry is taking off and profits are up! But wait, and think about cutting back on driving in our neighborhoods. Studies show that too much driving can really make you stressed. Like Carlos Arturo Plaza said in Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," using cars less can chill you out. Driving less is good, but if everyone in town does it, it's even better! Even though lots of folks still drive in America, fewer people are buying cars, which means they might be trying other stuff like carpooling. Taking the bus might be slower, but it's worth it. Driving less or not at all in our towns can lead to a bunch of cool and good things.

Cars make a big mess, polluting the air and hurting the ozone layer. We like how easy cars are, and getting a license at 16 feels awesome. But, we forget the bad stuff about owning a car, like needing fossil fuels. Imagine how much better things could be if we didn't use cars so much. Cutting back on cars would really help the ozone layer, and we could breathe cleaner air, like in other places. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," shows how well these bans can work. Paris, which is known for being polluted, stopped people from driving to clean up the air. Cold nights and warm days trap car fumes, making the pollution super bad, so they tried to fix it with the ban. After the ban, the air in Paris was better. The article said, "Paris typically has more smog than other European capitals... Last week Paris had 147 ,micrograms of particulate matter PM per cubic meter compared with the 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London..."

No one says we should ditch cars completely, but if we think about how bad they are, we might see that the bad outweighs the good. Vauban, Germany, gets this and has changed how they live. Elizabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," talks about how people live without cars in that town. People there say they're happier because there's no traffic or accidents. The town made it happen by getting rid of street parking, driveways, and garages. It's weird, but you don't need those things without cars. Instead, the town made better sidewalks and easy-to-reach stuff. One person in Vauban, a media trainer with two kids, said, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Before, the government spent 80 percent of its money on highways and only 20 percent on other ways to get around. But, that's going to change in the next six years, which means they're planning better.

So, when we think about getting around in cities, there's a lot to think about. But, driving less has big perks, like more government money for other stuff, cleaner air, feeling better, and saving money. We have to ask if driving all the time is worth it, considering all the problems it causes.

In the U.S., having a car is super common. Folks dig how fast cars get them places; walking seems way harder. But, guess what? When everyone's driving, roads turn into one big parking lot. And here's the kicker: cars mess up the planet and make the air gross. So, using fewer cars? Good news! It's like giving the environment and our bodies a high-five.

Cutting back on car use? Big win! We're talking a healthier planet and feeling awesome. Seriously, one report said, "Cars? They're behind 12 percent of Europe's greenhouse gases... and in some U.S. spots, it's up to 50 percent!" Since cars cough out those gases directly, carpooling or hopping on the bus can totally help. Plus, walking or biking? That's how we stay in tip-top shape.

Smog, that nasty air goo, hangs over cities because of cars. It's packed with bad stuff that can make you sick and even cause more asthma, especially in cities. For example, in Beijing, China, you can barely see because of car smog. "Paris? They stopped some cars to clean up the air." Yep, Paris did the same thing to fix this mess. Using fewer cars? It's like hitting the "clean air" button.

But wait, there's more! Driving can also mess with water and land. Cars slurp up gasoline, which we dig out of the ground. And guess what? Digging for it can hurt nature and cause oil spills, which are a nightmare for sea creatures. So, let's hop on trains or buses, or just walk or bike! That way, we won't need as much gas. Cars guzzle a lot of fuel, so fewer cars equals less demand.

People are saying life's easier and less stressful without a car. No more traffic jams or fender benders! At a "Day Without Cars" thing in Bogota, Colombia, a businessman named Carlos Arturo Plaza said, "'It's great to have less stress and less polluted air!'" See? Less car use equals less stress. Another person, Heidrun Walter, said, "'When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way!'" She lives in a German town without cars and is way happier now. Less stress? That's another reason to ditch the car.

Some folks only need a car when the buses and trains are a joke. Someone pointed out, "They walk, take the bus, or share cars." and "'A car is just for getting from A to B when the bus is awful.'" By skipping unnecessary car trips, we can build a better system where we don't need cars as much, and the other ways to get around get better. Better buses mean fewer cars, and that's a win for the environment and our lives.

Using cars less? It's good for the planet and chills you out. One source blabbed, "Cars? They're the second-biggest polluter in the U.S.!" By skipping those extra drives and using buses or other options, we can cut a ton of pollution in America. Less pollution, healthier people, and less stress? All that from using fewer cars!

More and more folks are realizing that driving less is a great idea. Elisabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," talks about Vauban, Germany, where you barely see any cars. As Heidrun Walter, who lives in Vauban, said, not having cars makes the town more fun; you hear kids playing and bikes, not just noisy cars. The streets are full of life because there's no car noise or pollution.

Cutting back on cars could really help suburbs a lot. Rosenthal's article mentions that for 20 years, cities have been getting denser and easier to walk around. The idea is to have smaller suburbs with awesome public transportation and shops you can walk to, so you don't have to drive everywhere. Even the US EPA is on board with moving away from relying so much on cars.

One big plus of driving less is that it cuts down on smog and air pollution. Robert Duffer's "Paris bans driving due to smog" explains how Paris tried to clean up its air by banning some cars. This cut traffic by 60% after five days of bad smog, and pollution levels got close to those in Beijing, China, which is known for having terrible air quality. By limiting car travel, Paris managed to lower air pollution and traffic.

Andrew Selsky's article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in bogota" gives more good reasons to drive less. It's about Bogota's car-free day, which tries to get people to use other ways to get around and reduce smog. This has led to new parks, sports areas, better sidewalks, new places to eat, and fancy shops. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, says that riding a tandem bike with his wife makes him less stressed and reduces air pollution.

Basically, there are tons of good things about driving less, like less smog and pollution, and easier access to stores nearby. This is not just good for us. And it also makes sure that our planet can support people in the future.

The creation of the wheel led to cars. Now, factories pump out tons of cars each year, making it simpler for people to travel in cities and across the globe. But, gas cars let out carbon dioxide. This gas hurts the earth, causing problems like ozone loss and acid rain. Also, too many cars in one place lead to traffic and other headaches. Big debts also play a role in the amount of cars. If we want to save our planet, we should drive and build fewer cars.

Our planet is getting hotter because of carbon dioxide in the air. Cars are a big part of this pollution. Like it says in article 1, paragraph 5, "Cars create 12% of the greenhouse gasses in Europe... and can be as high as half in the US". Less car production and use can help the Earth out. Reducing global warming can help ice regions stick around while keeping animals, like penguins and polar bears, safe and sound.

Traffic jams are the worst since you never know when they'll happen. Passage 2, paragraph 14, says, "traffic went down 60% in Paris" when they limited license plates on certain days. Less traffic means easier commutes. What could have been a three-hour wait can be a quick twenty-minute trip. This time gives people more chances to get things done. Less cars and traffic can also mean less accidents.

Paying for a car, whether you lease or buy it, can hurt your wallet. Debt has been around since the beginning of money. Anyone can go into debt. As it says in article 4, paragraph 32, "Americans without cash couldn't get new cars, and the unemployed weren't driving to work anyway". Spending too much money can harm you and the economy. Reducing car debt can help the country's economy.

In the end, there are lots of good reasons to cut back on driving. Gas cars mess up the planet, making ice melt faster, damaging the ozone, and creating bad weather. Less traffic makes it easier to get around and gives people more free time. Cutting back on car production and leasing can help the economy. In short, less cars are good for everyone.

So, the Electoral College, right? It's been around a long time, but people aren't always happy with it. Here's the deal: you don't vote for the President directly. Instead, you vote for "electors," but heads up – they might not vote the way they promised! Remember the year 2000? The Electoral College picked a person that most voters didn't prefer! Some say it keeps things from going to a runoff, but that's not really a strong argument.

This old system can cause trouble, just like in 2000. That election? Total mess. Ditching it would stop more messes from happening. Back in 1960, some racists almost switched Democratic electors in Louisiana to vote against Kennedy. And those electors can change their minds, even when they shouldn't. It doesn't happen often, but it's still a HUGE problem.

It's not just strange; the Electoral College is also unfair. Each state gets at least one vote, so someone from Wyoming (only 500,000 people) has the same power as California's 55 representatives (35 million voters!). Some say ties never happen, but remember 1979? A few thousand votes in Ohio could've caused a tie! Because of "winner-take-all," candidates ignore a bunch of states. In 2000, some states didn't see any visits or ads, so voters couldn't even choose who to back or reject.

A lot of folks feel left out, especially after Al Gore won the popular vote but lost in 2000. People might skip voting if they think their state is already decided, like Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. But don't assume people care more about showing off than making their vote count. As we said, every vote counts. Popular vote might mean more ties, but it's still better than this old, broken system. Over 60 percent of voters dislike the Electoral College – that's a big number! We need to fix these issues before things get worse.

It's pretty clear: we've seen failures, close calls, major risks, and unfairness. The Electoral College fans are wrong. The 2000 election and all the angry voters show just how crazy it is to keep it around. This system has been here for a while, and it probably won't disappear quickly. But now that more people see the issues, we can make some positive changes.

Studies indicate that less car usage leads to great results: The air becomes cleaner, people discover new ways of getting around, and communities might even become safer.

For starters, reducing the number of cars on the road can lead to a decrease in air pollution, which is great for our health and the environment. Vauban, Germany, serves as a great example. Research shows that cars are major contributors to greenhouse gas emissions. In Europe, cars account for approximately 12% of these emissions, but in car-dependent areas of the U.S., this figure can reach 50%! For this reason, cities like Vauban, Paris, Bogota, and some U.S. cities are working hard to reduce car emissions and create a cleaner environment. Experts generally believe that this trend of driving less will continue, which is excellent news for carbon emissions. After all, transportation is the second-largest source of pollution in the U.S. Secondly, reducing our driving leads to more fun travel alternatives. Instead of just driving, we might walk to parks, enjoy sports, and get more exercise. Also, fewer cars lead to less traffic. In Bogota, Colombia, they organized a car-free day, and everyone walked, biked, skated, or used public transport. The streets were noticeably clear! In Paris, certain cars were even banned to improve air quality, resulting in a 60% drop in traffic! During these times, public transport was free from Friday to Monday, proving that less car use leads to benefits for everyone.

Heidrun Walter, a car-free individual, expresses greater happiness without the stress of driving. Similarly, businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza views reducing car use as a way to lower stress and clean the air. Furthermore, reduced driving boosts safety. M. Sivak's research points out that fewer young people (ages 16 to 39) are getting driver's licenses, while older generations are more likely to maintain them. One study showed a 23% decrease in driving among young people between 2001 and 2009. This change in our travel habits is causing great improvements in different areas, resulting in a win-win situation. The number of miles driven is nearly 9% below its peak, and fewer people own cars now compared to before the economic downturn. In addition, due to the changing driving population, people may not go back to depending on cars as much as they did before.

To summarize, cutting back on driving can help the environment, provide more travel options, and increase community safety.

Okay, so, traffic is, like, such a pain, yeah? Doesn't matter if you drive or not! For ages, everyone used cars.
