But, because the air is getting all polluted, not as many people are driving, you know? If we stopped using cars so much, think of how awesome it would be: less stress, better air, and just a way better feeling!

Imagine how relaxed we could be without always getting stuck in traffic or having to be on time, all the time. We could bike, take the bus or train, or just walk! There's this town, Vauban, in Germany, that's mostly car-free, one study said. People there are said to be more chill because you can walk everywhere.

Plus, cars mess up the planet and make the air gross. In Europe, cars cause, like, 12% of all those global warming fumes! And in some parts of the US where everyone drives all the time, it's a crazy 50%! Paris is a good example of how cars can mess things up. One study showed that their air was, like, as bad as Beijing's at one point. They even had to stop cars until the air got better!

Using cars less isn't just good for the earth, it's good for us too. There's this place, Bogota, Colombia, that has a day without cars every year, another study said. It really makes the air cleaner, and people love it! Even when it's raining, people go to bike races and stuff. It worked so well that two other cities in Colombia are trying it too. Bogota has the most bike paths in Latin America – a cool 118 miles! It's not just the government trying to get people to use fewer cars, regular people are doing it too. One source said that driving is down by 9%. Maybe it's because more people are working from home and don't need to drive as much.

So, things are changing, maybe for the better. We're not using cars as much, which means less pollution, less stress, and a better life for everyone!

Under the Westminster way, the party that holds the most seats in the law-making body gets to pick who's in charge. But with a vote open to everyone, the entire nation gets to speak up. Voting by the people lets them use different methods, which is not like the Westminster system. Since some leaders can be good at getting others on their side but are actually bad, it's super important to make sure no one misuses their power in national votes. If we want to keep our freedoms safe and make sure the people really are in charge, then the United States needs to keep the Electoral College around.

The reason the U.S. has been a republic for so long is because the people who started it were smart. The Electoral College was thought up using ideas from the British system, but also from the Iroquois Confederacy, though not as many people talk about that because of unfair biases. Just like how in England, the main group of lawmakers chooses the Prime Minister, the people who founded America wanted a way to pick their leader. They were scared that if people voted directly for a leader, that person might try to take over, so they made the President have to listen to Congress. Congress can kick the President out if they do bad things. The Electoral College is like a wall that keeps dictators away. If you look back at history, Hitler didn't get voted in, and Mussolini only rose to power because everyone liked him. The Electoral College is made up of regular folks, and they keep things like that from happening. It's not perfect, but it's a form of democracy where people have someone representing them. Someone named Richard A. Posner said the Electoral College isn't really democratic these days because it's not the people voting for the president; it's the electors. All governments can mess up. Even though the U.S. government usually tries to do good, some people still want to get rid of the Electoral College. Even Nixon, who had to quit because of a scandal, used to say it should go. People like Bradford Plumer think it's not fair and that it's old news. But the Electoral College is like a guard dog for elections. It makes sure things are fair, and it gives a final vote through people who stand for each state and its people. That's what representative voting is all about.

The Electoral College is there to stand for all of us, and the people who started America thought about it carefully. Even though people argue about it, lots of great presidents, like Kennedy, Roosevelt, Lincoln, Washington, and Jefferson, all got elected because of it. If the U.S. wants to stay free from anyone taking over and keep the power with the people, then it has to keep the Electoral College.

Senator, this electoral college thing? Think it's past its prime. People should just pick the president themselves, straight up. Right now? It's unfair, messed up, just wrong. Life's hard, sure, but we gotta try for a fair country, especially with all that debt. And frankly, Obama made the debt worse. Unequal votes 'cause of where you live? That's not right at all.

Electors got all the power, making us feel small. We vote for them, but they ignore what we want. They don't listen, and it makes us not trust the government. We vote, so shouldn't we get a say on the electoral college too? Lots of folks probably agree. Maybe I'm young, but listen to what people are saying.

Plumer was right, we don't vote for president straight up. Remember 2000? Gore got more votes, lost anyway. If everyone's equal, why do electors get special treatment? It's like our votes don't matter. If they pick the president, why vote at all?

People say it stops one group from having all the power, but that's not how it works. Winner-take-all in each state decides everything. Who are these electors anyway, better than us? I'm young, but I'm smart. Why ignore my vote? I'm saying it: America's built on a lie, not fair. We think we're in charge, but the elected officials and the electoral college are really calling the shots.

America's going downhill and will keep on unless we change things. Getting rid of the electoral college? It would help. Why listen to a kid? 'Cause I know what it's like to be ignored, even if you're smart. America knows what's best. Not just a few people should decide. Let us vote for president, straight up, just once. If it doesn't work, go back to the old way. But if we don't try, we're stuck.

Okay, buckle up – our lives depend on this. Here’s a rewritten version, aiming for lower perplexity and higher burstiness, keeping everything else the same:

Cars? Most shrug. But think about it – they're a HUGE deal. We see them every day, yeah, but tons of people can't afford one or even drive. Check out Vauban, Germany. They straight-up BANNED cars! Okay, there's a train to Freiburg and roads around the edge, but inside? Nada. You *can* own a car, BUT parking? Forget about it. Super limited, crazy expensive. You gotta buy a spot in some garage way out for, like, $40,000 ON TOP of your house price! So, guess what? Seventy percent of the folks there DITCHED their cars. More than half SOLD their cars after they moved! Heidrun Walter, a media trainer and mom, said it best: "I was always stressed with a car. This is SO much better." Vauban, since 2006, shows where things are going: suburbs that DON'T need cars. Smart planning, people!

Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote a piece, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," and she nailed it. Cars are great, sure, but hello, AIR POLLUTION! They trash the environment! Paris got so smoggy, they had to ban drivers for a while. One Monday? Even-numbered plates STAYED HOME. Or pay a fine of about 31 dollars. Next day, maybe odd numbers? The air was SO BAD because the weather trapped all the car fumes. Crazy! Traffic dropped like a rock – 60% in FIVE DAYS! The air was Beijing-level bad. As soon as they banned half the cars, the air got better INSTANTLY. Odd numbers drove the next day.

Bogota, Colombia, went CAR-FREE for a DAY. Walk, bike, skate, bus – your call! No cars ANYWHERE. You know what that meant? ZERO ACCIDENTS! And get this – they do it EVERY YEAR. This was year THREE! They figured seven million people would use something other than a car. It rained, but who cared? People still came out! Mayor Antanas Mockus said, "Rain didn't stop anyone!" Everyone said they felt less stressed, and the air was cleaner. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, biked with his wife. "It's good for stress and pollution," he said.

Okay, in the US, cars are HUGE. But…are we done with them? Some think so. We're a big place with suburbs everywhere, so we love our cars. Model T, Detroit, "Mustang Sally" – all American. But maybe we're changing. By 2013, driving was down almost 8%. That's 1995 levels! Money problems didn't help, plus fewer people needed to commute. But Michael Sivak at Michigan said something else was happening. "Car ownership was dropping BEFORE the economy tanked." Huh. We need other ways to get around so people don't just go back to driving too much. New York's bike shares, tolls, car shares? That's progress. One study said young people drove 23% LESS between 2001 and 2009!

People want fewer cars for all sorts of reasons: safety, money, and the environment.

Driving is something most people do a lot each week. For me, it's school, sports, and visiting friends. Some say driving is good because it helps us travel and get things done. But driving too much? That's not good. It might be tough to drive less, and maybe some don't think it matters. But driving less is awesome! The air gets cleaner, we get healthier, and we feel happier. That's why leaders want us to cut back on driving.

Back in 2014, Robert Duffer pointed out that Beijing's air was terrible because of cars (source 2 paragraph 14). Paris had the same problem, so they came up with a rule: cars could only drive on certain days, depending on their license plates. Break the rule, and you'd get a fine (source 2 paragraphs 10-19)! This made Paris's air better super fast, so they stopped the rule after just one day. See? Driving less works! Elizabeth Rosenthal noted in 2009 that cars cause a lot of pollution in both Europe and the US (source 1 paragraph 5). We need to drive less for cleaner air and to use less gas.

Sure, driving to work might seem easy. But think about how much happier and healthier we could be if we drove less! Rosenthal wrote about Vauban, Germany, where most families don't even own a car (source 1 paragraph 3)! Heidrum Walter lives in Vauban without a car and says she feels way less stressed now (source 1 paragraph 3)! In Bogota, Columbia, Andrew Selsky reported that they banned cars for a day in 2002 (source 3 paragraph 21). Carlos Arturo Plaza in Bogota said it was nice to relax and have less pollution. These people prove that driving less makes us feel better. So, let's drive less!

Driving less is so great that tons of people want us to do it! The EPA is all about supporting communities where people drive less. Leaders are taking steps to help make it easier (source 1 paragraph 9). David Goldberg mentioned that we've built everything around cars since World War II, but that needs to change. Mimi Sheller said things are already changing, and we're seeing a big shift (source 4 paragraph 35)! This support shows that driving less has a ton of benefits. So, we should all drive less!

The world is changing, no joke! Young people are driving less these days (source 4 paragraph 41). Now is the perfect time to ditch the car and hop on a bus, bike, or train. Drive less, and we help keep the air clean. Plus, we feel happier and healthier! That's why the EPA wants us to drive less. We need to act now to protect the future. Driving less has so many awesome benefits. So, seriously, let's drive less!

Dear Florida Senator, please hear me out! As a student, my knowledge of politics is admittedly limited. Nevertheless, I feel compelled to express my support for keeping the Electoral College. Doesn't this established system streamline and expedite the presidential selection process?

The Electoral College consists of 538 electors, requiring a minimum of 270 votes for a candidate to secure victory. While some argue that we don't directly vote for the president but rather for electors pledged to them, I believe we're essentially endorsing a candidate's platform. We're voting for a promise, and this system elevates the importance of winning those swing states. That is a pivotal aspect, in my opinion. As highlighted in "In Defense of the Electoral College: Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing the President," swing states become more actively engaged in the election process. This potentially mitigates the risk of prolonged election disputes, ensuring that smaller states retain significance.

The "winner-take-all" approach, wherein the victor of a state's popular vote claims all of its electoral votes, is prevalent in many states. Alternatives like "proportional representation" exist, but I strongly favor the "winner-take-all" model. Although certain aspects of the Electoral College might seem unusual, this specific feature simplifies the allocation of all a state's votes to a single candidate, fostering internal stability. Proportional representation lacks this crucial effect, thus lengthening the duration of the presidential election.

Lastly, the Electoral College boasts a rich history. According to "What Is the Electoral College?", "The founding fathers established it in the Constitution as a compromise between election of the President by a vote in Congress and election of the President by a popular vote of qualified citizens." This historical foundation holds immense significance. I extend my gratitude for your attention to my perspective.

Dear State Senator,

I truly believe it is time to say goodbye to the Electoral College, and hello to a system where the president is chosen by the people through a simple popular vote. Every single one of us ought to have a say in selecting someone who can genuinely make our lives better. The way it is now, where we vote for electors who then pick the president, just feels plain wrong. Presidential elections should welcome everyone, not shut people out. The time is now to completely rethink our voting process.

Think about it: why should anyone have to vote for electors who then get to vote for the president? As "The Indefensible Electoral College" (source 2) so perfectly puts it, the Electoral College is stacked because candidates only focus on states they barely stand a chance of winning. In contrast, "In Defense of the Electoral College" (source 3) owns up to the fact that no single area has enough electoral votes to win on its own. This really drives home how the Electoral College screws over some areas because their votes might as well not matter, meaning the president can just ignore them completely.

Plus, everyone should have the right to vote, not just citizens. When so many people are left out just because they're not citizens, it's a far cry from truly reflecting what the people want. It’s similar to how some areas are at a disadvantage when it comes to electors. It makes things unfair, with some voices booming, and others barely audible. Source 3 mentions that voters in "tossup" states are more involved because they know their votes actually count. But it's not right to limit voting to only those "thoughtful voters" when others are dying to join in, too.

Okay, so the Electoral College isn't all bad. It makes voting for electors pretty straightforward. And if there's a tie, the House of Representatives jumps in, with each state getting one vote. The system is already here and functioning, with solutions already built in. However, while popular vote might have some issues, we can totally handle them. Plus, the Electoral College can result in some areas not having enough electors.

Basically, the United States should switch to electing its president by popular vote. This has the potential to reshape our society for the better, making everyone feel like they matter and can be a part of things.

Across the world, lots of people use their own cars to get around. But focusing so much on cars causes some big problems. It's bad for the environment and can make people feel more stressed. So, finding ways to use cars less could really help.

If we didn't rely on cars so much, the air in busy cities would be cleaner. Cars release harmful stuff from burning gasoline, which makes air pollution worse. Research shows that "cars cause 12% of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe and can be up to 50%...in the United States"Source 1. This really shows how much cars affect the environment. Paris, which has a lot of cars, had terrible smog from car emissions. Reuters said, "Paris had 147 micrograms of particulate matter per cubic matter, while Brussels had 114 and London had 79.7"Source 2. When they briefly banned cars, the smog went down, proving that car emissions are bad for the environment.

Also, using cars less can make communities less stressed. In Vauban, Germany, they made it so people didn't need cars, and it became a peaceful place. Heidrun Walter, who lives there, said, "When I had a car I was actually tense. I'm much happier this way"Source 1. This shows how nice it is when there aren't cars around. In Bogota, Columbia, they had a "Day Without Cars" and saw good changes. Andrew Selsky wrote that things improved, like "pitted sidewalks have been replaced by broad, smooth sidewalks" and "rush hour restrictions have dramatically cut traffic"Source 3. Less car use can ease the stress of driving and being stuck in traffic.

Even though cars are useful, they also have problems. Their emissions add to climate change, and traffic jams make people stressed. Cutting back on car use, or getting rid of it altogether, can lead to a healthier planet.

Dear Senator, My worries about the Electoral College must be heard. It doesn't feel right, the way we pick presidents. Even if it's got some good sides, I think the bad parts are bigger. To be honest, I believe it's flawed and must be deleted.

With 538 electors, we could end up tied, even though it hasn't happened. History shows that small vote changes could have changed who won. Richard A. Posner said ties are possible due to 538 being even, but it's rare. It's weird that just a few votes can cause a tie. In 1976, some flipped votes in Ohio and Hawaii could've tied the election. The numbers seem big but are tiny compared to all voters. Even with each state's electors based on size, a tie remains a worry, one we could stop by just counting the popular vote.

Also, electors are people who vote, but they can cause problems. Politics is hard. Back in 1960, Louisiana nearly used the system to choose electors that didn't want John F. Kennedy, all because of personal issues. Posner stated that parties pick electors they trust to vote for their candidate, and that is what usually happens. Still, you can win the electoral vote but lose the people's vote. Electors can, in theory, vote how they want, which isn't fair. Voters might feel like they don't matter. One vote might not change everything, but candidates should try to win over everyone. Often, they don't bother campaigning in states they know they'll win, since one region can't elect a president alone. This can make voters feel like they don't matter and make them not vote. If the person with the most votes wins, people would feel important and want to vote more.

Remember the 2000 election? There was a big fight over the popular vote. It's confusing when votes in one state decide who gets the electors, and that can lead to problems. When a candidate wins more votes, it helps them but hurts those who voted for the other person. Posner talked about problems with runoff elections. Sometimes, a candidate might not get most of the votes, which makes things tricky and leaves the decision to the Electoral College.

Basically, the Electoral College seems unfair because of the problems it can cause. The US needs a better and fairer way to elect presidents, where every vote counts equally. The way we do things now can cause ties, broken promises, and fights over close votes. If we switched to a popular vote system, we'd make sure everyone has a voice. Thank you for thinking about this important issue.

Dear Senator, many of us feel that the Electoral College is vital and should be kept. Okay, it has problems, but it really helps state and national votes. It is a way to vote that shows what people truly want, based on everyone having a say. This makes our votes stronger and more real.

First, normal people pick who is in the Electoral College. So, folks with shared thoughts and values can speak for us. This helps make sure votes show what the people want. Every state's voice counts based on its size. These electors? Often just people who get our needs. When they're picked right, they will fight for their state. Thus, the Electoral College gives a more spot-on way to vote.

Of course, some say it has risks, like electors not doing what they said they would. But this can happen anywhere! Presidents do it too; they don't always keep promises. The Electoral College also takes trust. Just like you trust a president to do what is best, you have to trust electors to do their job. Trust means everything in a vote.

In the end, the Electoral College makes votes clearer than simply counting them. A tie would be terrible! But this system can stop ties, maybe even helping a state or a party. Sure, luck is involved, but that is better than a coin flip. The Electoral College firms up voting.

To wrap it up, we must keep the Electoral College. It is more targeted, needs no more trust than other votes, and makes the entire process more sound.

Cars quickly became the go-to for long trips. More and more people now drive to work every day. People are getting lazy, even skipping bikes or skates for short trips. Instead of walking to the store down the street, lots more people are hopping in their cars. All this driving makes a ton of air pollution, which is bad for our health and warms the planet. Basically, driving less is a really good idea.

If we don't drive as much, like maybe driving less often, we can clean up the air and get more exercise. Vauban, a town near France and Switzerland, went totally car-free, banning street parking, driveways, and garages. Most families there don't even own cars, and many sold them before they moved. One resident, Heindrun Walter, said she loves how peaceful it is without cars, with kids playing and people biking on the streets.

Paris, known for its smog, is also trying to cut down on cars. "Because there were so many days of bad pollution, Paris made rules to stop some people from driving to help clean the air." Because of these rules, "Traffic got way better in Paris after five days of really bad smog..." When fewer people drive, they have to find other ways to get around, like biking or walking. This helps them stay healthy and avoid serious illnesses like cancer, diabetes, and heart problems. Less driving can also fix or stop global warming, saving us from floods and protecting the ozone layer.

"BOGOTA, Colombia—In a growing trend, millions of Colombians walked, biked, skated, or took public transportation to work on a car-free day, so the streets were empty of cars." These fun activities help people get where they need to go while getting exercise. Businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza said that biking with his wife is relaxing and helps the air. Professor Mimi Sheller said this is a long-term change that shows how society is changing. The world is changing, and it's affecting our health and environment.

Driving less can make the air cleaner and people healthier. If we make and drive fewer cars, people can bike, skate, or walk instead. Now is the time to make this change. Many cities have already started, and we all need to join in, step by step.

The Electoral College is still fiercely debated in American politics as the system that picks our president. A lot of people, about 60% according to some studies, want to get rid of it and just use the popular vote. But, the Electoral College definitely has some passionate defenders. Because of how it is set up, someone can win the presidency without winning the most votes from individual people, so perhaps a popular vote is the better choice.

Each state gets a specific number of electoral votes; each begins with two, and population size dictates how many more they receive-- from three to fifty-five, as seen on different sites. A candidate needs 270 of the 538 electoral votes to win. Usually, whoever wins the most votes in a state gets all of its electoral votes, which isn't always fair. Maine and Nebraska, however, are different. Some people don't think their vote matters, so they don't even bother to vote if their party always loses in their state because of the Electoral College. Supporters of the Electoral College say that it ensures a clear winner every time and prevents a candidate from only focusing on one part of the country to win. Plus, it can still produce a result even if the popular vote is super close.

The Electoral College was originally made to protect smaller states, but it doesn't really do that anymore. Presidential campaigns mostly focus on swing states to get their electoral votes. Candidates pretty much ignore states that always vote for one party because they know what's going to happen. So, if the system isn't working like it should, why keep it?

The Electoral College is old and isn't the greatest way to choose presidents. It usually picks a winner, but it can still lead to a tie. Some people claim that focusing on swing states makes voters more informed and helps them make better choices because their votes matter more. However, if we got rid of the Electoral College, candidates would just focus their energy elsewhere. The notion that some votes are worth more than others is an issue, so getting rid of the Electoral College is the best idea.

Dear Senator,

Folks argue a lot about what the Electoral College is even for. Everyone, from normal people to big-shot politicians, feels its effects. This setup picks our president and tries to make sure everyone's vote matters. When we made our country, we wanted things to be fair between the people and the government. Getting rid of the Electoral College messes that up. Keeping the government working well and letting regular folks have a say really depends on it.

The Electoral College helps the whole country in surprising ways. To win, you gotta talk to lots of different people in different places. As Source 3, line 19, says, you have to "appeal to different regions." You can't just be popular in one spot. We need things to be even so one area doesn't control everything. This also makes sure people know what they're voting for and why it matters.

Fairness is what makes our country strong. Keeping the Electoral College shows we care about doing things right. Source 1, line 1, says, "it's a process...the founders made it up to find a middle ground." It was created to find a balance for elections. We can see that balance is super important here.

Also, the Electoral College usually makes sure the winner is someone who deserves it based on all the votes. Source 3, line 22, says that, "it stops elections where no one gets most of the votes." This simple way of picking our President is key. Some folks complain about "electors" messing things up, but that's not really true. How can someone win without enough support?

Basically, the Electoral College is a must for keeping our country balanced. We count on this fairness. Getting rid of it would hurt our country, its future, and its people.

Truthfully, the Electoral College ought to go. It makes everything way too hard compared to just counting everyone's votes. Let's just pick the person with the most votes, plain and simple! This whole Electoral College thing is just old-fashioned. When states start choosing electors who don't care what the people want, that's a huge red flag. Sure, some say it's good, but it causes way more problems than it solves.

Here's the problem: it's super confusing. We don't actually vote for the President; we pick people who pick for us. If one group of electors wins in a state, their candidate gets all the votes from that state. Imagine if the candidate that the winning group in Florida likes gets all 29 of their votes. It gets really messy, especially when it's close. If it's a tie, it goes to the House, where each state gets one vote. All this drama is why the Electoral College is such a pain. If we just counted the votes, we'd be done in days, not months!

The Electoral College messes up voters. Most states give all their votes to whoever wins there, so candidates only care about a few swing states and ignore everyone else. Why bother with all this when we could just count the votes? Getting rid of the Electoral College makes elections easier and calms things down by fighting less.

Honestly, the Electoral College just seems unfair, old, and stupid. The reasons to keep it don't make much sense, and the reasons against just counting the votes are even worse. Keeping the Electoral College is really risky. Switching to a popular vote would make picking the President easier and, I think, make the country a little happier with fewer political freak-outs.

To the State Senate, I'm writing to really push for a big change: ditching the Electoral College and just using the popular vote to pick presidents. After digging into how the Electoral College works and hearing different opinions, I'm sure keeping things as they are is a no-go, and the popular vote is way better.

The Electoral College is tricky. Like Posner said, it's not really direct democracy. Instead, "electors vote for the president, not people... you're actually voting for a slate of electors." This means your vote goes to people who are already on board with a candidate. To make it clearer, the Office of the Federal Register says, "The Electoral College is a process, not a place," meaning it's all about the steps involved.

Plumer brings up a scary thought: "Maybe the scariest thing is if the electoral vote ends in a tie." If that happens, the House of Representatives decides who wins, but here's the catch: "Because each state casts only one vote, the single representative from Wyoming, representing 500,000 voters, would have as much say as the 55 representatives from California, who represent 35 million voters." So, a tiny state has the same power as a huge one, which is clearly unfair. The 2000 election, where Gore got more votes but Bush won because of the Electoral College, is a perfect example. Plumer says "...the electoral college is unfair to voters...swing states..." Candidates only focus on those states, so Plumer concludes, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational," because of possible ties, unfair representation, and built-in problems.

People who like the Electoral College say it brings "...Certainty of Outcome...Everyone's President...Swing States...Big States... Avoid RunOff Elections..." (Posner), but these aren't strong reasons. The 2000 election was anything but certain. "Everyone's President" isn't true because electors vote, not us. Swing states get special treatment while others don't. Posner thinks it helps big states, saying "The Electoral College restores some of the weight in the political balance that large states by population lose by virtue of the malapportionment of the Senate decreed in the Constitution..." However, it doesn't fix the unfairness. Also, while Posner believes the Electoral College "avoids that problem of elections in which no candidate receives a majority of the votes cast," votes aren't equal, thanks to the swing state issue.

Basically, we need to switch from the Electoral College to a popular vote. The current system has obvious problems, as seen in the 2000 election. Swing states having too much power and the risk of ties make things even worse. It's just not fair, makes no sense, and is clearly biased. Even if some disagree, a fair, democratic popular vote is the way to go.

Americans usually own cars, with many believing they must have one to get around. But, there are other ways to travel, and people are starting to want to drive less. It's not always easy, but it's worth it.

Cars cause a lot of pollution around the world, which hurts our health and the earth. Paris tried stopping some cars from driving to fight smog. Believe it or not, Source 2 says that after smog only lasted five days, there was 60% less traffic! Even one day of fewer cars made a difference. Think about how much better things would be if we drove less for weeks or months. We don't have to stop driving completely, but we should be careful about how much we drive.

Driving less can help you feel better and less stressed. It might seem weird, but other ways of getting around can be more relaxing. In Vauban, Germany, they got rid of almost all parking spots, driveways, and garages, so shops were close enough to walk to. Heidrun Walter, a media coach and mom, said in Source 1, "I was always stressed when I had a car. Now, I am much happier." If a busy mom can do it, then many other people can too. Being stuck inside with family all the time can make you tense. Making it easier and cheaper to walk instead of park encourages people to go outside, meet others, and breathe fresh air – which is much better than always being stuck in a car.

It's clear that driving less is good, and I'm all for it. But, for more people to drive less, it needs to be easier to get to places that usually require a car.

Cars have long been the main way people get around. But, because there are so many cars, there are now lots of problems. It would be much better if we didn't use cars as much. This change would help us exercise more, pollute the air less, and save money for both people and cities.

One good thing about using cars less is that it makes us more active. We often don't move around enough because we rely so much on cars. Cars have taken the place of walking, biking, and skating, so now only kids do these things. In places like Vauban, Germany, where not many people own cars, you can hear kids playing and bikes going by all the time. This shows that when people don't depend on cars as much, they move around more. Also, Bogota, Columbia's "Day Without Cars" was very successful and helped create over 118 miles of bike paths in just three years. So, using cars less clearly helps people stay active.

Also, if we used cars less, we would pollute the environment much less. There are so many cars in the world that they cause a lot of pollution. In Europe, cars cause about 12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions, while in the United States, they cause almost 50 percent. This pollution makes the air worse, especially in cities where lots of people live. In fact, cars pollute more than almost anything else, except for maybe power plants. So, using cars less will help the environment by reducing pollution. Really, cars pollute a lot, and cutting back on them would be good for the Earth.

Lastly, using cars less would save money for both people and cities. It costs a lot to maintain cars, buy gas, and keep them running. In April 2013, driving went down by 9 percent, which was as low as it was in 1995. This happened because many Americans couldn't afford to buy new cars. So, owning a car is really expensive for most people. Also, cities spend a lot of money on transportation, mostly on highways (80 percent), while other ways of getting around get very little money (20 percent), which worries lawmakers. Therefore, less driving is useful because cars cost everyone so much.

Envision a world devoid of automobiles. The streets are completely empty, free of traffic jams, quiet, and full of peace and space. A significant number of individuals would regard that as a far more pleasant environment to inhabit. Truthfully, reducing our driving habits would provide substantial assistance to the planet. Fewer of those fuel-inefficient, fast-moving vehicles on the road could have a very beneficial effect on the Earth. It would lead to financial savings for us and improve overall safety.

It's truly fantastic that automotive corporations are dedicating efforts to producing hybrid vehicles and those that minimize harm to the environment. Nevertheless, the single most impactful action we can take is to significantly reduce the number of cars in use. Studies indicate that greenhouse gas emissions can decrease "...up to 50 percent in some car-heavy spots in the United States." Furthermore, there is an increase in pollution, carbon emissions, and unpleasant smog. Shockingly, Paris found it necessary to implement a driving ban temporarily in order to address the smog issue! "...Last week Paris had 147 micrograms of PM per cubic meter compared with 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London...". These figures are becoming increasingly concerning in both Europe and the United States. Just imagine how dire the situation could become in twenty years if this pattern persists. Removing a substantial number of cars could substantially reduce those harmful levels. It would prevent gases from damaging the environment and cleanse the unpleasant air.

Everyone appreciates saving money, wouldn't you agree? Imagine saving thousands annually. By reducing your driving, you could accumulate substantial savings by utilizing public transportation, forgoing car insurance expenses, and refraining from owning a car altogether. Certain individuals believe that not having a car is challenging. After all, they have transportation requirements. However, in Vauban, Germany, automobiles are scarce, and people are managing effectively. Reports state that "Vauban, home to 5,500 residents within a rectangular square mile may be the most advanced experiment in lowcar suburban life". They are able to walk, bicycle, or utilize the tram system. Today, a large number of American cities provide carpool applications, bus services, and van services. Rather than squandering money on gasoline, car payments, and insurance premiums, consider sharing a ride or engaging in physical activity!

Whenever you observe someone driving off, do you ever have concerns about their safety? Statistics reveal that fatalities and injuries resulting from automobile accidents are significantly more prevalent compared to accidents involving alternative modes of transportation. A decrease in the number of cars means less drunk driving incidents, fewer collisions, and a reduction in the number of individuals sustaining injuries or worse. It is all too common to hear stories about teenagers driving under the influence of alcohol and causing devastating accidents. If we depend on buses and trains, we can prevent these incidents and ensure the safety of our youth. Furthermore, traffic congestion can induce stress and anger in individuals, which can result in more accidents as a result of reckless driving behavior. Taking a walk instead of driving can prove beneficial, providing you with fresh air and exercise.

In summary, automobiles offer both advantages and disadvantages. While they facilitate transportation, they contribute to environmental degradation, deplete our finances, and are not consistently secure. By diminishing our driving habits, we contribute to environmental protection by mitigating greenhouse gas emissions, pollution, and carbon emissions. We also accumulate substantial financial savings by bypassing car-related expenditures. A reduced number of automobiles ultimately saves lives by preventing drunk driving incidents and collisions, which translates to fewer fatalities and injuries. Next time you are walking, contemplate what life would be like without cars.

Globally, places want fewer cars. Some make "no-car" areas or limit cars. There are "Car-Free Days!" The big reason? To fight pollution, especially from dirty diesel trucks. Places like Vauban, Germany; Paris, France; and Bogotá, Colombia are trying this. Good results are popping up, so people are hopeful!

In Vauban, Germany, most people don't own cars. No street parking or car stuff in this "new test zone." You can own a car on the edge of town, but parking is scarce. To park, you buy a far-away garage spot. It can be $40,000!

Turns out, people love not having cars there. They like hearing "bikes and kids" more than car noise. Vauban is a great "smart plan." By 2006, most new people there sold their cars!

Vauban is how Germany tries for 20 years to make walkable, bikeable, bus-friendly suburbs. About 5,500 people live in one square mile. It's a top car-free suburb. Germany wants easier car-free travel. Shops are on main streets near homes, not distant highway malls.

Germany wants car-free towns, but some still like big suburbs with garages like in New York. Some still like old ideas, but many copy Vauban.

Paris, France, stopped some cars to fight Beijing-like smog! The city told drivers with even license plates to stay home. Breaking the rule cost 22 euros ($31 USD). The next day, odd-number plates couldn't drive.

Almost 4,000 cars got towed! But traffic fell by nearly 60%! French leaders blamed diesel cars, even with a tax break. 67% of French cars are diesel.

Paris has Europe's worst air from diesel cars. The driving ban hurt some, like delivery trucks. But there were good things! Electric cars, hybrids, and cars with 3+ people drove. Public transit was free, too. The air got better, and the ban ended in days.

Bogotá, Colombia, started "Car-Free Day" and hoped others would copy. Millions walked, skated, biked, or bussed to work. Colombia has this day for one day each year for three years. Driving costs $25. The city of 7 million wants less smog and more people using other ways to travel.

Even with rain, more people came than expected. Two more Colombian cities joined for the first time! Officials from other countries checked out Bogotá. They were excited and said Colombians were changing and thought this would spread!

This idea started in the mid-1990s. Bogotá built 118 miles of bike paths, the most in Latin America. They made parks and sports spaces too. Small sidewalks became wide paths. New places to eat and shop opened because of Car-Free Day.

Studies show Americans buy fewer cars and drive less. This fits Obama's goals. The US buys so many cars, people may not like them as much because of pollution.

One reason may be the recession. Many Americans couldn't buy new cars, and that continues. So, big car companies struggle financially.

Ford and Mercedes change and call themselves "mobility companies." They make different things. Young people (16 to 39) get fewer licenses. Many share rides, walk, or bus. They don't think they need a license.

Many countries sometimes stop driving to fight pollution. They see cleaner air. These countries address big problems that cause bigger problems, like global warming. Things change, and it helps all!

Even with its history, Senator, I stand by the Electoral College as the best way to elect our leaders. It has key strengths. This includes a focus on swing states, a system that ties electoral votes to state population, and a block against winning just by appealing to one area.

The Electoral College makes swing states crucial, forcing people to be more thoughtful. Voters there know their choices matter, so they think harder about who they support. This raises the chances of the best person winning. Without its winner-take-all style, these swing states would matter less under a popular vote.

The Electoral College's way of handing out votes based on state size is also vital. Presidential hopefuls go after bigger states because they have more votes to offer. A popular vote might lead candidates to ignore big states and chase smaller ones. Our system keeps things fairer, preventing smaller states from having too much influence.

Furthermore, the Electoral College stops a candidate from winning solely due to popularity in one region. They must campaign broadly, even in less friendly areas. If we shifted to a popular vote, campaigns would lack clear targets, as the winner-takes-all aspect would disappear. The Electoral College makes it harder for a regionally popular candidate to win, since other areas would no longer be perceived as relevant.

So, summing it all up, I believe the Electoral College is superior to a popular vote for electing our presidents, and those are the specific reasons why.

This Electoral College? It feels old when it's time to pick presidents. Why *still* use it after all this time? America *has* to change; whoever gets the most votes wins, that's all.

Okay, winner-take-all *seems* simple, but is it really fair? According to the Federal Register, most states do this: win the state, get all the electoral votes. Some people say if you work hard in a state, you deserve all the votes, even if you win by just a *little bit*. High risk, high reward, right? That's the argument for keeping it. Also, article one? Each candidate has electors, chosen by the *party*, who actually vote. So, we vote for *them* so they can vote for the candidate? No way. People should choose directly! End the Electoral College right now.

Bradford Plumer's "The Indefensible Electoral College" makes a strong case. We don't *actually* vote for president, but for electors, and *they* choose the president. Every vote *has* to count the same! Giving some votes more power because of who you are? Not fair! Plumer says, "If you lived in Texas... you'd vote for a group of 34 Democratic electors committed to Kerry.” Why would Kerry get 34 votes just because his party picked those people? He should only get votes from people who *chose* him! If they don't like him, they don't get a vote. All votes must be equal: get rid of it.

Plumer also brings up a scary idea: what if there's a tie? Then what happens? The House decides, and each *state* gets just one vote. Seriously? Wyoming, which is *small*, gets the same say as California, which has *millions* of people? That's not equal! With a popular vote, ties are *almost* impossible. Also, Plumer mentions, "Al Gore... won the popular vote but lost the presidency." The person most people wanted didn’t win because of the *Electoral College*! Bush became president. That's messed up and causes way more problems than it solves.

We need a popular vote, now. It's better for everyone and much fairer to candidates. The Electoral College has to go, finally.

Everyone knows cars are super important now – almost like you can't live without them! But while cars make things easier, we often forget all the bad stuff that comes with having so many. This writing will look into what happens because of them, explaining both the problems and how we might fix them.

One big worry is how much cars add to greenhouse gases around the world. In Europe, about 12% of these gases come from cars. A town called Vauban in Germany is really trying to fix this. They want to be a town with "no cars," which means big changes. More places are starting to like this idea, planning smaller, packed-together neighborhoods with good buses and trains. A person who lives there, Heidrun Walter, said, "With a car, I was always stressed. This is a happier life." This shows that even though some people find it hard to change, others find that things get better.

Smog, which is a really bad problem in big cities, is another thing that happens because of cars. Paris was having so much pollution that they had to stop people from driving to make the air better. Diesel fuel, which was cheaper because of the rules in France, made the smog even worse, showing that we need to change the rules. But, new numbers say that less people in America are buying cars, getting licenses, and driving. This could be good for the environment since America makes a lot of pollution. The internet is also helping because young people can talk to each other online, so they don't need to drive as much. Bill Ford, who is in charge at Ford Motor Company, said that "Pedestrians, bicycles, cars, and public transport can all work together in a connected system to save time, use less stuff, make less pollution, and be safer."

A place called Bogota in Colombia is trying to get people to use different ways to get around and make less smog. A businessman there, Carlos Arturo, said, "It's a good chance to get rid of stress and make the air cleaner." The city has made more places to play sports and parks, better sidewalks, less rules during busy times, and good places to shop. Other countries are watching to see how it goes.

These are just some good things that can happen if we use cars less. These first signs show that we can make things even better if more people and towns try to use cars less.

The way we pick our U.S. leader involves folk voting for who they dig. However, sometimes, the candidate with the most votes doesn't actually clinch the win! Instead, we've got this thing called the Electoral College. Basically, winning a state means snagging *all* of its "electoral votes." Then, a bunch of folks cast these pivotal votes. I reckon this system is pretty crummy, and I'm not alone here. It messes with who gets heard, makes people ditch voting altogether, and forces candidates to only cozy up to a few states.

A major buzzkill about the Electoral College? It's simply not a fair shake for everyone. Denser states score more electoral votes. For instance, Wyoming, with its measly 500,000 voters, barely gets any electoral votes. This means each voter's opinion kinda gets sidelined in the quest for those magical 270 electoral votes. It just stinks, plain and simple. And hey, the overall vote winner can *still* lose? Bush v. Gore in '00, anyone?

Some argue handing out electoral votes based on state population is A-OK. But, I believe a national popular vote would be way more balanced. Whoever gets the most votes *nationwide* wins, end of story. In states where one party's super popular—think Texas Republicans—that party steamrolls every time. So, if you're a Texas Democrat, your vote might feel pointless, so you skip voting. That's rotten for those voters and totally against the American spirit.

As states swell in population, their Electoral College clout grows too. Cue candidates zeroing in on those "big states," trying to charm the voters. The smaller vote states? Often ignored. Candidates flit around, speechifying, and some states get totally bypassed. Like, in '00, candidates straight-up snubbed seventeen states! That only drives folks in those states further away from voting.

So, yeah, the Electoral College? It's a dud. It doesn't vibe well with the people, saps voter enthusiasm, and turns candidates into "big state" groupies. A switch to a national popular vote would iron out all these wrinkles and vastly improve presidential elections.

Dear Senator,

I write to you about the Electoral College (EC): what's good and bad. It's how we pick our president and vice president, yet many Americans worry about it. Critics say it's old and unfair, wanting a simple popular vote instead. While not having direct say in who becomes president is annoying, the Electoral College does have some good points. For example, it fixes issues and makes voting smoother, also lowering the votes needed for a clear win. Plus, it tries to represent different parts of the US. But, we must ask if these positives beat the negatives. Is ease a good enough reason to keep the public and their president separate? Some think the Electoral College is a "safety net" to make elections easier. However, many Americans disagree with the EC, seeing it as a threat to the core values of the US. So, the Electoral College needs to change.

The idea of the EC as a voting safety net causes concern. While it sounds good, it often leads to unfair representation. People vote for party electors, who should support their candidate. Richard Posner, in "In Defense of the Electoral College," says the Electoral College winner might not always win the popular vote. He notes that it is not common. Posner is right, as it doesn't happen often. The EC mainly uses a "winner-take-all" approach, where one candidate gets all of a state's electoral votes. This leads to ethical problems. The president should represent most people, but someone will always be unhappy. To truly represent Americans, the winner should get the most votes. A smart voter learns about each candidate and decides who is best. Voting is them saying who would be good as president. If most people pick someone, it makes sense for that person to win. However, the EC gives all state electoral votes to the candidate with the most electors in that state. So, people who would be fairly represented by a popular vote may not get real representation. This gap hurts the country, and even the president.

A big worry about the Electoral College is it cuts off Americans from picking their president. Supporters say "the EC avoids the problems of polling every American." They say polling everyone has risks like too many votes, making counting hard. But, making sure votes are accurate is the president's job and even near the end, they must represent those who voted for them by giving them the power to pick their president. Here, "practicality" is just their excuse to stop Americans from voting. A popular vote might have errors, but the Electoral College has fewer electors so less errors. The EC aims to mirror the population, making sure the candidate is good for many in an area, not just one area. Even though it tries its best, the EC is not exact. Posner says, "the most thoughtful voters should decide the election," and he makes a valid point. An uneducated voter is a danger to any nation, no matter what. But, the US promises every citizen the right to vote. Someone in power can't just say "only the educated should decide," because you can't easily define an educated voter.

The EC's problems are made worse by big states. Larger states have more people, so a candidate chosen by popular vote represents those people, no matter where they live. Smaller states argue they should have an equal say in the Senate and EC. Each state got two senators, no matter the size, because of the Senate. But, the Electoral College doesn't give equal representation to smaller states. A candidate's win in a popular vote depends on the state's population. The EC also depends on the state's population. Since smaller states don't have many electoral votes, they want equal votes in the US Senate. Unfortunately, the EC doesn't do that.

Knowing where this comes from is key to making real change. Accuracy should be a goal if possible, but aimed for even if thought impossible. Keeping the EC as it is lacks accuracy, and offers representation only for ease. A good Electoral College should see itself as helping, not fighting, popular votes. I must state my disappointment with the EC's current state. I know some things can't be changed, but getting the best method is crucial. Senator, you were voted in. We trusted you to defend our needs. We want you to represent us when picking a president. Because of what I've said, we want the Electoral College removed for a better voting system.

Sure, using a car for long trips is helpful, but there are other ways to get around locally, like taking the bus or riding a bike. Using cars less has big benefits. Cars are convenient, yes, but cheaper ways to travel are easy to find.

For example, buses and trains give us both public and private options for moving around. These choices save energy, cut down on pollution, and make travel safer. Cars cause accidents because drivers get tired, lose focus, or can't find parking. Plus, owning a car costs a lot, with gas and repairs being a constant expense. When money is tight, buses or subways are way cheaper. Also, having your car towed makes things much worse. When the air is still, car exhaust stays put, making pollution even worse. Some people say they need a car to get around, but public transit shows that's not true.

Besides, using cars less helps the planet. Roads might seem busy at first, but there's less smog when people drive less. Car-free projects show the good things that happen when we use cars less, like less stress and cleaner air. These things make a big difference. We get more parks, better sidewalks, less traffic during rush hour, and new places to shop and hang out. Even though the US loves cars, recent problems might stop people from going back to driving all the time, especially with these good changes happening.

To sum it up, owning a car is a big deal, and while we need them sometimes, we can live without them at other times. Sure, cars are still important, but we need to think about saving the planet too. Cars are easy, but there are other ways to get where we need to go without spending so much.

That never-ending soundtrack of blaring car alarms, honking horns, and roaring engines? It's become our everyday norm, practically the theme music of our lives. But honestly, all these cars are just too much!

Seriously though, we need to cut back on driving.

Just think of all the good that would come from it!

Cars seem like a necessity, but often, they really aren't. Sure, maybe for those long road trips. But a quick ten-minute walk to the store? Come on!

Seriously, driving less is crucial. Every single week, families are dropping serious cash on gas - we're talking $20 to even $50! Drive less, and you save big, meaning more money for groceries and family fun.

"Having a car stressed me out so much. Life's better without it," one person mentioned. People who ditch their cars? They always seem way happier.

And hey, better air is a fantastic bonus! Healthier living for everyone!

"It cuts down on stress and cleans the air," says Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, as he bikes alongside his wife. People driving less? They're usually pretty darn happy about it.

If more people realized how awesome it is to drive less and gave it a try, the air would be cleaner, and everyone would feel much better.

One former driver explained that her kids "...plan summer jobs and fun around walking, the bus, or getting rides." Plus, they're not rushing to get their licenses, even though cars are still pretty useful where they live. People even prefer walking to driving!

Driving isn't inherently bad, but driving less? That's definitely good for you. You save money just by walking, the air quality improves for everyone, and people can really focus on what matters most: work, loved ones, and family.

Just try it! Park the car and stash those keys somewhere. Walking can be surprisingly fun, a nice little change from always driving.

Listen, Senator, I'm begging you – as a student, let's ditch the Electoral College. We need a national popular vote for president! This way, every single vote actually matters, instead of this weird, roundabout thing we have now.

Seriously, the way things are, we're not even voting for the president directly! These electors, picked by state parties, just mess with our power. We need a bigger voice in choosing who leads us.

Remember that time Louisiana almost screwed up their elector picks in the '60s? That close call proved how easy it is to mess with the Electoral College. It could have been total chaos! Keeping this system is just asking for trouble.

And get this: "winner-take-all" messes with campaigns, too. Candidates only care about those swing states, ignoring tons of people in states they think they've already won or lost.

The Electoral College is old and broken. It ruins direct representation, invites disaster, and rigs elections with its "winner-take-all" nonsense. We deserve a clear system, one that avoids past mistakes, and fixes the ridiculous "winner-take-all" thing.

The electoral college, with its electors acting as intermediaries between voters and presidential candidates, is undeniably flawed. Instead of a straightforward direct election of the president, citizens select individuals to cast the decisive votes, a process that obscures the true meaning of their ballots. Wouldn't a simple aggregation of all individual votes be more transparent? Frankly, the electoral college falls short as a beacon of democratic ideals.

While intended to balance power for smaller states, larger states still dominate the electoral landscape. Certain states command a significant number of electoral votes, while others possess only a few. However, smaller states uniting can collectively wield influence exceeding that of larger states. Furthermore, a candidate can win the popular vote but lose the election due to their supporters being concentrated in specific regions. The electoral college, therefore, can distort the overall count through its system of electors.

Understanding the disparity between the popular vote and electoral votes can be quite perplexing. A candidate can achieve substantial success in California, gaining numerous electoral votes. However, similar success in Montana yields far fewer votes. Despite equal effort invested in both states, the electoral vote system favors candidates who win big in populous states.

Compounding the issue, the electoral college operates on a winner-takes-all basis. Candidates prioritize campaigning in states like California and New York because winning, even by a narrow margin, secures all of the state's electoral votes. Even with a close split, such as 49% to 51%, the losing candidate receives no electoral votes. A fairer system would allocate electoral votes proportionally based on the actual vote distribution.

In conclusion, the electoral college is far from ideal. A simple popular vote would offer clarity, fairness, and ease of understanding, eliminating the need for complex interpretations.

Things aren't looking so great in our country right now, honestly. The environment's in trouble, and folks are really feeling the pinch financially. We need to jump on this, like, yesterday. Here's an idea: drive less! It could actually hit both problems at once. Fewer cars on the road means less yucky stuff in the air, which is great for the planet. Plus, people would save a boatload of cash, which could really help with the money worries. Basically, less driving? Big win.

The environment is getting hammered by all the pollution we're pumping out. Seriously, Robert Duffer was saying that Paris, France, sometimes gets smoggy and polluted, like Beijing, China! So, what did the French government do? They clamped down on driving and slapped fines on people who broke the rules. Bam! The smog cleared up. Of course, then they stopped the rules. But still, there's a lesson here for places choked with pollution: rules like that can really clean things up and keep people healthier.

If you're strapped for cash, cutting back on driving can be a lifesaver, no joke. All that money you save on the car can go to stuff you really need. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote about some towns in Germany where, get this, 70% of people don't even own cars! Why? Because parking's a nightmare and those parking spots can cost you, like, $40,000. Seriously! So, ditching the car is way cheaper. Some folks sold their cars just to move there. Rosenthal's point is clear: not having a car can save you a ton, especially if you're struggling.

Seriously, there are a ton of upsides to driving less. Cleaner air means a healthier environment, which is good for everyone and the whole planet. Plus, you save money, which is awesome if you're broke. Now, some people might whine that it makes getting to work or school or whatever harder. But hey, there are other options! Bikes, buses, trains, those cool monorail things. Think about what happens when we drive too much: tons of pollution and spending a fortune on gas and fixing up the car. Cut back on driving, and we get rid of those headaches.

Esteemed Senator,

Let's get rid of the Electoral College, pronto! Even though the founders put it in the Constitution way back when, it's time for it to go. We need a popular vote, where every single person's vote actually counts. The Electoral College is unfair and, frankly, it mistreats states. It’s gotta go! We all want freedom and equality, but this system just gets in the way.

The USA is supposed to be all about rights for everyone. But how true is that when folks can't directly vote for the president? According to the Office of the Federal Register, you're voting for electors who then vote for the president. What's the point of our vote then? It's bad news when electors don't follow the people’s wishes. Bradford Plumer noted that Al Gore "won the vote but lost the presidency" back in 2000 thanks to this system. After that, a Gallup poll showed over 60 percent of voters wanted a straight-up, direct vote. Also, get this: in 1960, someone tried to switch the electors in Louisiana against John F. Kennedy. That is someone trying to mess with the election! It doesn't happen a lot, but how often will it? Before people start to think the whole thing is rigged? People in the USA should pick their own leaders, period.

Plus, the Electoral College creates inequality in the U.S. because it doesn't treat states equally. The winner-takes-all approach means candidates only bother with a few states – the big ones, the swing states, or states important to them. Bradford Plumer pointed out that "during the 2000 campaign, seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all." It’s just messed up. How can people really choose if candidates aren't even talking to them? Equality means everyone gets a fair vote, plain and simple.

Basically, we need to ditch the Electoral College because it's unfair to both individuals and states. By getting rid of it, you'll help the U.S. stay true to its ideals of freedom and equality.

Sincerely,

United States Citizen.

Our habits change as the world does. We are still producing pollution. As a result, we are increasing our attempts to stop it. Many countries are taking part, not just the U.S. Colombia, France, and Germany are all involved. Certain Germans decided to live car-free and get rid of their vehicles. You know what? They’re happier! A Colombian city has a day each year that is car-free; this is a welcome break from pollution and stress. There is a fee for violating the rules. Could a better life be achieved with less driving, you may be asking?

Vauban, a town in Germany built by 2006 to eliminate vehicles, is worth mentioning. It was successful! Approximately 70% of the households are car-free, and more than half sold their vehicles to relocate there. There are almost no cars and no street parking, garages, or driveways. A tram on the main street goes directly to the city. More Vauban-like developments are appearing in Europe, which speaks volumes. The EPA wants car-lite areas here in the U.S., and some politicians are working on it. This falls under the umbrella of "smart planning," which includes closer cities, ease of walking, and eliminating the need to drive to the store.

Because of the severe pollution in Paris, a law was passed prohibiting driving on particular days to improve the air quality. Odd-numbered license plates were not allowed on one Monday. There's a fine if you drive. The following day, even numbers were excluded. Nearly 4,000 people were fined, and 27 cars were towed; people were furious. But there were 30% fewer cars, despite the commotion!

Bogota, Colombia started a car-free day, and it's now a major event. More people are walking or cycling, and there is less smog. Only buses and taxis are permitted. Millions of people skate, bike, hike, or walk. They are always present, come rain or shine. They claim that it's a wonderful way to unwind and breathe clean air. Valledupar and Cali, two other Colombian cities, are implementing similar policies. The mayor of Asuncion, Paraguay even came to observe. The city has constructed bike paths, new sidewalks, sports facilities, and parks to improve pedestrian accessibility.

Do you understand why so many countries are trying hard to save the environment? Car-free days and car-free zones both contribute significantly. Perhaps I've encouraged you to consider living more while driving less.

Mr. State Senator, the Electoral College worries me a lot. It feels unfair, it's ancient, and it just doesn't add up. I want it gone, as voters lose out, we could have a tie, and—get this—we vote for electors, not the president!

The Electoral College hurts voters. Candidates only focus on swing states, since most states give ALL their votes to the winner there, and they ignore states they can't win, like they are invisible! In 2000, they ignored seventeen states! Big media markets saw no ads either. Voters in these states lack info. Candidates keep secrets. Without ads or visits, voters can't know the person they are voting for, which makes the Electoral College unfair.

An Electoral College tie? A nightmare. If that happens, the House decides, with one vote per state. Imagine that? The result may not match what people in those states want. This rests on a few voters and is awful. A tie would hurt the election.

Also, it hides the real link between us and the president. We don't vote for the president; we vote for electors. You're in Texas and like John Kerry? You vote for 34 Democratic electors who promised to vote for him. And, anyone can be an elector, so even if they aren't political, they might be biased. The Electoral College is therefore, unfair.

Mr. Senator, the Electoral College is bad. Voters lose out, we might tie, and the election is not direct. It is unfair to voters, there could be a tie, and voters vote for an elector, not the president. I think it is unjust, old, and makes no sense.

So, you're thinking about ditching the car? Excellent! It's beneficial for the earth, your bank account, and keeps you out of that terrible traffic.

One of the main upsides? You're assisting the planet. Cars, as Elizabeth Rosenthal points out, contribute greatly to greenhouse gases. While Europe accounts for 12%, car-loving areas such as the United States may reach as high as 50%! That nasty exhaust, especially in urban areas, leads to the creation of smog. Robert Duffer mentions Paris' attempts to eliminate automobiles in order to combat smog. Some planners envision suburbs with a decreased dependency on vehicles. Less cars equals purer air, simply put.

Also, having fewer automobiles equates to less traffic congestion! Bogota, Colombia had a "no car day" in order to have clean air and open roadways. Reduced traffic equated to reduced stress, as well as increased activity. No more work delays! People went by foot, by bicycle, or by bus. As a bonus, Bogota has added green areas and sports facilities. There are benefits to both health and the environment when fewer vehicles are present.

Finally, there's money to be saved! You'll spend less on gas and have lower car-related expenses. You'll also pay less in taxes for roads, parking, and lights. Less material will be needed for roads and parking. Because many city residents already use the subway or bus to save money, reducing the number of cars is an easy approach to lowering pollution.

To sum it up, cutting back on driving is a huge win. It improves the environment, reduces traffic, motivates you to exercise, and saves you money. Although it may be more difficult in spread-out areas, it is a treasure in cities!

Cars? They’re everywhere, truly! All across the U.S. and the globe. People depend heavily on them for commuting to jobs, seeing family, and general transport. But check this out: a novel phenomenon is emerging across Europe, the U.S., and beyond: car-free living! Take Vauban, a German town, which largely rejected cars. Heidrun Walter, a local media trainer and mother, stated, "I felt stressed with a car, now I am far happier.” This shows car-free living isn't just feasible, it's uplifting!

Alright, while challenging, it offers fantastic benefits. Cars create 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases, possibly even half in the U.S., states a report. Reduced driving lowers pollution, a significant issue in many areas. Recall Paris? They forbade cars because of smog, allowing them only on certain days based on license plates, slashing traffic by 60% and purifying the air! These quick successes beg the question: what if cities were designed car-free, permanently? The potential payoff is massive!

Car-free living isn’t always forever. Have you heard of Bogota, Colombia? They host a hugely successful annual car-free day, involving millions, another report says. For three years, they banned cars (excluding buses and taxis) to encourage alternative transport and combat smog in the city of seven million! Violators faced fines. Additionally, Cali and Valledupar joined for the first time! Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, noted, "It's a lovely time to unwind and cleanse the air." This proves that even partial car-free periods yield significant results!

Surprisingly, the U.S. may be gradually reducing car use, according to another report. Studies suggest Americans are purchasing fewer cars, driving less, and acquiring fewer licenses annually. Doug Short of Advisor Perspectives (an investment research firm) found peak miles driven occurred in 2005 and has since declined. By April 2013, individual driving had decreased by almost 9% from the peak. Americans are becoming less reliant on cars! The article claims this could drastically lower carbon emissions and benefit the environment. Experts anticipate this trend to continue, with increased availability of alternative transportation. New York City offers bike-sharing; San Francisco has BART. Numerous options abound!

Ultimately: alternative transit and car-free lifestyles are fantastic! With ample ways to minimize driving, greener travel is more accessible than imagined. Myriad choices exist, and studies highlight that the advantages outweigh the disadvantages.

Dear Senator Adams,

Picking our president? It's more tangled than folks think! The Electoral College means we vote for electors, not directly for the president. Parties select these electors, and they might not always do what we want. You can win the most votes but still lose because you lack electoral votes. Voters are confused; they don't get how it runs. It feels outdated and needs a big change.

To start, these electors aren't always reliable, which is worrying. Parties pick them, and states do their own thing when choosing them. Voters don't get much say in electors' votes. We should just count the popular vote since "electors could ignore what people want." This makes it unreliable; electors have too much power.

Also, you can win the popular vote but lose the election if you don't get enough electoral votes. Voters get bummed out, knowing electors decide everything. "Like Gore beating Bush in popular votes, but not electoral votes?" That happens! Each election, we might see another close race where the most votes lose. That's a big problem.

And get this: lots of folks are confused about how the Electoral College works. An article said, "voters mix up electors and mess up their vote." The system doesn't clarify who we're voting for. A writer shared how people vote for a group of electors, and had to reassure them the votes were right! Voters vote and might be shocked.

So, let's ditch the Electoral College and use the popular vote. Electors, because they love their party, aren't always trusted. The popular vote winner can lose if they lack electoral votes. And people are plain confused. It's a mess, and it's time for a change.

Dear Senator,

The Electoral College? It's still causing lots of fights. Ditching it seems good for us Americans. Right now, we don't vote for the president directly. Instead, we pick people who then vote for the president. Good luck changing their minds! They might even vote how they want! Imagine one vote changing everything. People want this fixed. After the mess in 2000, where Gore won the popular vote but didn't win the election, a poll showed most people wanted to vote directly.

Okay, the Bush v. Kerry thing doesn't happen often, but it *has* happened. Do we need another one to see the problem? We like democracy, don't we? The people rule? But do we *really* rule? It looks like it, but the Electoral College – just 538 folks – really calls the shots. This is broken. It can be rigged to really help one side. Back in 1960, some people almost got rid of the Democratic electors in Louisiana to stop Kennedy. Scary!

Also, electoral votes aren't fair. It's like the House, where bigger states get more electors because they have more people. California has 55 votes, which is way more than South Dakota or Alaska. The Senate makes things fair in Congress, but we don't have that here. Big states win! Plus, what if there's a tie? The House decides, with each state getting *one* vote. Crazy!

Yep, the Electoral College is old, unfair, and doesn't work. We can just vote for our president ourselves, without the electors. It's not democratic, and it should go away.

Thanks.

Okay, an old system, right? The Electoral College isn't a building. Picture it as a deal made a long, long time ago. The people who started the country wrote it into the rules. They didn't want just the big bosses in Congress to pick the president. However, they were also nervous about letting *everyone* vote directly. So, they created electors to cast votes. Later, Congress counts those votes. Super old! Is it fair? Does your vote even count? Some say these electors think carefully. But most folks feel like their say is limited. Maybe it is time to change things up now?

So, voting? It doesn’t *really* pick the president. You pick *electors*! Then, these electors pick the president. That's who picks the winner, even if the popular vote goes the other way! Call it a swing vote. You might be the top choice, but lose. Because you lost the electors' votes. It has happened! Remember 2000? Gore won the people's vote, then lost the election because of this Electoral College thing. Some people say it's tradition, so leave it alone! But let's consider what else we could do.

The Electoral College is rooted in history, never forget that. But it's not perfect! Sometimes, it ignores the people's choice. So, a candidate wins the vote, but loses the election. A new way could be better. One that includes everyone, not just those electors.

Because it's so old, the Electoral College has issues. It's time to fix it instead of pretending it’s not there. Your vote doesn't even matter. Fix this Electoral College. Otherwise, what's the point of voting in the first place?

Dear Senator, this whole way we pick our President has got me thinking. The Electoral College? I'm just not so sure about it. I see why the founders put it in the Constitution way back when the country was brand new. But now that we all know "The Electoral College consists of 538 electors," is this the best we can do these days? Wouldn't it be easier for everyone to just vote for who they want to be President? According to The Office of the Federal Register, "The Electoral College consists of the selection of the electors," and with "a majority of 270 electoral votes is required to elect the President," does this feel like the people are really being heard? With all the smart people around, shouldn't we just let them choose?

It's a hard life, no doubt, but you, Senator, could help make this better. The "electors are generally chosen by the candidate's political party," so shouldn't we give regular people more of a voice? Since "the presidential election is held every four years," Americans have to wait a long time to choose their leader. Why not let them vote directly, like they want to?
