Right now, "most states have a winner-takes-all system that awards all electors to the winning presidential candidate. However, Maine and Nebraska each have a variation of proportional representation." We could skip this mess and just do what the people want: a direct vote for President. Isn't that what America is all about, Senator?

Actually, "according to a gallop poll in 2000, taken shortly after Al Gorethanks to the quirks of the electoral collegewon the popular vote but lost the presidency, over 60% of voters would prefer a direct election to the kind we have now." And like Bradford Plumer said not long ago, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational. The best arguments in favor of it are mostly assertions without much basis in reality. And the arguments against direct elections are spurious at best."

Living here in sunny Florida, I've got a good reason to like the Electoral College. It just feels right, you know? It boosts the odds that the president is someone most folks actually want. Why? It wraps up elections nice and neat, helps us rally behind a single leader, and makes those swing states super important.

Sure, the Electoral College isn't perfect, but dumping it for a straight-up popular vote could backfire big time. How's that? The Electoral College usually delivers a bigger win than you'd see in the popular vote. Plus, it throws a bone to the smaller states. Even though we have 538 electors, an Electoral College tie? Highly unlikely. And the winner? They've GOT to play fair with everyone. No picking favorites – religion, region, nothing. Otherwise, people feel left out, like their votes don't matter, and the president doesn't give a hoot.

Candidates spend most of their time hanging out in those swing states. Why? Word on the street is they've got the "smartest" voters, so they're the ones who should be calling the shots.

Folks whine that the Electoral College could hand too much power to the government. But guess what? Without it, we're even MORE likely to see that happen! The Electoral College actually flips the power back to us, the voters, letting us pick who we want as president.

Bottom line? Sticking with the Electoral College lets everyday folks carefully choose who's going to lead us for the next four years.

The Electoral College, this really old way we pick presidents, matters a lot. We should keep it because it spreads out power pretty evenly across all the states and usually gives us a clear winner. Plus, it keeps our elections on a schedule we can count on. Over time, the College has picked some good leaders who understand what we want. We, the people, get to pick our leader, and the Electoral College makes sure that process goes smoothly.

Think about it this way: states with lots of people, like Florida, get more votes (like 29!), while states with fewer people, like Wyoming, get fewer (only 3). It's kind of like how each state gets senators and representatives. Every state gets at least three votes because of its population. So, it actually makes a lot of sense. This setup keeps the big states from bossing everyone around. If Wyoming's population suddenly exploded, they might get more votes, and Florida's might even go down! Florida has so many different kinds of people, so it makes sense they get more votes. The Electoral College also makes people think twice before just voting for someone they know. Without it, a candidate from California could win just because California has so many voters.

Okay, so ties are possible, but they almost never happen because most states have this "winner-take-all" thing going on. That means swing states are super important! Candidates know they shouldn't waste time campaigning in states that always vote for the other party. Instead, they focus on states where people haven't made up their minds yet. They also count on the states that usually support them. It's like going to the store: you buy what you need, not what you already have or can't use. So, let's not complain about where candidates choose to campaign, alright?

Our presidential inaugurations always happen on January 20th or March 4th, and let's keep it that way! Sticking to this schedule is super important, and it would be way harder without the Electoral College. It helps our elections go smoothly and on time, with voting always starting on the second Thursday of November. If we got rid of the Electoral College and just counted all the votes across the country, it could mess everything up. Imagine how long it would take to recount all the votes in a huge state like Texas – it could take months! Then we'd be without a leader and that could cause some major problems.

The Electoral College has been a big part of American democracy since the beginning, and things could get tricky without it. It's good to keep traditions, and the Electoral College is definitely one of those. It spreads out the power, makes sure we have a clear winner, and keeps our election schedule on track. It brings order, and we all benefit from the structure the Electoral College gives us.

Driving less? Fewer problems, period. Less accidents and stress. Though I am nearing the legal age to acquire a learner's permit, I can still wait. It is not something I consider essential right now. I am not against cars. The mall and restaurants are nearby, so driving is unnecessary. If everyone drove less, there would be fewer accidents, and people would save money on car insurance and gas!

Following a day of subpar air quality, Paris took measures to encourage alternatives that are better for the environment. Can you picture a world where every country had more car-free days? This is a golden opportunity to improve the air quality and reduce traffic incidents. Cities have been built solely for cars since World War II. Our urban planning needs to be rethought. We should refocus our efforts on something that helps us live better lives. While cars offer a simple way to get around, they also cause air pollution. We need clean air! It might be beneficial to drive less. “In previous bills, 80 percent of appropriations have by law gone to highways and only 20 percent to other transport".

Car expenses can be upsetting and a massive waste of money. Instead, you could spend that money on your kids' college education or an unforgettable vacation! Going broke over a car? Not worth it. Why be stressed when you could live your best life? Cars cause a great deal of financial stress and often lead to more problems than they solve. Senator, people are really upset about the Electoral College and are wondering if it should be changed. Some people like it the way it is, but many others do not. So, what is the plan? I think it needs some work. As it is now, the system seems to care more about the number of votes than the actual voters. Also, it is strange that we do not directly vote for the president, and those electors could even go against what the people want. And get this: one state, or even one elector, could change the whole election. This makes our democracy seem very unstable.

Even though some people defend it, the Electoral College has many problems. Candidates tend to focus on states with a lot of electoral votes, possibly ignoring people in states with fewer people. For example, a candidate might spend a lot of time in California, which has 55 electoral votes, but ignore Montana, which only has 3. This makes it seem like it is more about getting wins than really talking to everyone. It is as if the election is just about getting a few big states to like you instead of representing all of us.

Like "The Indefensible Electoral College" says, we are really voting for electors who then vote for the president. Maybe we should call it the "Electors Election" instead of the "Presidential Election"! Even though electors are supposed to vote for their candidates, they do not always do what they are told. If an elector changes their mind, it could mess up the whole election and cause problems for the whole country. That is not how voting should work – it should be about what the people want, not what the electors want. Some people say that the Electoral College has been around for a long time and works fine, so why change it? But we should always be trying to get better as a country, and that means looking at our systems and fixing them. What if we never questioned segregation or the British? We would not be who we are today if we did not question things. The Electoral College needs to be looked at closely, so we can make sure it shows who the people really want as president.

Basically, the Electoral College cares more about electoral power than what the people want. Some states have more power than others, and electors have too much power. If we want a government that really represents us and makes everyone feel important, we need to fix the Electoral College. We need to make sure it shows what the people really want, so we can move forward as a country.

So, what's the deal with the Electoral College, really? Have you ever wondered where your vote goes? Voting does not directly elect the President, but rather picks electors who are supposed to vote for the President. But can we really trust them to do what's best for everyone?

I feel that the Electoral College is not fair. Some voters have more influence than others. People may trust the electors because they're seen as smart. They can analyze things, it's understandable. But can we truly trust them to make such a big decision for voters who can think for themselves? This whole system feels wrong. The winner-take-all rule means candidates ignore states they're sure to lose and target "swing" states. Why do we continue using a system that so many dislike? It makes people feel like their vote means nothing. The Electoral College might think they know best, and that's understandable, but shouldn't we be able to choose the President ourselves? "...candidates don't spend time in states they know they have no chance of winning..." Why is this okay? We should get to directly pick our leader.

And consider this. How do we even know who to vote for? We can and should think carefully when voting for electors, and we must vote. It's vital to vote. However, we don't always have all the information. "The American people should consider themselves lucky that the two thousand fiasco was the biggest election crisis in a century the system allows for much worse. Consider the state legislatures are technically responsible for picking electors , and that those electors could always defy the will of the people." Voters can make mistakes, but not always! We should vote wisely, but the Electoral College shouldn't be able to overrule us. There are different views and different electoral parties, but think about the 2000 election issue. "Consider the state legislatures are technically responsible for picking electors, and that those electors could always defy the will of the people." We must vote for our President, but also be mindful of who we're voting for, to avoid problems.

So, I hope you are pondering this. It's hard to simply trust others with something this crucial. We need to trust ourselves and make well-informed choices.

Imagine this: Friday, 5 PM hits, work's done! No crazy traffic or blaring horns—just a chill ride home. Sounds great, right? But for many Europeans, this is just a regular Tuesday. It shows how awesome things can be when people don't rely so much on cars.

Big plus: money saved! Owning a car can be a huge drain. Accidents and speeding tickets pop up, and suddenly, you're broke faster than you can say "insurance." Plus, ditching the car means no more gas bills. Sure, gas prices might be okay now, but they always go up. If gas gets too pricey, you might skip fixing your car, making things worse later. A bus ride might be cheap, but driving could cost you six bucks just for gas! Or, how about biking? Way cheaper to keep up a bike than a whole car.

Then there's pollution. Cars make a ton of noise, light, and air pollution. Whether you notice it or not, it messes with nature. The U.S. might not be the worst polluter right now, but more cars could change that. Maybe totally ditching cars is impossible, but what about limits? Like, only driving on certain days based on your license plate, or carpooling with coworkers? Even small changes help.

Here's the thing: People in car-free cities are happier! Less stress from no traffic jams or fender-benders. Seriously, driving can be a pain, especially with all the distractions. Those distractions can cause accidents or just make you super anxious. Heidrun Walter, a mom of two, nailed it: "Having a car always stressed me out. Now I'm way happier!" She loved the busy streets, where bike sounds and kids laughing drowned out cars. Imagine having everything you need close by, so you can just chill and walk or bike with your kids. Nice, huh?

So, how would less car use change your life? Could it make you healthier and happier? Worth thinking about, right? What if cutting back on cars made things way better?

Folks hop into their vehicles each day for work, chores, or trips, often forgetting about the greenhouse gases affecting our planet. This pollution creates a significant issue, especially in urban areas. Consequently, countries and organizations have launched initiatives to encourage reduced driving. These actions have successfully lowered harmful emissions and improved overall conditions!

The greatest benefit of less driving is its impact on decreasing pollution and those nasty greenhouse gases. Anti-driving communities allow individuals to save money on gas and contribute to a healthier planet. Elisabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," highlights the large contribution of cars to greenhouse gases. Europe sees a 12% contribution, while the US reaches 50%. These statistics display just how much cars impact gas emissions. Imagine if numerous cities reduced car usage. A comparison of greenhouse gas levels in car-free areas versus car-heavy cities would reveal a remarkable difference, with significantly lower emissions in the car-free zones.

Additionally, fewer cars can greatly benefit urban landscapes. Businesses might relocate to more accessible locations if people drive less frequently, attracting more patrons. Afterwards, related businesses might decide to move closer. The article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" illustrates this, stating, "Parks and sports centers have bloomed, uneven sidewalks have been replaced by broad, smooth sidewalks, rush hour restrictions have dramatically cut traffic and new restaurants and upscale shopping districts have cropped up." Lower car use allows positive transformations in cities.

Ultimately, there are numerous incentives to reduce driving! From purifying the air to enhancing cities, minimizing driving can create a real impact.

In today's super speedy world, cars and getting around are a big deal. Many families own lots of cars for getting around, almost like they can't live without them! While cars are helpful, they also cause problems. Less car use would make Earth safer and greener, and help save important stuff.

Most drivers are careful, but some cause crashes by driving crazy. People often break the rules, leading to terrible outcomes. Careless driving is a major killer, especially for young folks, which makes people worry about driving. Car crashes sadly kill loads of people each year. Without cars, we could stop these awful tragedies. We could walk, bike, bus, or subway instead, which would mean fewer accidents and happier lives.

Less car use means less dirty air! Cars really mess up the air we breathe. One study shows that transport causes half of all air pollution in the US. Reducing car use would greatly clean up the air. Also, "cars carrying people cause 12% of Europe's air pollution... sometimes as high as 50% in car-heavy US areas." Ditching cars would cut tons of pollution, improving our future.

No cars means saving precious materials. Cars burn gas and diesel, which we're quickly using up. Other things also need these resources. Cutting back on cars would lower gas use, saving it for key needs. This helps the Earth recover and ensures resources for future generations.

In conclusion, using fewer cars would greatly improve the world. It secures the future and eases worries. We can't undo the past, but reducing car use prevents future harm and keeps the future safe. It promises a cleaner world, safer lives, and a chance to treat the Earth kindly.

Cars have totally changed the world, with both good and bad results. They've made getting around easier, but also caused lots of crashes. Whether the good outweighs the bad is always changing. For 100 years, cars helped us. But now, we really see how bad they are. Stopping or cutting down on car use could mean less pollution, saving resources, and way fewer accidents.

Countries all over are trying out different ways to cut pollution, from banning cars completely to just limiting when you can use them. For example, in Vauban, Germany, owning a car is super expensive. So, most families (70%) don't have cars, and over half of the people moving there sold their cars first. More and more places are thinking about doing things like this. Vauban, with about 5,500 people, shows that it can work. People say they're happier without cars. In Paris, France, they had a system where cars with certain license plates couldn't drive on certain days to fight smog. It cut smog by 60% in just five days! Bogota, Colombia, also wants to protect the environment like Germany. Carlos Arturo Plaza says this is "a great chance to lower stress and air pollution." In Europe, cars cause 12% of the gases that warm the planet and air pollution. Also, driving less in the US helps cut pollution and take care of the environment. Around the world, countries are working hard to protect the Earth by cutting pollution.

For the last 100 years, cars have used up tons of resources that can't be replaced, which hurts the environment. The Earth's supply of these resources is running out fast, and it takes forever to get them back. Using these resources is bad because it puts carbon into the air, which causes the greenhouse effect. Saving resources is super important for keeping the air clean.

Now, more and more people are calling for car bans or limits, mostly because so many people die in car accidents. Car crashes are a big reason people die in the US, and probably everywhere else. If banning cars could save thousands of lives, we should really think about it. Fewer cars could mean fewer crashes, injuries, and deaths. Plus, governments could use the money they save on fixing roads to make things safer for people walking and build better playgrounds, which would make communities better.

The world is changing fast, so we need to change too to protect the planet and the people on it. Banning cars could be one of many steps towards a future that works for everyone. It could lower the gases that warm the planet, save important resources, and lower the number of people who die in car crashes. Maybe, in the next few decades, cars will be a thing of the past.

The way we pick the president now isn't just about counting up who got the most votes. Switching to a system where every single vote counts directly would be much fairer. Honestly, the system we have feels super old and out of touch with today's world.

The Electoral College? Not exactly fair. Like source two points out, the "winner-take-all" thing pushes candidates to spend all their time and money on just a few swing states, which is a total bummer for everyone else. Basically, some states get all the love, while others are completely ignored.

Plus, the Electoral College is just ancient. Source two even brings up the 1960 election in Hawaii to show how long it's been around. Seriously, we need a voting system that actually makes sense for how we live now.

And let's be real, the whole reason for the Electoral College is kinda weak. We don't even directly vote for the president! We vote for these "electors" who then cast the actual votes, according to source two. This whole roundabout thing should just be scrapped in favor of a simple, direct vote for who we want in charge.

Bottom line? We should totally ditch the Electoral College. It's unfair, outdated, and doesn't really make sense, which is bad for democracy. Getting rid of it would make voting more meaningful and get more people involved.

Giving up cars might seem weird, but lots of folks are doing it. Why, you ask? They want to feel healthier and happier. Heidrun Walter, from Elisabeth Rosenthal's piece about a town in Germany, said, "Driving stressed me out. Now, I feel way better." She, like many others in Vauban, Germany, finds life is more chill without a car. The town doesn't allow driveways or garages, pushing people to ditch their rides. Instead, they hop on buses, bikes, or just walk. Instead of car stress, Vauban folks see owning a car as the stressful thing. More than half gave up their cars to move there and don't even miss them. Andrew Selsky, in his article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," quotes Carlos Arturo Plaza, who was biking with his wife and said, "It's a great way to relax."

Plaza also mentioned it's "a good way to make the air cleaner." People are finally realizing that cars make a lot of pollution. Paris, known for its smog, has really tried to clean up its air. Robert Duffer wrote in "After days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city" that cars with even-numbered license plates couldn't drive on some days, and odd-numbered plates on other days. This really made a difference. Duffer said, "Traffic dropped 60 percent in Paris, after the smog had been building for five days." Elizabeth Rosenthal, in "The End of Car Culture," thinks, "if this keeps up...it'll help the Earth, since cars are a big source of pollution in America." Americans use a lot of stuff, which leads to a lot of pollution. We want all the newest gadgets, but it costs more than just money. Greenhouse gases are piling up, and we have to do something. Rosenthal points out that "President Obama set some big goals to cut America's greenhouse gas emissions last week." In areas without cars, the good stuff goes beyond just cleaner air. Andrew Selsky wrote about "the construction of 118 miles of bicycle paths" and how "rough sidewalks have been replaced by smooth ones" in Bogota, Colombia. This makes people want to walk more. The author says that "officials from other countries came to Bogota to see carfree day and were pumped," including the mayor of Paraguay's capital. Seeing the good stuff makes people want to copy it. Columbia's one-day event has started talks in nearby towns, with "two other Colombian cities, Calli and Valledupar, joined in." But, car companies are changing. Rosenthal says, "But it could hurt the car business. So, companies like Ford and Mercedes are calling themselves "mobility" companies that do more than just make cars." As people start walking, biking, or taking the bus, car companies are trying new things to keep customers, even if they don't have cars. She also mentions Bill Ford's hope for car dealers to "work with phone companies to build cities where 'walking, bikes, cars, and buses all work together to save time, use less stuff, pollute less, and be safer.'" This means mixing different ways to get around with new tech to help everyone.

No matter what, things are changing. Cities are trying out new ways to get around or making the old ways better. Henry Ford might be sad that cars aren't as popular, but Mother Nature would be happy about our efforts to save the planet. We don't know what the future holds, but we know we only have one Earth, so we need to take care of it.

Many are eager to be rid of the Electoral College, bringing back memories of the 2000 election mess. To a lot of people, it seems wrong and useless and ought to be removed.

But wait, the Electoral College does have some good qualities. Richard A. Posner, in his article, writes that it "stops elections where no one gets over half the votes." Basically, it makes sure there is a clear winner, avoiding messy runoffs that might bring about chaos. Posner also says that the victor usually gets a larger share of Electoral College votes than the popular vote, which lowers the risk of bitter disagreements about the result when you compare it to just counting individual votes. Despite these arguments, the main idea is still this: the people should choose their president. So, the Electoral College is widely considered faulty and confusing, so it needs to be gotten rid of.

A big worry is that the popular vote winner might lose, possibly depriving America of the leader it really wants. Bradford Plumer notes that the Electoral College means "the popular vote winner could lose again." Taking away people's power to elect their leader goes against the idea of freedom. Plumer also says that voters can't always "control who their electors vote for," which removes people's control over electoral votes. This lessens the importance of popular opinion. So, we should get rid of the Electoral College right away.

Another problem is the huge power held by electors. Plumer points out that "electors could always go against what the people want," showing how much they affect the presidential result. That power should belong to the public. He writes that electors "have sometimes voted against their party and picked whoever they wanted," showing that they can just change their minds. The popular vote should decide everything. What happens to America should depend on what its people want, and they should be able to choose their President.

In the end, the Electoral College is not fair to Americans. We should have the right to elect our leader. The popular vote should be the one that decides, and the Electoral College should be done away with for good.

Each day, many of us hop in our cars to get to work or school. But guess what? Things are shifting! Driving less is a smart move. It's good for the Earth and can make us feel more relaxed.

Less driving means fewer car fumes. This is awesome for the planet! Car exhaust creates smog, which traps yucky stuff in the air. Cities can become super smoggy. For example, Paris once had 147 micrograms of junk in the air. After they stopped some cars, the smog vanished FAST – just one day, and they didn't need to do it again! Cars create a TON of greenhouse gases: 12% in Europe and a whopping 50% in the US. That's how much cars harm the environment. Even one car-free day each week can seriously cut down on those gases. Americans are changing their habits too. Fewer people are buying cars and driving around. Since '95, the number of miles each person drives has dropped by almost 9%. If we keep ditching cars, we could dramatically reduce gas emissions and improve the environment.

Also, skipping the daily drive to work can reduce stress. In Vauban, Germany, most folks don't even own cars – only 30% do. Even though some think you NEED a minivan for kids, Heidrun Walter prefers being car-free. She says, "When I had a car, I was always stressed. I'm much happier now." For three years, Bogota, Colombia, has had a "Day Without Cars." Only taxis and buses can drive then. Even in the rain and dark, people like the car-free day. Businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza said, "It's a great way to chill and clean up the air." Now two other Colombian cities want to do it too. And guess what? Tech lets us hang out with friends, work from home, and skip the commute. That's a HUGE stress buster!

We used to think cars were the best way to get around. We didn't consider how they hurt the planet or our own happiness. Now, we need to rethink things and see the bigger picture.

Ugh, the Electoral College. Seriously? It's a terrible way to choose the president. It cheats voters because state electors sometimes don’t listen to what people actually want. We don’t want another Al Gore situation. Winning the most votes but still losing? That's awful. This old system? It's totally outdated.

When you vote, you’re really voting for "electors." They’re supposed to vote for the winner, but they can do whatever they want. Someone could win the popular vote and still lose the election. How? They don’t get enough electoral votes from each state. That’s not fair. The person everyone wants should just win, period. Back in the day, electors might have had to be smart to pick for the dummies. But now? Everyone can pick a good leader. Al Gore's 2000 loss, even though he won the popular vote, shows the system is bad.

What’s bad about the Electoral College? It’s all or nothing. You win a state, even by a tiny bit, and you get ALL the electoral votes. So, candidates ignore small states or states they know they’ll win and focus on big, swing states. Like Ohio. It’s known for changing its mind. It might seem smart, but in 2000, 17 states got no attention. Voters there were ignored and didn't know what was happening. In close races, almost half the voters in some states don’t matter. In California, half of the 35 million voters don’t matter either! Elections shouldn't depend on Ohio while everyone else is ignored.

We need a better way. A national popular vote is the only way to make sure the most popular person wins. Candidates would have to listen to everyone, including rural voters! American citizens should have a fair election that makes most people happy.

The Electoral College is the current system in the U.S. for electing presidents. The Founding Fathers created it. Voters choose electors, who then vote for president and VP. Congress counts these votes. Supporters believe it ensures a clear victory. However, others find it problematic. They claim it overly favors large states, may lead to odd results, and find the winner-take-all approach unfair.

Each state has a set number of electors based on its Congressional representatives, meaning big states get more. For instance, Hawaii has only four electors, while Texas has thirty-eight! Consequently, candidates concentrate their efforts on the most populous areas. As Bradford Plumer pointed out, Wyoming, "each state casts only one vote, the single representative from Wyoming, representing 500,000 voters, would have as much say as the 55 representatives from California, who represent 35 million voters. Given that many voters vote one party for president and another for congress, the House's selection can hardly be expected to represent the will of the people".

The current presidential election process is susceptible to complications. In 2000, Gore received more popular votes, yet he lost. Additionally, can electors be fully trusted to uphold their commitments? If an elector changes their vote, it could alter the election's outcome. There have been other oddities, like Hawaii's dual lists of electors in 1960.

A major issue with the Electoral College is its winner-take-all nature. Winning the popular vote in a state grants all of its electors. As a result, candidates neglect unwinnable states and focus on "swing states." Richard A. Posner noted that "During the 2000 campaign, seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't get to see a single ad."

In summary, the Electoral College has flaws. It generates numerous issues and lacks fairness for voters. Even figures like Nixon and Carter supported its abolishment. Between excessive focus on specific states, unreliable electors, and the unjust winner-take-all system, the Electoral College causes more harm than good.

Wow, I feel so free all of a sudden! And you know what? It's because I got rid of my car! I know, I know, cars are everywhere, and everyone seems to want one. Giving it up might sound terrible. But hey, maybe it's not so bad after all. Maybe things are changing, and it's not as awful as we think.

Elisabeth Rosenthal thinks we might be driving less in the U.S. soon. Numbers show that fewer people are buying cars, driving less, and getting driver's licenses. Since the U.S. is so important, and car use is going down, governments could help speed things up. They could make small changes that make cars less needed all over the world. This could even lead to people talking on the bus and making new friends! So, yeah, driving less might even help us make friends!

From cleaning up the air to making life better and even making friends, there are tons of good reasons to drive less.

Lately, it feels like folks are ditching their cars more and more. Instead, they're carpooling like crazy or finding all sorts of new ways to get around town. Elisabeth Rosenthal even points to Vauban, Germany, where cars are basically banned, showing how car use is really dropping. Then there's Robert Duffer, who writes all about Paris's pollution headaches and the creative ways they're trying to fix them. And Andrew Selsky? He covered this cool project in Colombia where people are experimenting with travel methods that don't involve driving. The best part is, less car use leads to awesome stuff: cleaner air, fewer traffic jams, and streets that feel way safer.

Cars are serious pollution machines. Robert Duffer drops this bomb: "Diesels are 67 percent of cars in France..." (Duffer 16). The main culprit? The government taxes diesel fuel less. Problem is, diesel's way nastier for the environment than gasoline, so all that extra use just makes pollution skyrocket. Cutting back on these fuels could make a huge difference in cleaning up the air. Also, cars are responsible for about 12 percent of greenhouse gases in Europe. However, in parts of the U.S., it jumps to a crazy 50 percent (Rosenthal 5). That shows just how much cars are adding to pollution in the United States. Luckily, switching to things like trains or carpooling can drastically lower this number.

More cars equal more traffic, plain and simple. Rosenthal even mentions, "New York's bikesharing and rising bridge tolls show these new goals.(Rosenthal 37)" The never-ending stream of personal cars, for work and everyday stuff, means toll bridges are raking in the cash. Carpooling is a good way to make us use toll bridges less. Sharing rides with coworkers can really ease traffic jams.

Plus, public transport, like the BART in San Francisco, has seriously cut down on the number of drivers. Sure, public transport might take a bit longer, but it's a far more efficient way to get around. (Rosenthal 40) This lowers the number of cars on the road. Fewer cars make it easier for everyone to travel.

Safety's a huge reason to ditch the car, too. In Colombia, they have this car-free day every year where everyone walks or bikes. (Selsky 20) That means fewer cars and way less danger of car accidents. If we explore other ways to travel, there will be fewer accidents. While many people need a car, safety matters a lot. Also, new tech helps people drive less. Telecommunication lets people talk to friends and family remotely. (Rosenthal 35) So, people don't need to travel as much. Texting and social media help people stay in touch, meaning less trouble.

Bottom line: cars bring a whole mess of problems like accidents, brutal traffic jams, and nasty pollution. If we ease up on car use, we can tackle these issues head-on and build a safer, healthier place for everyone.

The Electoral College involves electors casting votes for the President and Vice President, followed by Congress counting these votes. A significant debate exists on whether to maintain the Electoral College or transition to a popular vote system. Despite its long history, the Electoral College can lead to problems. Eliminating it in favor of a popular vote would increase fairness, lessen the chances of ties, and simply be more logical.

The Electoral College system? Not exactly fair, or logical! It doesn't allow voters to directly choose their preferred president. According to Source 1, in a piece titled "What is the Electoral College?", voters are actually selecting electors, who promise to vote for a particular candidate. And here's the kicker: most states use a "winner-take-all" system. This is where all the state's electors go to the candidate who wins the most votes in that state. So, voters aren't really choosing the president directly. They're picking electors who ultimately make the decision. To make it worse, voters often don't even know for sure how their elector will vote, leading to confusion and unexpected outcomes. A popular vote would be fairer, reducing confusion and the risk of "wrong" elector picks. Some argue parties carefully vet electors, but the confusion and vote misallocation persist.

The Electoral College? It could even end in a tie! Source 2, an article called "The Indefensible Electoral College," notes that sometimes states have sent conflicting slates of electors to Congress, leading to downright chaos. Ditching the Electoral College would lower the odds of these tie situations. The "winner-take-all" approach has another consequence: candidates often ignore states they believe they can't win anyway. Source 2 points out that in past elections, seventeen states received little to no attention, and some voters never even saw a single campaign ad!

Look, a popular vote just makes more sense. Sure, there might be downsides to getting rid of the Electoral College, but the advantages are greater. The election process would be simpler and fairer. Some might argue we need the Electoral College to amplify the voices of smaller states. However, a popular vote would give every single voter more power, no matter where they live. A more direct impact! The Electoral College waters that down. Remember, electors could even ignore the popular vote and pick someone they personally prefer. That's a big slap in the face to the voters. So really, getting rid of the Electoral College is a far better solution. Electors are picked according to rules at the state level, which brings in other problems.

Getting rid of the Electoral College? The right thing to do. Fair, logical, and beneficial. Sticking with the Electoral College just does not improve presidential elections. Fairness must be the key in voting. Voters should be able to trust that they are directly choosing their preferred candidate, without any confusion or risking accidentally supporting someone else. Voters shouldn't have to worry that electors might ignore the popular vote and instead vote according to their personal preferences, basically undermining the whole point of voting. Leaving the choice to electors has too many risks, like a single elector, trusted by a party, could change those votes! A direct popular vote would simplify the whole process, giving voters the confidence that their vote is being respected, and it would make sure that presidential elections are fair for everyone.

Giving up your vehicle could appear to be difficult, loaded with complications. However, a better view reveals many advantages. You improve the atmosphere's condition, aiding all people. Besides, alternative transportation approaches could result in financial savings.

Decreasing vehicle usage significantly reduces heat-trapping gases. Elisabeth Rosenthal observes, in component five, that automobiles are responsible for approximately half of these gases within the U.S. This may appear sizeable, but particular towns have effectively resolved pollution. Paris faced intense pollution because of vehicle emissions. They disallowed sure motors on precise days. Abruptly, smog vanished, and car site visitors dropped by 60 percent! If a small modification helped Paris, different towns should without problems lessen pollution.

Saving coins is another benefit of decreased vehicle usage. Autos are costly to buy. Also, as Rosenthal mentions in paragraph twenty-nine, bridges and tunnels incur additional expenses. Conversely, bikes are inexpensive and can be ridden free of charge on paths. Some may consider that switching from cars to bikes is impractical, however, it's going on in Bogota, Colombia. For one day each year, most automobiles are prohibited. Selsky mentions, in paragraph twenty, that tens of millions take part, even if it rains. This proves that alternative transportation isn't restricted to a select few; it holds extensive significance.

To summarize, reduced vehicle usage offers a number of blessings. You purify the air and perhaps store some cash. Everyday changes can drastically advantage your society.

Every day, we drive cars, but do we think about the problems they cause, like stress and pollution? Imagine if we used cars less. We'd be less stressed, roads would be safer, and the air would be cleaner. This helps the planet and keeps our families safe from car dangers.

We're always stressed, often from traffic jams when we're in a hurry. If we cut back on cars, there would be less traffic. Andrew Selsky said it's "a golden chance to ditch the stress." Nobody likes traffic; it's annoying and makes us mad. Stress is bad for our health. Driving stress can hurt us a lot.

Cars are useful, but they can be dangerous. People make cars, and people make mistakes. Some mistakes are small, but others are deadly. Many people have died in car accidents, and many more have lost loved ones. Less driving means fewer accidents, making the world safer for our families.

Elisabeth Rosenthal said that transportation is the second-biggest source of pollution in the US, after power plants. We often ignore pollution, but it's a big deal. It's taking away our clean air. Pollution from power plants and cars is making our air dirty fast. If we drive less, we can help clean up the air. This is good for the environment and gives future generations healthier air.

Because there are so many good things about using cars less, maybe we should do what Europe and Colombia do. Selsky mentioned Colombia's "Day Without Cars," which they've done for three years. Germany has a suburb with no cars, showing us a different way to live. Paris has also had car-free days, which made the air cleaner and people happier. Cars are easy to use, but is that worth all the problems they cause?

You see vehicles everywhere these days. They're seriously inescapable. If you need to travel somewhere, you're likely using a car. But here's a surprise: public transit is gaining popularity again! And guess what? It's actually beneficial because of lower pollution rates, fewer cars obstructing streets, and it makes the cities nicer in general. Back when the concept of cleaner cities seemed unrealistic, it's now catching on quickly!

Think about cities like Chicago or New York. Remember the polluted skies? Cars are largely at fault! Drive less, and you get cleaner air and happier people! To combat significant pollution, Paris once banned certain cars. Smog was completely uncontrollable! Using fewer cars cuts down on that filth, not only for Paris, but for the rest of the globe.

Traffic is really the worst! Driving less leads to better streets and safer roads. To prove that concept, Paris' car ban? There was a 60% decrease in traffic – yes, 60%! – despite lingering smog. Fewer cars also reduce accidents! Chicago's highways become manageable. Less traffic improves road upkeep and road longevity. Car-Free Day demonstrates how this concept works! And it would be greater in bigger countries, like the US! Driving less does tons of good for everyone!

Public transport appeared unrealistic before. Today? Businesses and cities drastically reduce car counts. Less car use in busy areas? Great! New sidewalks, great restaurants, and stores show up everywhere - major wins! Instead of fixing damaged roads, invest in public spaces like parks and active sport centers! Consider new restaurants and stores! Cities benefit from public transport instead of cars!

Cars have always been the go-to choice. Shockingly, reduced pollution, fewer cars, and improved cities make fewer cars attainable. Giving up cars appears hard, but the groundwork already exists! Major cities have public transport that expands to smaller towns. Reduced pollution outweighs minor troubles. Smart planning can separate the need for cars when living outside the city and become a push for smart growth and improvement. Smart planning is the best approach to limiting cars and creating safer, cleaner futures!

Okay, so driving? A lot of people just aren't into it. A growing number of countries are either limiting cars or banning them completely. And guess what? Maybe this is actually a good idea.

Cars, right? They put out some really nasty things. So, less driving equals less pollution. That way, we can cool down on wrecking the planet quite so much. Sure, the pollution's here, but we can make a huge difference! And get this: the money we would save – no payments on a car, and less for gas. Spend it on what we want! Seriously, we can find other ways to get around.

Sociologists believe that fewer cars could greatly help the environment and carbon emissions. One said: "Transportation is the biggest source of emissions, apart from power plants, in the US!" Simply put: drive less = healthier planet = happier people.

But get this - the cash! Folks could keep the money they spend on gas, repairs, and new cars, mainly when job security is not guaranteed. This money? People can use it for more important stuff!

Honestly, we don’t always need a car. For example, teens drive to hang out with friends or go to work, but there are other ways! I'm not saying ditch cars completely, but ditch the extra drives. Walk if you can!

Elisabeth Rosenthal, writer of "The End of Car Culture," noted that her children, ages 19 and 21, did not bother getting licenses! They are doing fine. She said, "They work their summer jobs and social life around walking, buses, or sharing rides with friends." Sharing rides is a possibility, and it leads to less traffic, and less spending.

So, yeah, cutting back on cars? It has a ton of benefits. And bet there's even MORE good stuff to come from it!

Why not just drive less? It's great for you and the planet.

Think about it – it's a win for you too. Ditching the car is like getting a weight off your shoulders. A German family nailed it: "Having a car was always stressful. Now we're way happier!" And it's not just less stress. You also save money on gas and maybe even get a bit healthier. Instead of driving, walk or bike to the shops! Look at Bogota's "Car-Free Day." Suddenly, parks and sports areas popped up, sidewalks got nicer, traffic flowed better, and new stores opened. One report said it was "a chance to chill and breathe easy." That's just one awesome thing about driving less, and it's only the start.

Think bigger, too. Less driving is a huge help for the planet. Experts agree cars cause pollution; in fact, "Cars cause 12% of Europe's pollution!" So fewer cars mean cleaner air. Paris, which is usually smoggy, tried using different license plates on different days. People complained at first, but it did cut down on gas use. Columbia has a yearly car-free day where only buses and taxis are allowed, "to get people to try other ways to get around and cut down on smog." Everyone can help! Even in the US, fewer people are buying cars, driving as much, or even getting licenses, which shows people are rethinking cars. Some might not like it, but the planet will be healthier. Happier folks and a healthier Earth? Those are just a couple of reasons to drive less.

Every four years, on the Tuesday after November's first Monday, the U.S. holds Election Day. It's not a direct vote for President; it's complicated. Though we vote in a democracy, the Electoral College makes it complex. Electors, representing a candidate, decide who becomes President and Vice President; then Congress counts their votes. Political parties usually pick these electors. But is this the fairest way? I think a change is needed.

We don't vote straight for the President. Our votes go through the Electoral College to electors. Not the citizens, but those electors decide who leads. "What Is the Electoral College?" says when you vote for a candidate, you back their electors. Anyone can be an elector, as long as they don't hold public office, and they have the final say. This isn't a direct election. "Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong" notes over 60% want direct elections. That shows many voters want simpler voting.

Electoral votes aren't split fairly. Big states, like California, get more electoral votes than Vermont. While the House ensures fair representation, the Electoral College doesn't. It's uneven. This unfairness clashes with equality, which this country is founded on.

People find the Electoral College confusing. Voters might not know who they're voting for and can't control electors' votes. It's disappointing when electors go against the popular vote. "Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong" points out that voters pick electors who pick the President, so people don't directly choose.

Some say getting rid of the Electoral College could be bad or undemocratic. "In defense of the Electoral College..." says parties pick electors who should back their nominee, but the popular vote winner might not win. So, your vote might not lead to your choice winning. It can stop runoffs and help when no one has a majority, but it has big problems.

Overall, the Electoral College doesn't help our country. It's confusing and can mislead. It might have some good sides, but the bad ones are worse. It's unfair and could hurt the country, so we should drop it for a fairer America.

Alright, dialing back car use can really make things better, right? A mom, Heidrun Walter, mentioned she felt much calmer when she didn't have to drive all the time. And wow, parking can be super pricey – imagine paying $40,000 just to park at your own place! Plus, cars release a lot of pollution, about 12% of greenhouse gases in Europe, and even more in the US (up to half!). If we drive less, we could enjoy neighborhoods where everything's within walking distance. Also, fewer cars would mean cleaner air, which is great for our planet. Hybrid cars are becoming more popular since they're cleaner than older models. But keep in mind that weather can worsen pollution; cold nights and hot days trap all the bad stuff, making the air even dirtier.

Paris had a big problem with smog from cars, so they put in place some rules to combat this pollution. If you broke the rules, you'd be fined 31 bucks, and they might even take away your car if you kept at it! This had a real impact on traffic, reducing it by around 60%! The air improved after the rules were implemented, so Paris allowed some cars back, like those with odd license plate numbers on Tuesdays. Carlos Arturo shared that biking with his wife is way less stressful, and the air is cleaner, according to Andrew Selsky. More and more cities are becoming interested in this idea. For example, in Bogota, Colombia, they built over 118 miles of bike paths.

The mayor of Asuncion, Paraguay, Enrique Rivera, noticed that other city leaders were visiting Bogota because they really liked the changes and thought they could apply them in their own cities too. By 2013, Americans were driving about 9% less, similar to levels in 1995. Part of this is because of the economy, with people having less money for new cars, and unemployed people not needing to drive as much. If a person couldn't afford a car, a bike may have seemed to be a better option to get to work or look for a job. However, some folks just might not like to bike or walk. Plus, having a car can be costly, what with gas and insurance.

Well, the good news is that Americans may be less dependent on cars these days. As families grew, people drove less in 2005. Also, cars make roads less safe. There are fewer young drivers (16-39 years old) now, and older folks are keeping their licenses longer. Studies show that young people drove 23% less between 2001 and 2009. Bill Ford wants to partner with phone companies to create smart cities where walking, biking, cars, trucks, and buses all work together to be more efficient, save resources, reduce pollution, and be safer. In Germany, some areas banned cars, and now 70% of families don't own cars, with 57% selling their cars before moving there. Making such changes can help clean up our air and protect our planet.

Automobiles are ubiquitous nowadays, with numerous individuals worldwide utilizing them for conveyance. They simplify existence, facilitating tasks and journeys. Notwithstanding their remarkable convenience, we occasionally neglect the drawbacks. While vehicles prove beneficial, over-reliance on them might generate further issues. Extensive automobile utilization can severely degrade the environment and induce stress, even subconsciously. Consequently, we ought to reflect profoundly on our vehicular usage.

Our environment provides sustenance, like nourishment and air, but automobiles present a substantial predicament. They emit carbon dioxide, contaminating the atmosphere. In Robert Duff's piece, "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," Paris encountered such extreme air pollution that driving was prohibited. This stemmed from widespread diesel car usage spurred by tax incentives. Duff notes that "diesels constitute 67 percent of vehicles in France, contrasted with a.3 percent average of diesel engines in the remainder of Western Europe, according to Reuters." Moreover, Duff illustrates that densely populated urban centers typically exhibit poorer air quality. As automobiles harm the environment, efforts to reduce their prevalence are underway. Elisabeth Rosenthal's "In German Suburb, Life Goes on Without Cars" chronicles Vauban, Germany, where residents abstain from car ownership. In Vauban, 70 percent of families lack cars, with 57 percent divesting their vehicles upon relocation. Car ownership remains permissible, but parking fees are exorbitant – approximately 40,000. Residents opt for walking or cycling. Vauban exemplifies the feasibility of mitigating automobile pollution.

Aside from ecological harm, automobiles can insidiously induce stress. Heidrun Walter, in "In German Suburb, Life Goes on Without Cars," professes heightened contentment sans car: "When I possessed a car I was perpetually tense. I'm much happier this way." Furthermore, businessman Carlos Arturo asserted that a car-free day in Bogota provided "a good opportunity to alleviate stress and diminish air pollution." Automobiles can dominate lives, with individuals potentially overlooking the repercussions until reduced usage occurs.

People just don't care about cars like they used to. Long ago, a car was just a way to get from one place to another. However, because of all the new cool technology, fewer people are driving cars now, especially in some cities. You might think that less driving is a bad thing to think about, but wait – it's actually a good thing in many different ways!

America has always really loved cars. We made the Model T, and who can forget "Mustang Sally?" Even President Obama wanted us to quit driving so much to help make the air cleaner. In "The End of Car Culture," he said that things were changing a lot. Studies show that people are buying fewer cars, driving less, and not even getting their driver's licenses as much. By 2013, driving had gone down almost 9% compared to before. What's more, fewer young adults are even getting licenses. This is all because of the internet, which lets us work from home, plus cities are getting better, and we have easy phone apps for carpooling. It's not that cars are not important, but life is just easier now than it was in the sixties because of tech. However, in the U.S., driving is kind of a choice, but places such as Paris don't let you drive without any rules.

Paris, France, is a really beautiful city. But it has a big smog problem. The news said, "Paris bans driving due to smog." They stopped some people from driving to help make the air cleaner. If you drive when you're not supposed to, you have to pay 22 euros. They only let cars with certain license plates drive on certain days. The article said that after they did this, traffic went down 60%! Some cars could still drive, like hybrids and those with three or more people inside. Diesel cars, which are very popular in France, were the cause of most of the pollution. Regular gas cars weren't quite as bad. Just like Paris, Bogota, Colombia is trying to do better.

Colombia has a car-free day – nobody can drive at all! It's a 24-hour ban, which is even stricter than Paris. If you drive, you'll pay 25. The article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" said that "millions of Colombians hike, bike, skate, or take buses to work during the carfree day." Like Paris, Bogota wants people to find other ways to get around and stop the smog. A businessman, Carlos Arturo Plaza, said it's "a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution." Driving can be stressful, especially with road rage, which can ruin your day. Driving is okay sometimes, but there are other options. Colombia wants to "promote alternative transportation and reduce smog." Cars are getting nicer, but people aren't as crazy about them anymore, even though they used to be great. Now, they're mostly for getting around when you don't have other choices.

A big plus of using cars less? Cleaner air, no doubt! Cities struggle with nasty pollution from cars, especially where traffic is thickest. Like Paris—they even banned driving sometimes because the air was so bad, news said. That pollution messes with the climate and hurts our lungs. After a bout of crazy smog, Paris stopped cars and traffic got way better—like 60 percent less! Temperature changes also make pollution worse; cold nights, warm days, and bam!

Another good thing about ditching cars? You move more! You end up walking or biking more often, which is great exercise. Plus, you meet people and chat outside. As more people walked, gyms and parks got busier, and new shops and food places popped up, making more money.

More and more people are choosing other options, like buses, bikes, or just walking. Studies show less young people are driving cars—a big 23 percent drop from 2001 to 2009. If more people shared rides and used other ways to get around, pollution would definitely drop everywhere. Lower pollution from vehicles, would certainly improve air quality and reduce breathing problems.

The money we spend on roads and traffic could go to better things if we drove less. Even though everyone uses cars a lot, there are always other ways. Less car use also saves money, especially since gas prices jump around so much.

Basically, there are tons of great reasons to cut back on cars or stop using them completely. Besides helping the planet with cleaner air, it's good for your body too. Walk, bike, or hop on a bus for cheap. Plus, you save cash on gas, repairs, and car payments.

Folks in the U.S. spend tons of time driving, but they often forget there are other ways to get around. So, what's so great about finding new ways to travel? Well, ditching the car habit has some major perks.

Think about it: if we didn't rely so much on cars, shops could be closer to where we live, see? Instead of huge malls miles away, stores could be a short walk down the block. This would lead to new buildings, shops, and homes all within easy walking distance.

Walking to stores? That's pretty cool! It definitely cuts down on the need for a car. And if we all drove less, we'd probably walk and bike more, which would be a huge boost to our health. Research even shows a daily walk is great for your brain.

Plus, less driving means cleaner air, no question. Cars pump out pollution that turns into smog, which is bad for everyone's health. So, by driving less, we lower stress and clean up the air we breathe.

Cars are big sources of greenhouse gases. In Europe, they make up about 12% of those gases. But in some car-dependent parts of the U.S., that number can jump to 50%! Driving less helps us and the planet.

We don't have to drive everywhere, that's for sure! We can try other ways of getting around that are good for us and the Earth. With so many benefits from less driving, why not give it a try and improve our lives and the world?

Can you picture a world without cars? It might sound crazy. Yet, these ideas are becoming real! For example, some new towns are popping up, some cities are restricting where you can drive, and certain countries are even having car-free days. People often forget that cars make a lot of pollution. We should really notice the harm and do something!

Take Vauban, Germany, as a neat example. Here's the deal: live there, and you must get rid of your car or cough up a crazy $40,000 to park! As a result, check this out: a report shows "70% of families in Vauban don't own cars, and 57% actually sold them to move there." One mom, Heidrun Walter, commented, "I was always stressed with a car. I'm way happier now!" This is "Smart Planning" – building areas so folks don't need cars all the time. Cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases and a huge 50% in the U.S. Smart Planning can cut these gases and clean our air!

Paris, France, shows how awful car pollution gets. One source noted, "After days of major pollution, Paris stopped some cars to clean the air." This move slashed traffic by a whopping 60% in just five days. Diesel fuel, which powers 67% of French vehicles, was the main smog culprit. The weather pitched in; as the report pointed out, "cold nights and warm days trapped car emissions."

But it's not just Europe. Bogota, Colombia, had its third "Day Without Cars," only allowing buses and taxis. Carlos Arturo Plaza stated, "It's a good chance to cut stress and pollution." Also, two other Colombian cities, Cali and Valledupar, had their first Day Without Cars this year!

Even in the U.S., car use is shifting. Believe it or not, the miles driven peaked in 2005 and have been dropping since. One source says, "In April 2013, miles driven per person were almost 9% lower than the peak, like going back to January 1995." Less car use is great for the environment, but tough for car companies. Now, Ford and Mercedes are becoming "mobility" companies. New York City even has bike-sharing, plus new bike paths. Tunnel tolls also show this change.

Cars have hurt our planet badly, and we must fix it. With these cool, eco-friendly ideas, we're moving towards a future where cars don't rule us.

Deciphering the Electoral College? It's quite a conundrum. While it boasts a long history, nearly from the country's inception, the original reasons for its design now appear outdated. Initially, limited education among citizens made informed voting difficult. Now, knowledge is widespread, due to news and easily accessible information on candidates. Direct popular vote for president would be more logical, right? The president should be accountable to the populace, not just insiders.

The Electoral College has its issues, even if you are a supporter. Presidential votes are cast for electors, selected uniquely by each state. These electors commit to vote for a specific candidate, then actually cast their presidential vote. Yet, as Bradford Plumer observes, electors can't be controlled! Plumer also highlights voter confusion surrounding electors, leading to incorrect voting. Sometimes, these "faithless electors" even break their pledge.

"Swing states" get all the candidate attention. These are states where either party could win, making them decisive. Given that the Electoral College assigns all state votes to the winner, a Democrat in a strongly Republican state might not vote, as their vote is unlikely to make a difference. Consequently, campaigns focus solely on swing states. A national popular vote would require campaigns to engage with everyone.

A popular vote for president promotes fairness. Even smaller groups in reliably one-sided states gain influence. A poll once revealed most voters prefer direct presidential selection. Even a Electoral College proponent admits that it could lead to a tie. Furthermore, they argue that one vote is meaningless, which is ridiculous! Such thinking would discourage voting and undermine elections. Every vote counts, and every group merits representation. A popular vote stands as the ideal way to elect a president who serves all Americans. Everyone should participate. It should apply to everyone, so let's get out and vote!

Post-WWII, cars dominated daily routines. Sources 1 & 4 show some folks driving less or not at all. But, sources 2 & 3 explore cities with car bans on certain days. Less driving means less pollution and easier city travel.

Air pollution is a big worry. Rosenthal points out in source 1, "cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases... even up to 50% in some car-heavy U.S. spots P5." Smog often hits cities because of lots of cars. During the 2009 Beijing Olympics, less car use made the air better. This protected athletes and visitors from lung issues, since Beijing folks already wore masks. Though tough, limiting cars avoids bad stuff. Duffer says in source 2 that Paris, a big city, now has way less pollution P19. They even banned cars based on license plates. Less car pollution means cleaner air and nicer views.

Even though they grew up with cars, many young adults now wait or skip getting licenses. Selsky notes fewer cars mean more use of parks and sports fields, plus popular shops and walking areas P28. Less driving helps new businesses and cool restaurants pop up. Instead of malls by highways, shopping areas are now near transit or within walking distance. Rosenthal mentions his family using transit, carpools, and walking instead of driving P40. Public transit is now a smart way to travel in the city. Carpooling is now a valuable resource for everyone involved. With carpools, walking, and transit, it's now easier to get around and see the city.

Cities are changing to favor those without cars. Car restrictions have lots of perks. Cleaner air and easier ways to get around are making cars less needed. Less car use will probably bring even more good things later.

Okay, so the deal with the electoral college? Individuals are picked to be electors, and then they cast votes for who will be president, and those votes get tallied, correct? Is this truly a fantastic system, or is it simply a disaster in the making? It really should just be eliminated, given that it's not a direct vote, and things might rapidly take a turn for the worse.

Some believe the electoral college stops those complicated runoff elections, claiming it could, potentially, make ruling easier. Richard A. Posner emphasized how, in a really tight race, people actually desire runoffs. Keeping the electoral college, they insist, results in fewer runoffs happening. However, is it actually a dependable fix, and what issues could it present? Given electors vote for the president, can we even trust them to stop runoffs from occurring? Posner argues it does take away any pressure to mess things up further. But still, this one perk doesn't outweigh all the glaring issues.

What occurs if everything goes totally haywire? We've got a huge issue! The writer shares that we were fortunate the 2000 election was the absolute worst in a hundred years; things could absolutely get much worse. It's occurred in the past. Plumer believes the electoral college has deeply wounded the U.S., and deciding to keep it might cause even more chaos. The writer reminds that electors have voted against their parties, according to Plumer. Therefore, it's not just useless, but may also be easily tampered with. It should be tossed aside given it could completely devastate our government.

On top of all of this, it is not a direct election. The Office of the Federal Register notes that whenever you are voting for a candidate, what you're really doing is casting a vote for *their* electors. Therefore, you aren't actually selecting who runs the whole country. It simply feels wrong for leaders to be making massive calls that touch people's lives, even when they did not vote for them. Plumer calls it unfair, archaic, and honestly just dumb. These concerns highlight how the electoral college damages trust in government. Plumer also adds that candidates simply ignore those states they believe they can't win, choosing to focus only on "swing" states. Therefore, winning has become far more important than acting in the best interest of the country. Are we really going to allow this thing to just destroy us?

In short, the electoral college? Get rid of it.

Respected Florida Senator, the Constitution’s framers set up electors to reflect the public's presidential choice. However, this system can lead to a popular vote winner losing the election. It is outdated and prone to creating a tie. Quite a few citizens in the U.S. would prefer to follow democratic principles, which this system might undermine.

The popular vote reflects the nation's true desire. A substantial majority – "over 60 percent" – favors direct presidential voting, indicating a wish for more impactful votes. When the President sends troops to Afghanistan, Iraq, or Iran, voters want their presidential choice to matter, and their voice to count, instead of being overshadowed by the electoral college. The 538 electors, few in number, wield more influence than millions of citizens' votes. And we can't forget, the Declaration of Independence promises "unalienable rights, that among these are: life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness," with liberty being tied to voting.

The electoral college has been around a while. But that's the problem. To keep the U.S. competitive, we must update constitutional rules. As society progresses, our systems must adapt in order to improve, evolve, and not cause issues. For some evidence, "The American people should consider themselves lucky that the 2000 fiasco was the biggest election crisis in a century," highlighting a flaw that could repeat itself.

Making things even worse, the risk of a tie amplifies the issues. As it has been said, "perhaps most worrying is the prospect of a tie in the electoral vote." Such a tie clashes with democratic ideas. A democracy should focus on the public vote, not a legislative stalemate. According to Source 2, "the election is only a few swing voters away from catastrophe." In a tie, the popular vote gets ignored, which would be risky.

In conclusion, the electoral college ought to be removed because it harms U.S. people. It diminishes the popular vote's impact, is outdated, and risks a tie. In the future, I hope the president will be chosen by the people's will, instead of the will of those in the capital.

Okay, so picture cars, right? They whisk you here and there, for errands or picking up pals. But what if they vanished, poof? Weird, huh? But maybe, just maybe, some good stuff could actually come from it.

First off, Earth gets a major win! Cars are such awful polluters, aren't they? Get this: "Passenger cars? 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases are because of them!" And get this! "Up to 50% in super car-obsessed spots in the US!" (That's from "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph 5). So, bye-bye cars, hello cleaner air, bye-bye yucky smog. Air's way better, period!

Another point: we'd be healthier too. Cleaner air, sure! But also, no cars mean you gotta hoof it or bike around. "Vauban," says "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph 6, "5,500 people in a tight spot, are going car-free." Shops are nearby, a "walkable close," not "miles away." So, folks walk, bike, bus it. More moving, less couch-potatoing. Sweet!

Plus, cars might be getting less cool. "Obama's goals to cut US emissions," says "The End of Car Culture," paragraph 29, "are helped by people buying fewer cars, driving less, getting fewer licenses." Gas is pricey, texting and driving is a danger, new drivers have no idea what's going on. Seems people aren't as car-crazy. Great for the planet, right? Maybe driving just won't even matter down the line.

So, fewer cars? That is a total score! Earth can breathe, and folks are already ditching their rides. Why burn gas when you can walk, bike, and get in shape? Less driving? Awesome sauce!

The Electoral College, a long-debated subject, sparks mixed reactions. Should we abolish it, or keep things as they are? Some say it's unfair, harming voters and campaigns alike. However, others consider it the superior method for choosing a president. Both viewpoints have valid points, so what modifications, if any, should be implemented?

The Electoral College, instead of merely tallying individual votes, serves as a sort of presidential selection compromise. Political parties in each state nominate a slate of electors. Consequently, your vote for a candidate essentially supports their chosen electors.

Why the push to eliminate the Electoral College? It presents considerable challenges. A primary concern lies in the potential for an electoral deadlock. How so? Electors could deviate and vote according to their own preferences. Though you assume your vote selects electors, their actions might not always align with expectations. This situation poses a problem if electors forsake their party's candidate. A desire for direct elections surged among many voters following Al Gore's popular vote victory in 2000, despite losing the election. A lot of voters show disdain for the Electoral College.

However, some individuals advocate for its preservation. Each party chooses electors likely to vote for their candidate, and generally, they do. It is likely that the party will choose people who will support their candidate. It also highlights the importance of appealing to diverse regions. A president who wins only in a limited region is likely unfit to lead. They won't meet the needs of everyone if they just focus on one region. Therefore, they likely won't win the election. Furthermore, the Electoral College can resolve situations when no candidate secures a clear majority.

When considering the future of the Electoral College, whether to retain it or discard it, both arguments hold weight. Ultimately, the decision rests with the public.

Cars are important for many, helping with things like taking kids to school. Still, more people think using cars less could help fix some problems.

Everyone knows cars hurt the planet. Dirty air and gases that warm the Earth are big worries. In Europe, cars cause about 12% of these gases. In the US, where people use cars a lot, it can be as high as 50%! Also, too much traffic makes things worse. Like in France, cars that use diesel fuel made the smog in cities worse, mostly when it got warmer. Using cars less means cleaner air for everyone.

Besides helping the Earth, using cars less can also help people. All over the world, some folks have tried living without cars, using other ways to get around, and they usually like it. Heidrun Walkter, a rich mom in Germany, gave up her car and now loves walking and hearing bikes and kids. She said, "I was always stressed with a car. Now, I'm much happier." Also, driving less makes people move more. In Colombia, millions walked, biked, or used buses on a day without cars to fight smog. It worked well, made people feel closer, and lowered stress. One guy biking with his wife said it helped both the Earth and them.

So, using cars less clearly makes the air better and reduces smog everywhere, while there are more kids and bikes. From Germany to France, Colombia, and the US, countries are trying to find ways to make driving less bad, hoping to always do better.

Esteemed state senator, I implore you to back the idea of choosing U.S. Presidents through a nationwide popular vote. This reform is absolutely essential, and allow me to clarify why. To begin, electors might just disregard the public's choices. Furthermore, we could face a deadlock, pushing the election decision to the House. Lastly, the winner-take-all method is simply unjust.

Electors haven't always followed the script, and frankly, it could easily recur. As Bradford Plumer mentioned in "The Indefensible Electoral College," individuals nearly upended the 1960 election by attempting to swap out electors. Occasionally, electors simply cast ballots as they see fit, even against their party's direction!

The mere idea of an Electoral College tie is genuinely alarming. Should it occur, the House of Representatives steps in, yet each state only gets a single vote. Think about it: Wyoming, with a mere 500,000 residents, wields equal power to California, boasting roughly 35 million! Back in 1976, a minor swing in votes in Ohio and Hawaii could have resulted in a tie. We've skirted too close for comfort!

To conclude, the Electoral College's winner-take-all structure treats voters in an inequitable manner. Candidates only zero in on swing states, neglecting the rest. In 2000, a staggering seventeen states received zero attention from the candidates. Numerous voters in major media hubs didn't even see a single campaign advertisement. How can we validate allowing a tiny cluster of voters in locales such as Ohio to determine who occupies the highest office? Defending it is a tall order.

Therefore, state senator, let me reiterate: please advocate for a popular vote system in presidential elections. Never forget that electors could defy voters, a tie would punt the decision to the House, and the winner-take-all structure is undeniably unfair and unsuitable for our nation.
